Ellen Spitz

Ellen Spitz

Ellen Handler Spitz
Center for Advanced Study in
the Behavioral Sciences
Stanford, California 94305

Ellen Spitz  

Several months ago, over coffee in a Cambridge café, a colleague and I were debating the ethical implications of Father Rabbit’s ‘accident’ in Mr McGregor’s garden. (As you may remember, he was put in a pie by Mrs McGregor.) A palpable silence revealed that our conversation was being monitored by the couple seated nearby. At length, leaning towards us, one of them broke in.

Breakdown and Breakthrough.

By Nathan Field. London: Routledge. 1996. Pp. 157.

W. Layland

W. Layland

W. Ralph Layland
13 Jeffrey
’
s Place, Jeffrey
’
s Street
Camden Town, London NW1 9PP

W. Layland  

As I was reading this book for the first time, in order to write a review, I realised that my approach to it was very similar to the one I use when conducting a consultation. As the story unfolded, I was trying to fit it into a known diagnostic category. For the first four chapters I thought that I was doing fine. What the author was saying seemed to be familiar ground to me. Basically, his message was that there had to be breakdown in order to achieve breakthrough. Put another way, disintegration precedes integration, and for there to be further integration there has to be a period of disintegration. I liked my patient/author, because I was able to start to formulate a diagnosis. Then, at the end of Chapter Four, he introduced some new symptoms/ideas, and started to make me consider a differential diagnosis, to make me think, and to make me feel out of my depth. I was asked to consider quantum theory, holography, the work of Bohm, the neurosciences and pure consciousness. I was not feeling quite so positive towards him. By the time that I had reached the last chapter, I was still unable to make a diagnosis, when the author came to my help. I quote:

It was my original intention to organise the various themes of this book into a logical picture, one leading naturally to the next. But in practice this has proved more difficult than I anticipated. It was as if the material itself resisted this kind of sequential ordering and each section tended to become an exploration of similar themes from a succession of different perspectives (p. 137).

If I were to find a fault with this book, it is that at the end of it, the reader is left the task of trying to tie all the loose ends together.

It is important before reading this book to know that the author, Nathan Field, belongs to the school of Jungian-trained psychotherapists who have introduced into their theoretical thinking the work of Melanie Klein and her followers and the work of the Group of Independent Psychoanalysts, particularly those, such as Winnicott and Balint, who were interested in early infant development.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Au vif de l’infantile—réflexions sur la situation analytique.

[At the heart of childhood: reflections on the analytic situation]. By Florence Guignard. Lausanne: Delachaux et Niestlé. 1996. Pp. 232.

Juan Manzano

Juan Manzano

Juan Manzano
Service m
é
dico-p
é
dagogique
Case postale 50, 1211 Geneva 8

Juan Manzano  

This book is surely one of the purest expressions of French psychoanalytic thought, whose typical features of constant flux, creativity and the calling into question of established ideas it exhibits. French psychoanalysis, having always been firmly grounded in Freudian metapsychology and the first topography, has been particularly resistant to the structural approach of ego-psychology; by contrast, it has received substantial external contributions that it has reinterpreted and assimilated. The author offers a graphic account of the effects of the encounter between these disparate elements, which she subjects to sensitive and open analysis; in particular, she discusses the elaboration of the ideas of Klein and her school on the early Oedipus complex and narcissistic identifications, as well as Bion's establishment of a psychoanalytic theory of thought processes and his observation of a group mentality at the heart of individual oedipal functioning.

The thread that runs through Guignard's thought is the concept of the ‘infantile’ in the analytic relationship. The ‘infantile’ is an ‘ineffable’ notion, by its very nature hard to define, yet in her view omnipresent implicitly in the works of Freud and of other analytical authors. It is a ‘strange historical-ahistorical conglomerate, the crucible of primal fantasies and sensorimotor experiences that are memorisable in the form of mnemic traces. It may be regarded as the psychic locus of the first unrepresentable emergences of the drives’ (pp. 16-7). In the author's opinion, this part of the psyche is always at work in all subjects, whether children or adults. Guignard makes no attempt to link this concept to the structural theory but places it rather in the context of Freud's first theories of the psychic apparatus. What might be deemed an archaic mode of organisation of psychic life could in our view be likened to the Kleinian analysts’ notion of the ‘infantile part’, a counterpoint to the ‘adult part’. That is generally seen as a split-off part of the ego operating synchronously with its adult counterpart, each of these two parts having its own representation (the infantile self and the adult self). The originality of the author's conception of the infantile lies in its ‘exciting’ and seductive aspect, involving a kind of crude ‘protosymbolic’ energy that stimulates the entire psychic apparatus, in search as it is of representations and sublimations, and at the same time triggering automatic defences, in particular repression. The most interesting aspect of this idea is the way the author sees it working in the analytic relationship, as outlined below.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Processes of repetition and offerings of the ego

By Anna Potamianou. Paris: Delachaux et Niestlé, Collection Champs Psychanalytiques. 1995. Pp. 161.

Paul Paul

Paul Paul

Paul Israël
83 Av. d'Italie
75013 Paris

Paul Paul  

Anna Potamianou is a doctor of philosophy and a member of both the Hellenic Psychoanalytical Study Group and the Paris Psychoanalytical Society. The references to Greek mythology that permeate her book demonstrate the author’s deep roots in the culture from which she draws her metaphors. However, when she tells us in her preface that she regards what she has written as the ‘flesh of the psyche’, we know that we are listening to one of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society’s most authoritative champions of the prevalence of the drive in Freud’s oeuvre and hence in the process and practice of psychoanalysis. Arguing by ‘recapitulative cycles of the subjectmatter under consideration’ as she herself puts it, Anna Potamianou is concerned to review and analyse the processes of repetition, as a universal tendency not only of organic life but also, and in particular—as Freud clearly showed —of psychic life and the life of the drives. While she in no way fails to recognise the importance of the repetitive activities that constitute the foundation of psychic life, she is mainly, if not exclusively, concerned here with the repetition compulsion, as a tendency that simultaneously opposes and combines with the efforts at transformation in the treatment.

Hence the author’s discussion in the second part of the book of patients whose behaviour ‘respects neither the possibilities of the soma nor the limits of mental life’ and who thus compulsively seek out martyr or victim situations. Sacrificial behaviour of this kind, described as a paradigm of repetitions falling outside the pleasure-unpleasure system, suggests to the author the paradoxical idea of ‘offerings of the ego’ [the offrandes du moi of the title], which she uses in order to pursue on a deeper level the semantic and mythological discussion about the concept of sacrifice commenced at the beginning of the book. The paradox arises out of the inconsistency between the idea of offerings of the ego and the involuntary and in particular unconscious nature of the relinquishments. Anna Potamianou resolves it by contending that ‘the unconsciousness of the ego does not mean that its role in the process is innocent’. She is clearly at one with those contemporary authors who, taking Freud’s discovery as their starting point, are developing theoretical approaches to tackling the technical difficulties presented by borderline states.

responseSet.title: Interaction And Transference
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Interaction And Transference

Joseph Schacter

Joseph Schacter

Joseph Schacter
401 Shady Ave.
Pittsburgh, PA 15206

Joseph Schacter  

Dear Sir

Ponsi (IJPA, 1997, 78: 243-263) presents a clinical vignette in which she characterizes her first interpretation as a here and now transference interpretation that focuses on interaction, whereas the second interpretation has more to do with transference than interaction. Gill, she states, fails to recognize such a difference, and reduces all transference to interaction. This, she believes, ‘impoverishes rather than enriches the capacity for psychoanalytic investigation’ (p. 259). I disagree, and will try to demonstrate from her vignette that she does not understand Gill’s concept of interaction. I write from the vantage point of having had the privilege of being supervised by Gill during the last years of his life. Despite the lucidity of his writing it is difficult for the reader to know what he would do in a particular clinical situation.

Here is a summary of her vignette. The session began with a ten- to fifteen-minute silence and then the patient talked about roaming around with her thoughts, relaxing. She mentioned she had forgotten to bring the analyst’s ‘shekels’, and then talked in a bored tone about a dinner party to which she had to go. She repeated that she didn’t understand how she forgot to bring the cash. The analyst interpreted: ‘You forgot it “again” and you are worried and bothered by this repetition: you are afraid there is something serious to be confronted, something that may put the relationship with me severely to the test. Perhaps you felt at some point that whenever you forgot like this, it might also be a sign of something good: relaxation, letting yourself go in fantasies (as you did at the beginning of the session), not having to keep things under control, trusting that you will be accepted—at least here with me—for what you are, without having to worry about behaving properly—as you feel you have to do with the people at your dinner. Having to remember the cash [soldi] —the “shekels” … is like having to give up something good: something pleasant—like your roaming about with your thoughts at the beginning of the session—which you also feel to be positive, because then you are more spontaneous, less formal and more open. So you try to get rid of thoughts about your forgetting: you treat it as something contemptible and spoilt: “shekels” are really something not worth thinking about’ (p. 254).

[[RunningHead]]

Response

Maria Ponsi

Maria Ponsi

Maria Ponsi
Via della Chiesa 77
50125 Florence

Maria Ponsi  

Dear Sir

The sentence of my paper J. Schachter refers to—‘that the reduction of transference to interaction … impoverishes rather than enriches the capacity for psychoanalytic investigation’ (p. 259)—must be completed by the parenthetic clause ‘as might be deduced from some of Gill’s radical arguments’. I mention the sentence in full because in my reading of Gill—whom I very much admire for his lucid, precise and profound investigation into psychoanalytic concepts—I have at times felt puzzled by a usage of the concept of interaction that in my opinion is often absolute and radical; this is particularly evident when Gill brings his theoretical and methodological speculation close to the clinical and technical field. While maintaining that the analytic situation, including interpretations, is an interactive one, and while demonstrating that interaction is intrinsic to the analytic procedure and that the decisive criterion of psychoanalysis is the analysis of transference (i.e., in his words, that the patient’s experience of the interaction is made explicit)

[[RunningHead]]

Response

Drew Westen

Drew Westen

Drew Westen
Dept. of Psychiatry
Cambridge Hospital and Harvard Medical
School
1493 Cambridge St.
Cambridge, MA 02139

Drew Westen  

Dear Sir

In reading Dr Spezzano’s letter, and rereading my remarks to which he refers regarding his thoughtful treatise on affect in psychoanalysis, I hope I did not inadvertently leave readers with a largely negative impression of his contributions. As I noted early in the article, Spezzano is one of the theorists who has been working toward a more comprehensive understanding of the role of affect in motivation in psychoanalysis, along with Kernberg, Sandler, and others.

I may be misreading Spezzano’s book, but I did sense in it a conflict familiar in psychoanalytic writing between two goals. One is to develop a sensible theory, in this case a psychoanalytic theory of affect. The other is to ‘extract a theory of affect from the psychoanalytic literature’ (1993, p. xi). As Spezzano writes later in the book, ‘Freud was on the brink of developing the reading of his theory of affects that I am undertaking here, but he did not read it as radically as I want to read it. It seems to me, in other words, as if Freud was on the way to where I want to take him’ (p. 61). This is a conflict with which all of us have had to deal since Freud, which reflects both the necessity for humility when standing on the shoulders of a giant, but also a dynamic in the analytic community that I think has been bad for psychoanalysis, namely a confusion of the question of ‘is it true?’ with the questions of ‘is it analytic?’ or ‘is it what Freud meant?’ I did not mean to single out Spezzano in this respect, although I could see how my comments may have read that way. As Spezzano rightly points out, he himself nicely placed Rapaport’s failed efforts to develop a coherent theory of affect in perspective of his having tethered himself too closely to Freud’s formulations on affect, which were clinically brilliant but theoretically confusing.

If Spezzano and I disagree, I suspect that the major area, and one perhaps worthy of discussion, is the question of just how far we can go with affect theory. For example, Spezzano argues that ‘the natural extension of this reality of how we talk to our patients is the recognition that a theory of affects is the superordinate perspective for which we have been searching, the one that transcends theories of drive, ego, object, self, and interpersonal relations’ (p. 39). That affect theory represents a superordinate theory of this sort is less clear to me. As I have argued elsewhere (‘Case formulation and personality diagnosis: two processes or one?’ In Making Diagnosis Meaningful, ed. James Barron, Washington, DC: American Psychological Association Press), formulating a case dynamically means answering three sets of questions: (1) what does the person wish for, fear, and value, and to what degree are these motives conscious and conflicting? (2) What psychological resources—cognitive, affective, behavioral, and affect-regulatory processes —does the person have at his disposal as he tries to satisfy his desires in his particular environmental context? (3)

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response

Evelyne Schwaber

Evelyne Schwaber

Evelyne Albrecht Schwaber
4 Welland Road
Brookline
MA 02146

Evelyne Schwaber  

Dear Sir

Although, as I have indicated, I have consistently argued that the analyst must employ her perspective to help her locate the patient’s, the clinical and epistemological stance embedded in my position remains different from that of Dr Gabbard. It is one thing to draw upon our vantage point to enable us to observe something we might share with the patient, the psychic meaning of which we may not know and is yet to be discovered, and another to use it to infer the ‘unconscious intent’ of the patient. The former position facilitates our inquiry into a far greater range of yet unexplored data, a matter which I tried to indicate in my response to the clinical example. The questions I raise regarding this vignette do not have to do with a problem of psychoanalytic discourse in which space is a concern, but with what I view as a problem inherent in a clinical stance which leaves aside inquiry into further potential information about the patient’s still unconscious meaning because the analyst believed, based on his own vantage point, that he already knew it—that is, that the feelings he derived from his position as ‘object’ offered him not only an awareness of his difference, but a greater ‘objectivity’ about what was unconscious to the patient. In presuming that because he felt ‘bullied’ and ‘overwhelmed’ (as apparently had other men in her life) it was the patient’s unconscious intent for him to feel this, the analyst blurred the distinction between their different vantage points, a leap of inference foreclosing exploration of communications that may have led to yet uncharted paths, and to other possibilities in the patient’s unconscious meaning.

As may be gleaned from past correspondence, my persistent effort to search for the nuances in the patient’s vantage point, while drawing upon what I know of my own, has nonetheless led to a not infrequent critique that I am suggesting that the analyst can somehow divest herself of her own separate experience. That is surely not the case, but I do propose that the sustained recognition that our vantage point, even our view of ourselves, is simply that—our own view— and does not yet tell us the patient’s, has profound clinical and conceptual implications with which we might yet grapple.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis: the Basic Concepts.

Edited by Burness E. Moore and Bernard D. Fine. New Haven and London: Yale University Press. 1995. Pp. 577.

David Werman

David Werman

David S. Werman
Duke University Medical Center
Durham, North Carolina 27710

David Werman  

Over the past several decades we have seen numerous clinical and theoretical emendations and additions to the psychoanalytical literature. Questions have been raised as to whether psychoanalysis possesses one theory or two—or even more; there are various claims as to what is the superordinate theory of psychoanalysis, and almost every basic concept has been challenged by some and defended by others. These exchanges, however, have generally been piecemeal, and a comprehensive and intensive overview of what may be described as contemporary standard psychoanalytic theory has not beeen available. With the publication of Psychoanalysis: the Basic Concepts that overview has been richly achieved.

There is no work of a scope with which one can properly compare this book. The editors of this work, Burness Moore and the late Bernard Fine, previously published the Glossary

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘The Witch's Kitchen’, Freud, ‘Faust’, and the Transference.

By Sabine Prokhoris. Translated by G. M. Goshgarian. With a Foreword by Monique Schneider. Ithaca and London: Cornell University Press. 1988. Pp. 195.

Helen Robinson

Helen Robinson

Helen Taylor Robinson
4 Whellock Road, London W4 1DZ

Helen Robinson  

Dreams and dreaming are the unsure ground upon which the activity Freud designated as ‘psychoanalysis’ is based. His starting-point for reading and attempting to understand mental activity was dreaming—nocturnal mental functioning as opposed to that of the daytime mind—and his own nocturnal productivity in particular, relayed in fragments in letters to his friend Fliess.

The formal rendering of this unsure ground came in the first psychoanalytic text, the one in which Freud took greatest pride throughout his life, The Interpretation of Dreams (1900, S.E. 4-5). Without dreams and dreaming (and thereafter daytime phantasising and all that followed from that) and Freud's lifelong study of them, informal and fragmentary, prescribed formally in his metapsychological texts or theory of mind, we could say there could not have been ‘psychoanalysis’, something else perhaps.

The meaning of ‘unsure’ in this context is that of unknowing, or undermining knowing, or in contrast or counterpoint to knowing, to address the essential and defining paradox at the heart of Freud's revelations and thinking about dreams and dreaming. It is also to retain the meaning that is usual, that ‘unsure’ means unsteady, unclear, unlit, with no easy path or route anywhere, indeed possibly a path away from clarity and security, a path towards illusion and delusion.

Starting with dreams and dreaming is starting at an intersection or crossroads at which the illumination and vision of the nocturnal states, in the benign sense, prophecies, wishes, desires, intuitions, the poetic and creative imagination sit alongside nightmares, hauntings, obsessions and preoccupations, aberrations, hallucinations, ‘the hearing of voices’, delusional states, madness. We could call this the location at which absense is allied to, and spawned from, sense.

Staying at this intersection is staying with psychoanalysis and the psychoanalytic. This intersection is famous in psychoanalysis because it was almost synonymously with the dreams and dreaming discoveries that Freud started to make from the dreams following his father's death that the discovery grew of the dream of every one of us at another mythic crossroads, the dream or wish to remove the obstacle (father or mother) that stood between us and the beloved and desired ‘other’ of the two parents. This is the dream that has a famous literary formulation in Oedipus Rex by Sophocles, which placed dreams and dreaming alongside literature, the one an illusive, the other a more definable seat of unconscious and conscious in play. Staying at this intersection, that is to say, staying in literal dreams or in the dreamings of the poet and others akin to him, where they may be approached and engaged with, is where the essentially dream-psychoanalytic may be said to occur.

responseSet.title: Freud and Judaism.: Edited by David Meghnagi. London: Karnac Books. 1993. Pp. 174.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud and Judaism.

Edited by David Meghnagi. London: Karnac Books. 1993. Pp. 174.

Bernard Barnett

Bernard Barnett

Bernard Barnett
73 Fortune Green Road, London NW6 1DR

Bernard Barnett  

Freud and Judaism is the result of publishing the proceedings of a seminar held in Milan in May 1989. It is made up of a number of articles by various authors on such issues as Nazism, death, ethics, humour, research, assimilation and anti-Semitism. According to its editor, David Meghnagi, the general aim of the book is to seek ‘realistic and practical answers’ to certain questions about psychoanalysis and its connection with ‘Jewish origins and Jewish destiny’.

This ambitious aim has not, in my view, been achieved and the reasons for this are the familiar ones when a project of this kind is attempted. Firstly, I think that the editor and contributors have not been successful in tackling the difficult problem of transforming the conference proceedings into a coherent and unified whole that would interest and inform a professional and lay readership. Instead the book has turned out to be a hotch-potch of essays of very uneven quality that often seem to me to be only loosely related to either ‘Freud’ or ‘Judaism’.

That many of the contributors are Italians writing in English seems to have led, unfortunately, to some passages being obscure and even nonsensical. What may have been clear and interesting in an oral presentation at a conference has clearly lost its meaning in the production of a written text, especially when the ‘everlasting sentence’ is the preferred style. One of many examples is the following passage:

In this case psychoanalysis and certain psychoanalysts would become the object, rather than the subject, of proselytism—unless the ‘projective assimilation’, instead of taking the more numerous ways, which have in any case a defensive outcome from the inside point of view and an offensive outcome from the point of view of relationships, takes the way of an evolutional outcome, both at an intra-group and an inter-group level (by analogy with what may be deduced from clinical examples at an intra- and inter-individual level) (p. 137).

A further difficulty is a tendency to make exaggerated claims when applying the findings of psychoanalytic research outside the consulting room. For example: ‘Until Freud this type of secret [the secrets that we each keep from ourselves] was unsuspected’ (p. xviii). In response to this comment, I found myself questioning whether these were not the very ‘secrets’ that artists, poets, novelists etc. have always tried to reveal.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Suicidal Adolescent.

Edited by Moses Laufer. Madison, CT: International Universities Press. 1995. Pp. 154.

Moisy Shopper

Moisy Shopper

Moisy Shopper
4524 Forest Park Ave., St. Louis, MO 63108

Moisy Shopper  

Moses Laufer, director of the Brent Adolescent Centre (Centre for Research into Adolescent Breakdown) in London, has gathered papers from staff and former staff to address the serious problem of adolescent suicide. Their walk-in clinic and therapeutic services form the basis of their clinical research. Five of the eleven chapters are the proceedings of a 1993 conference on ‘The Suicidal Adolescent’. Of the thirty-two references, all but the four written by the Laufers are pre-1976. The latest reference is their 1984 book. The references are solely analytic; many are familiar classics and speak to the somewhat isolated and inbred nature of this book. Nevertheless, a professional lifetime of clinical experience and focused thoughtfulness of Centre staff provides us with an in-depth view of the adolescent process gone awry. As the editor of the book is the director and senior analyst at the Centre, it is not surprising to find a rather high degree of uniformity of opinion, viewpoints, and emphasis.

According to the authors, the Centre's first and crucial diagnostic task is to distinguish between the normal temporary developmental stresses occurring in adolescence and those problems that are the harbingers of serious and often far-reaching pathology. Laufer presents eight criteria that he uses to evaluate the adolescent. Almost as an afterthought, he suggests an additional criterion: the analyst's personal reaction to the adolescent. Since adolescents characteristically evoke strong and counterproductive adult reactions, I would have welcomed greater elaboration on this criterion.

M. Eglé Laufer's chapter on ‘Depression and self-hatred’ focuses on two depressed girls’ feelings of worthlessness and self-hatred, as expressed in each girl's wish to hurt her body through a suicide attempt. The reader is left wanting by both the briefness of the vignettes and the sparsity of the discussion.

Myer Wohl's chapter on ‘Depression and guilt’ presents a longer vignette of a depressed, disturbed adolescent. While demonstrating the extent of the adolescent's disturbance, Wohl says little about guilt in the aetiology or persistence of the pathology.

responseSet.title: A Thing Apart: Love and Reality in the Therapeutic Relationship.: By Irving Steingart. Northvale, New Jersey: Jason Aronson. 1995. Pp. 291.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Thing Apart: Love and Reality in the Therapeutic Relationship.

By Irving Steingart. Northvale, New Jersey: Jason Aronson. 1995. Pp. 291.

Eleanor Schuker

Eleanor Schuker

Eleanor Schuker
150 West End Ave., #26A
New York, NY 10023

Eleanor Schuker  

This is an extraordinarily difficult book to read through, but worth the effort. Had I started Irving Steingart's A Thing Apart: Love and Reality in the Therapeutic Relationship by skipping the first one hundred or so pages, I might have obtained more of its sweet rewards earlier. The reader will especially welcome Steingart's ideas about working with patients whose conflicts are revealed in behavioural enactments rather than in analytic verbalisations. Steingart contributes to our conceptualising and handling of enactments, so as to embrace these as a felt part of meaningful experiences in a treatment relationship.

The first chapter of this book is devoted to the place of psychoanalysis among knowledgeseeking pursuits, i.e. whether it is hermeneutic or scientific. Steingart argues for the latter. Unfortunately, his stylistic density makes it hard to follow his complex thinking. In Chapter Two, he compares three types of psychoanalytic practice, a Freudian practice that verbalises interpretively within the analysand's psychic reality, a practice that emphasises the analysand's here-and-now experience, and a practice in which the analyst expresses the countertransference. Again, I felt buffeted in the thick stew of Steingart's Talmudic debates with other writers, so laced with quotations and references that his direction was unclear. A clinical vignette and some brief comments about language encouraged my persistence. When I returned later to these first two ponderous chapters, I could then discern Steingart's purpose in emphasising the realist-correspondence view of psychoanalysis. His argument that the psychic reality of the patient exists independently serves as a groundwork for conceptualising the treatment relationship as one of real and unique love, and for describing how an analyst can utilise countertransference as well as individual work-style to elaborate collaboratively and resolve a patient's transferences.

Steingart finds his own voice and requires less documentation from others to declare his views by Chapter Three. He asserts that the love of the analyst for his patient is real, unique, and extraordinary, comprising a love for the analysand's mind, ‘a loving desire … to know every nook and cranny of the analysand's psychic reality’ (p. 127). This real love for the analysand, i.e. for the analysand's way of using his or her mind to create psychic reality, contains neither therapeutic ambition nor wishes for the analysand to change. Rather, it involves steadfast devotion to the wish to understand. In this special way, each analysand is loved ‘because his or her analyst loves understanding even the repetitious minutiae of the analysand's (mind)’ (p. 113). Insight constitutes the outcome of a vital and unique relationship. Thus, Steingart places the analytic relationship in the realm of a moral activity and good human relationship, including a love of truth. (We can feel good about our work!)

responseSet.title: The Umbilicus as Sexual Focus
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Umbilicus as Sexual Focus

E. Brown Ph.D., M.D.

E. Brown

E. Sherwood Brown, Ph.D., M.D.
5323 Harry Hines Blvd.
Dallas, Texas 75235-9070

E. Brown  

Dear Sir:

I tremendously enjoyed reading André Green’s recent paper ‘Has sexuality anything to do with psychoanalysis?’ in Volume 76, 1996, 871-883. This paper has led to several lively discussions with colleagues and certainly increased my own awareness of the importance of discussing sexual issues during psychotherapy. I would like to share with your readers a most interesting finding that emerged shortly after the publication of this paper.

The patient, Mr A, a 27-year-old man treated for ‘mood swings’, frequently described jealousy of his wife during weekly therapy sessions. During one session he described particularly intense jealousy when told by a friend that his wife had engaged in an activity called a ‘body shot’ at a local pub. Mr A described a ‘body shot’ as a currently popular activity in which whisky is poured into the umbilicus of one partner, in this case his wife, after which the other partner slowly ‘licks it out’ with their tongue. He noted much greater feelings of jealousy when thinking about this act than when thinking about actual sexual intercourse involving his wife and another man. ‘No man has the right’ to engage in this ‘sin’ with my wife he once said.

A literature search revealed no previous reports of the ‘body shot’. Surprisingly, only five brief reports on the umbilicus were found in the psychiatric literature (Waltzer, 1974; Heilbrunn, 1975; Friedman, 1976; Rosner, 1978; Yazmajian, 1983). Each article appeared in psychoanalytic journals, and reported on sexual interest or fear directed at this portion of the anatomy. The umbilicus may, in fact, be a rather common focus of sexual interest as illustrated by the apparent popularity of the ‘body shot’, but has been largely ignored in the psychiatric literature.

In conclusion, Dr Green’s paper has heightened my appreciation of the importance of exploring sexuality in therapy. However, when sexual issues did emerge in therapy, I was surprised at the paucity of recent literature available to guide me.

responseSet.title: Personal recollections and impressions of Harry Guntrip
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Personal recollections and impressions of Harry Guntrip

Alan Dabbs

Alan Dabbs

Alan R Dabbs
Senior Lecturer in Clinical Psychology
The School of Medicine
15 Hyde Terrace
Leeds LS2 9LT

Alan Dabbs  

Dear Sir:

It was with great interest that I read the ‘Personal recollections and impressions of Harry Guntrip’, which appeared in a recent issue of the Journal (1996, Vol. 77, p. 763), written by Ronald Markillie, an erstwhile colleague now retired this past 14 years.

When I joined the staff of the Department of Psychiatry at the University of Leeds in 1972 it was pleasing to note the interest in psychopathology demonstrated by the academic staff, particularly Professor Max Hamilton, no doubt a tradition of previous professors such as Henry Dick and George Hargreaves. Although he had retired many years earlier, Harry Guntrip continued to provide teaching to postgraduate students in the Department and his contribution was generally welcomed by the academic staff. It was not long before Harry came knocking on my door on those days when he attended to give his lectures.

Our weekly meetings always began with Harry offering some comments about service issues and the need to widen the scope of the education and training of mental health professionals. It often surprised me how informed he was of current issues appearing in the scientific journals. He was an avid reader and expressed concern about what he perceived as the superficiality of behaviour therapy which was dominating professional publications of the time. Harry saw symptoms as representing personal adaptations to cope with inner distress and worried what might happen if these maladaptive adjustments were removed without providing alternative ways of dealing with personal unconscious conflicts. The publications of Hans Eysenck and his cohorts loomed large in Harry’s conversation and I had to point out to him that I was not an apologist for the behaviourist movement.

He took this in good part and it proved to be a turning point in our relationship. He began to pay more attention to me as an individual and was very curious about my experiences when working at the Tavistock Clinic in the mid 1950s at a time of heightened stress in the psychoanalytic community which had only fairly recently settled down after the Anna Freud-Melanie Klein conflicts. I believe I brought Harry’s attention to the work of Bowlby, Ainsworth and Rosenbluth and he perceived an affinity between the ideas thrown up by Attachment Theory and the contributions of Fairbairn with which he was very familiar.

responseSet.title: Obituary: ERICH GUMBEL (1908-1994)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Obituary

ERICH GUMBEL (1908-1994)

Tina Sandbank

Tina Sandbank

Tina Sandbank
29 Derech Ha
’
sachayot
Ein Kerem
Jerusalem 90872

Tina Sandbank  

Dr Erich Gumbel was an innovator, a pioneer. After Max Eitingon, he was the central figure responsible for establishing psychoanalysis in Palestine. Palestine, with its pioneer spirit, might have seemed an unlikely terrain for this highly sophisticated and inner-directed discipline to take root, blossom and give fruit. But it did, and in no small part, thanks to him.

Born and educated in Germany, but being a Jew, it was impossible for him to pursue his psychoanalytic training in Europe. He came to Palestine in 1934 in the wake of Max Eitingon, who received him warmly and accepted him immediately as a candidate. Gumbel was in both analysis and supervision with Eitingon.

Psychoanalytic work was done in a spirit of enthusiasm and devotion. Money was no issue. No one was rejected for treatment because of lack of means. In fact, a small fund was raised in order to provide needy patients with a daily lunch, as an empty stomach was not considered conducive to psychoanalysis (Gumbel, 1978). There were many language difficulties. Both analysts and patients were immigrants and often there was no common language except that of the unconscious. Gumbel wrote of this period:

There were many difficult hours—too many— when I felt that the task which I had set myself was too formidable. But in spite of this, the old question, why Palestine of all places, had become irrelevant. I now saw myself as a pioneer of a new science in a new land. Psychoanalysis was my vocation. I was young and I had found my true calling in life (Gumbel, 1995).

Gumbel quickly became the central figure in the Society, working with a heavy caseload of patients, in addition to teaching. In 1953 he became President of the Society, and he stayed in this office, in rotation with H. Z. Winnik, for almost twenty years. From 1962 he was also Director of the Institute. He had a profound interest in psychoanalytic training and supervision and took part in several international panels on this subject. In 1963 he was appointed by Dr van der Leeuw to be a member of the Standing Committee on Training for the European Association. He was also a corresponding member of the programme committee of the 23rd International Conference. From 1975 to 1977 he was the associate secretary of the International Psychoanalytical Association. He was also very central in Israel's becoming host to the International Congress in 1977.

[[RunningHead]]

Response

Kenneth Eisold PhD

Kenneth Eisold

Kenneth Eisold, PhD
285 Central Park West
New York, NY 10024

Kenneth Eisold  

To the Editor

Saul Scheidlinger’s letter provides an opportunity to emphasize a key point in my paper —one that defines an important difference between perspectives on individual and group behavior. As many of your readers may not be aware of the controversies that have arisen around these clinical issues, it may be worth the effort to clarify them.

Scheidlinger is an extremely distinguished contributor to analytic work in groups, but he represents an analytic approach stressing individual behavior in group settings. That is, he may explore particular instances of transference, for example, that may be revealed in a group setting by one member to other group members, but he is relatively unaware of complex group processes involving the entire group. Many others, myself included, stress what has come to be called the ‘group-as-a-whole,’ that is, the ways in which the behavior of individuals in groups arises out of the interplay between individual motives and group process: individual members shape the group and are simultaneously shaped by it.

What Bion taught us to see was the subtle, hidden or unconscious collusive ways in which group members bring about group ends. That is, groups are not simply the passive victims of their leaders; they powerfully shape the thoughts and actions possible for members within them, including the thoughts and actions of leaders. In my view, this is not merely a matter of Bion’s ‘complex and controversial group theories’, as Scheidlinger puts it. It is a matter of clinical observation, observation about group behavior that has been verified and extended in countless circumstances.

In using Bion’s work on groups, I wanted to stress the participation of the entire Viennese Society in bringing about this first psychoanalytic schism, not merely the admittedly authoritarian behavior of Freud. Scheidlinger, because of his stress on individual behavior, places the emphasis on the primary responsibility of Freud, and so he points to his ‘authoritarian’ and ‘brazen’ behavior, demanding ‘prerogatives’, ‘loyalty’ and ‘blind obedience’. I have no interest in defending Freud’s behavior. My interest was in elucidating the complex process involving the entire group, and discriminating the different interests represented in the Society.

responseSet.title: To the Editor
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

To the Editor

Evelyne Schwaber

Evelyne Schwaber

Evelyne Schwaber  

There are surely two distinct vantage points at issue in the analytic situation, but it is Grossman (and Renik) who blur the distinction in failing to regard the patient’s view of the analyst’s vantage point as a vital focus of inquiry. In neglecting this dimension they assign a greater objectivity or truth value to the analyst’s view of his own vantage point. The analyst, in their position, becomes the Objective knower of his own Subjectivity.

Thus, while we may not be able to ‘set aside’ our own understanding, nor to ‘isolate and abstract’ our personal responses, we might recognize that we have only our own version of our understanding and of our responses. The patient may have another version—however expressed or hidden—about and from which both patient and analyst may have yet to learn.

Cordially,
Evelyne Albrecht Schwaber

159

responseSet.title: Fantasy and Reality in History.: By Peter Loewenberg. New York & Oxford: Oxford University Press. 1995. Pp. viii + 235.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fantasy and Reality in History.

By Peter Loewenberg. New York & Oxford: Oxford University Press. 1995. Pp. viii + 235.

Stanley Coen

Stanley Coen

Stanley J. Coen
390 West End Avenue, #1H,
New York, NY 10024-6200

Stanley Coen  

This book demonstrates how effective and illuminating applied psychoanalysis can be when psychoanalytic thinking is applied sparingly, as it were, with a light touch. Peter Loewenberg, distinguished academician and practising psychoanalyst, shows how he has integrated his dual training in history and psychology. Eschewing the application of psychoanalysis to history, he focuses instead on ‘the point of contact’ (p. 3) between the psychological historian and his data. Like the contemporary, post-modern psychoanalyst or literary critic, this historian seeks to understand his materials through passionate involvement with them. His examples are clear, persuasive, and engaging.

For example, I read this book at the same time I prepared a review of Daniel Goldhagen's book Hitler's Willing Executioners: Ordinary Germans and the Holocaust for another journal (Coen, 1996b). When I thought of Loewenberg's complex understanding of the multiple factors driving anti-Semitism in Germany and Austria in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries and hence the ambivalence of Jews towards Germanic society, I wished that Goldhagen, also a historian, had a psychoanalytic or at least a psychological perspective. Goldhagen views cognition and ideals as the determining factors in German behaviour without connecting these with other feelings, wishes, and needs. In part, Goldhagen uses this strategy to privilege anti-Semitic belief as the primary motivation for the willingness of ‘ordinary’ Germans to exterminate Jews. He is concerned that consideration of the motivational power of irrational feelings as universal forces would detract from the horror of German humiliation, torture, and murder of Jews. Despite Goldhagen's disclaimers, many believe that his book would have been much more enthusiastically received if he had been able to temper his argument with more balanced historical and psychological analysis. Peter Loewenberg could have provided him with excellent consultation.

Loewenberg's book offers brief sketches of people, all of whom except for Freud were (William Gladstone, Walther Rathenau, Karl Renner) or still are (Vladimir Zhirinovsky) political leaders, and of social phenomena (group creativity at the Burghölzli, anxiety in history, racism, nationalism, diplomacy). These cameos showcase Loewenberg's skill in psychological portraiture. He presents an outline of his methodology in reading autobiography of political leaders. He focuses on: affect; imagery; repetition of themes; internal conflict; absence of material; action versus inhibition of action; frustration tolerance; rationalisation; polarisation and splitting; symbolism and anxiety; trauma, demography, and leadership; and, narcissistic rage.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response

Arnold Goldberg MD

Arnold Goldberg

Arnold Goldberg, MD
Suite 1305B
Chicago, Illinois 60603

Arnold Goldberg  

Dear Sir

Dr Gabbard’s welcome letter launches what I hope will be an extended conversation about the writing and presenting of case histories. For a start it would be wonderful if we did have data about the disguise of histories, and I certainly did not mean to say that any were available. My own impression and that of numerous colleagues that I queried was that case conference presentations were regularly undisguised, and the reported cases in journal articles were only casually disguised. But some firm data to rebut my impression would be most appreciated. In terms of Dr Gabbard’s other points, I can answer briefly:

1. Disguising features of a patient may or may not have an impact on the meaning of the case. Our experience in preparing a new casebook is that it is not so simple, and even seemingly minor changes have a significance that escapes an author’s notice.

2. Of course we aim to disguise a patient report to conceal identification from others, but some patients may object to any reports about themselves. Do we get a patient’s consent for what Dr Gabbard feels is our goal? Once again, a disguise is a very complicated affair.

3. Cautioning us not to write about patients currently in treatment means either that it will affect the treatment or lead to difficulties in one’s objectivity. Does this mean that one should not even plan during a treatment to write up a case or even take notes with such in mind?

4. The problem of patient consent depends not only upon the above question of writing up patients currently in treatment but seems to require one to ask a patient for permission after the treatment is over. This is also a complicated affair. One suggestion which has very little chance of being realized is that all patients be told of the probability of being written up in disguised form at the beginning of treatment, just as our candidates inform their patients about supervision. Although this is probably a stillborn idea, it does have the virtue of uniformity and would solve lots of problems.

responseSet.title: Sabbath's Theater.: By Philip Roth. Boston and New York: Houghton Mifflin Co. 1995. Pp. 400.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sabbath's Theater.

By Philip Roth. Boston and New York: Houghton Mifflin Co. 1995. Pp. 400.

Theodore Shapiro

Theodore Shapiro

Theodore Shapiro
Cornell University Medical College
525 East 68th Street, Box 147, New York, NY 10021

Theodore Shapiro  

To paraphrase Samuel Johnson, there is something about the imminence of death that wonderfully concentrates the mind. But what sort of concentration, and what sort of thoughts fill our penultimate hours? This is essentially the theme of Sabbath's Theater—a novel with all the creativity implied in that designation and also a biographical sketch of a man revealing his anguish and rage as well as his poignant longings for some emotional equilibrium and love. Roth brazenly offers a paean to life-affirming sexuality—a desperate attempt to battle with death and its sting in a frantic, hilarious disturbance of propriety that fills the pages of this drama. Freud's vision of the discontents of civilisation is dramatically played out in the insistent intrusions of sexuality on every page as Mickey Sabbath, the anti-hero of the volume, tries to hold on to the theatre of life with all its illusions. Sabbath as Dionysius, as seducer of the young, as Priapus, as Falstaff, leaps from the pages of this raucous, almost manic journey towards and from the grave. The following dialogue depicting Sabbath defending himself to the father of the young woman whose panties he had inadvertently stolen (read: parapraxis) reveals the counterpoint of the narrative that Freud also exploits in his description of the war between Eros and the necessary social constraints of civilisation.

‘What's awful?’ Sabbath asked. ‘The kids underpants wrapped around my dick to help me through the night after the day I’d been through? That's so awful? Come off it Norm. Panties in my pocket at a funeral? That's hope’.

‘And what do you live in?’ Norm inquired. ‘You live in the failure of this civilization. The investment of everything in eroticism, the final investment of everything in sex’ (pp. 344-6).

How closely does art follow reality and culture? How much of what our novelists write is taken from other writers in this age of communication? Although Roth grew up in a post-Freudian world, his anguished tale of the plight of men in their sixties is not simply a paraphrase of Freud. Every author of ageing, or for that matter of youth, offers his/her vision of the universal problems of each period of life while lending a particular and unique twist to elicit the reader's belief in the character and his/her multifaceted struggles. The capacity to elicit a nuanced and real person is in the art. The universality of the themes can be measured across the centuries and across the author's own context and art. The problem of death does not recede from the artist's eye or the philosopher's pen, or the scientist's study in each generation. Freud, too, struggled with the theme of death repeatedly in his writing and personal struggle with cancer. We could also bring to bear Shakespeare's Lear and Falstaff, Bellow's Mr. Sammler's Planet, Freud's Civilization and Its Discontents, Sophocles’ Oedipus at Colonus, as reflective writings concerning how one remains humanly sentient and survives as the final horizon seems near. Each author offers reflections on death from the vantage point of the entry into the unknown during the later part of our three score and ten.

responseSet.title: The Talking Cure: The Science Behind Psychotherapy.: By Susan C. Vaughan. New York: G. P. Putnam's Sons. 1997. Pp. 224.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Talking Cure: The Science Behind Psychotherapy.

By Susan C. Vaughan. New York: G. P. Putnam's Sons. 1997. Pp. 224.

Theodore Shapiro

Theodore Shapiro

Theodore Shapiro
Cornell University Medical College
525 East 68th St., New York, NY 10021

Theodore Shapiro  

The talking cure, as most of us trained in psychoanalysis know, was Anna O's folksy way of describing her treatment with Freud when he replaced Breuer. Little did she realise that she and Freud had opened a new century of psychological treatment based upon talking, and as she also noted, ‘chimney-sweeping her mind’. Now, a young and thoroughly twenty-first-century psychoanalyst uses recent advances in mind-brain research to designate her role and that of psychodynamically oriented psychotherapists as ‘microsurgeons of the mind’. What better metaphor for the recent ‘decade of the brain’ in the United States and the new integration that Freud had hoped for mind and brain sciences in the next century! Indeed, Freud's own surgical analogies for technique are returned to with the prefix, micro, as the author suggests that she seeks to ‘put the neuro back in neurosis’. Caveat lector —for even as I write ‘metaphor’ I am imposing my own bias on the text. This author disavows her use of microsurgery as figurative, but tries instead to convince us of the penetrance of our words to the nuclei and fibres of the brain itself, just as the youthful Freud tried to explicate the neurophysiology of neuronal types for his Project.

In so far as I am writing to and for psychoanalysts, I first offer a caution that this is a book intended for intelligent lay people who may decide they want to visit a psychoanalyst, or for psychoanalysts or neuroscientists who wish to learn about integrative models of mind and brain described in plain English. If either of the latter reads this with the expectation that she will receive the last word on the complexities of such integration she will be disappointed. This volume is written for its appeal to a larger audience, and as such gives a tremendous advantage to our profession in its tolerance, its clarity of presentation and its simplicity. Moreover, this is a lively exposition of the case histories of a small number of patients and their interactions with their therapist that will, I would predict, convince many readers to try therapy even if they are already on Prozac or a similar drug.

[[RunningHead]]

Response

León Grinberg

León Grinberg

León Grinberg
Paseo de Bonanova 92
Barcelona 08017

León Grinberg  

Dear Sir,

I appreciate Dr Ganzarain’s interest in my paper ‘Is the transference feared by the psychoanalyst?, as well as the comments he makes regarding one of the sessions and, more specifically, my interventions. I find some of his comments interesting, whereas I disagree with others.

To his first statement, that the difficulties in handling projective counteridentification are much greater than those with transference, I would answer that ‘the transference is feared’, precisely, because it can provoke projective counteridentification reactions.

His comment that I have disregarded the patient’s guilt seems inaccurate to me. At the beginning of the clinical vignette, I specified that she was a patient ‘with a pronounced depressive background’ (p. 7). This implies that guilt was one of her main symptoms. The subject of guilt was exhaustively dealt with in previous sessions with regard to her younger brother’s death, her father’s illness as well to the denial of this guilt.

1214

responseSet.title: New Directions In This And Future Issues
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

New Directions In This And Future Issues

David Tuckett

David Tuckett

David Tuckett  

Peer reviewed papers

For some time now the Editorial team at the Journal has been seeking to explore the limits that might apply to conducting reasoned international and cross-cultural peer review. The importance of anonymously conducted peer review for the development of a healthy discipline which develops reasoned confidence in some ideas and is able to reject others was alluded to in a Guest Editorial by Michels (1995, 76: 217-235) and in subsequent policy statements (1995, 76: 653-662). The practical issue has been how to achieve peer review in an international and pluralistic discipline that in its first hundred years has paid insufficient attention to its methodological foundations and is often noted for ideological schism and reliance on argument by appeal to authority.

For some years the Editorial Boards in each region have been arranging for colleagues first independently to assess and write reports on a series of papers and then to discuss and debate the results. A joint meeting of all the Boards also attempted this for the first time at the San Francisco Congress. This has been followed both by a joint meeting of the UK and European Boards and by a series of meetings in Brazil, Chile and Uruguay. In the latter series various members of the Editorial Boards from several regions have taken part in the discussion and assessment of several pre-circulated papers together with those members of local societies who cared to do so. These efforts will be continuing.

These various meetings, spanning cultures and continents, have convinced us that it is possible to achieve a large measure of informed agreement on the fundamentals required to develop a common international standard for evaluating psychoanalytic papers. Such agreement is possible when we move from abstract discussion of possible differences to the concrete task of deciding about a particular paper. Evaluation must also be conducted suspending personal agreement or disagreement with a paper and by trying to focus instead on several indicators of the quality of argumentation: the degree of depth of thought and elaboration of ideas, the formal logical characteristics of argumentation, certain issues to do with errors of fact or narrowness of view and the quality of supporting evidence— clinical or otherwise. A paper describing our efforts and growing out of the ongoing discussion at meetings will be published later this year (Tuckett, forthcoming). What is important to note is that such evaluation criteria do not involve agreement or disagreement with the opinions expressed in the paper being assessed—something which has allowed considerable consensus to be achieved when evaluating papers even by authors whose ideas were clearly not congenial to the particular reviewer.

responseSet.title: Educational Section
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Educational Section

Robert Michels

Robert Michels

Robert Michels
418 East 71st Street
New York, NY 10021

Robert Michels  

Professional and scientific journals in general, and psychoanalytic journals in particular, serve several functions. They facilitate communication about new observations, such as interesting case histories or clinical experiences, new ideas, such as technical innovations or experimental curricula, and new theories—new ways of thinking both about and within the discipline. They also provide a forum for the review and analysis of the current state of thinking in the field. Papers in this second category focus on what is already known rather than what is new, but in doing so they can offer important new perspectives and point to new questions and problems. They discuss some concept or issue in the field, tracing its history, outlining its current status, discussing disparate views or perspectives, pointing out contradictions, paradoxes, problems and unanswered questions, and suggesting revisions or strategies for further scholarly work. They are particularly important in maintaining communication among various groups in the profession: groups defined by history, culture, language, ideological commitment, or sub-specialty interest.

The Journal is embarking on a new series of papers in this latter category. They will discuss selected essential concepts in psychoanalysis. The series will be edited by Cláudio Eizirik, of Porto Alegre, Brazil; Alain Gibeault, of Paris, France; Richard Rusbridger, of London, England, and myself. We have begun to discuss how it should be organised, which concepts will be addressed, which authors to invite, and how submissions should be reviewed. We hope to make it as interesting, useful and user-friendly as possible.

Of course this is not the first time that such an endeavour has been attempted. Laplanche & Pontalis’ The Language of Psycho-Analysis (published in French in 1967 and in English in 1973) traced the history of this type of effort to Richard Sterba's Handwörterbuch der Psychoanalyse, published in 1936-37. Daniel Lagache, in his introduction to their work, described their task as resulting from the fact that ‘words, like ideas (and together with ideas), are not merely created —they have a fate: they may fall into disuse or lose their currency, giving way to others which are better suited to the needs of fresh orientations in research and theory’ (p. viii). Laplanche & Pontalis themselves discuss the essence of their work under three headings: ‘Historical’ (‘the origins of each concept and the chief stages in its evolution’) (p. xii), ‘Structural’ (‘the position of each particular element vis-à-vis the overall structure’) (p. xii), and ‘Problematic’ (‘to make plain the ambiguities of the principal notions, to expose their contradictory aspects’) (pp. xii-xiii).

responseSet.title: A Reconsideration Of Objectivity In The Analyst
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Reconsideration Of Objectivity In The Analyst

Evelyne Schwaber

Evelyne Schwaber

Evelyne Albrecht Schwaber
4 Welland Road
Brookline
MA 02146

Evelyne Schwaber  

Dear Sir

I am writing in response to Glen Gabbard’s paper ‘A reconsideration of objectivity in

1219

the analyst’ (IJPA, 1997, 78: 15-26) to try again to clarify my own position. Gabbard faults me for seemingly holding a view which, however, is quite at odds with that which I have put forward.

He states that I do not move beyond the patient’s perspective to offer a different one, and suggests that I lose sight of the importance of objectivity in our work, that I seek simply to affirm conscious experience, and would bypass much of the patient’s unconscious communication. Yet, as I cited in a letter in the same issue in which Gabbard’s paper appears (Letters to the Editor; in response to ‘“Psychic reality” and reality testing’ by L. Grossman; IJPA, 78: 157-158, p. 159), I have written just the opposite:

It is not that the analyst ‘goes along’ with the patient’s view nor simply accepts it as such; rather, she uses her own view to enable her to locate the subtleties of the inner logic of the patient’s, which have not yet been clear to her. We must surely employ our view, or experience, even vigorously so, as an avenue to find the patient’s, as long as we acknowledge ours for what it is—how it seems from within our vantage point—and listen with this realization (1996, ‘Toward a definition of the term and concept of interaction: its reflection in psychoanalytic listening’, Psychoanal. Inq., 16: 5-24; p. 20).

Indeed, the clinical illustrations in my writings are offered to convey my consistent effort, drawing upon my own vantage point, with its subjective, experiential, sensory, and cognitive attributes, to try to locate the still hidden, as yet unconscious dimensions of the vantage point of my patient.

My argument is conceptual and epistemological: the need to keep clear the question, ‘from whose point of view?’ A more closely data-based, scientifically credible psychoanalytic elucidation of what has been unconsciously communicated, delineating hypothesis from evidence, would follow an actively sustained recognition of the centrality of this question in our clinical stance.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Delusions of Everyday Life.

By Leonard Shengold. New Haven, Connecticut: Yale University Press. 1995. Pp. 221.

Eric Marcus

Eric Marcus

Eric R. Marcus
4 East 89th Street, New York, NY 10128

Eric Marcus  

Dr Shengold has written a delightful book about the psychodynamics of narcissistic envy in its many forms, including neurotic symptoms, malignant envy, paranoid delusions, being in love, and sexual perversions. He includes an applied analysis of Samuel Butler and his work, using the pathographic approach. The book is an application of psychodynamic knowledge to these various topics. Written in a relaxed and personal style, the book is easy to read and promotes much thought about one's own patient case-load, one's approach to such patients, and one's feelings about such issues both in treatment and in daily life. The book should be used in teaching medical students, residents, as well as early psychoanalytic candidates because it is filled with understandable clinical and everyday life examples. There is a dearth of such teaching materials.

168

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Internal Mother: Conceptual and Technical Aspects of Object Constancy.

Edited by Salman Akhtar, Selma Kramer and Henri Parens. Northvale, New Jersey: Jason Aronson. 1996. Pp. 207.

Calvin Settlage

Calvin Settlage

Calvin F. Settlage
Route 4, Box 188D, Huntsville, Arkansas 72740

Calvin Settlage  

This book takes its title from Mahler's observation: ‘By object constancy we mean that the maternal image [the “internal mother” (Mahler, Pine and Bergman, 1975, p. 118] has become intrapsychically available to the child in the same way as the actual mother had been libidinally available—for sustenance, comfort, and love’ (Mahler & Furer, 1968, p. 222). On the bases of research on early mother-child interaction and clinical application, the book reviews, extends and further defines the concept of object constancy.

Two features of the book make it particularly valuable. One is the single theme. The other is the fact that the book is based on the contributions of German and American analysts who participated in the Second International Margaret S. Mahler Symposium on Child Development. The advantage of this is that evolving psychoanalytic knowledge is critically addressed through a dialogue among analysts with similar and with different cultural and training backgrounds, and with different theoretical inclinations.

The evolution of Hartmann's concept of object constancy is insightfully portrayed through the combined perspectives of several of the authors. Blum's historical review perceptively places it in the context of broader psychoanalytic thought. He notes, for example, the shifts in Freud's thinking whereby the mother, instead of being simply the object of the drives, is seen to have a crucial role in the infant's development. With the mother in this role, the threat of loss of the mother, and later, the loss of mother's love powerfully influence the child's adaptation and development as these take place initially within the mother-child relationship.

Tyson traces the evolution of the concept of object constancy from Hartmann through Spitz, Anna Freud, Piaget and Mahler, noting changes in criteria, meaning and timing of the structure's emergence in the developmental progression. Spitz and Anna Freud saw the establishment of object constancy as occurring during the latter half of the first year and as enabling the mother to keep her special place for the child whether she satisfies or frustrates. Piaget's term, object permanence, refers to the child's ability, by 18 months, to maintain and recall a stable, cognitive representation of the mother. Focusing more broadly on the internalisation process and the role of object relations in psychic structure formation, Mahler extended the concept to include libidinal constancy. She saw object constancy as reaching an initial level of structuring during the third and fourth years of life.

<autaff><addr><ln><cs style="AuthorName">Glen O. Gabbard</cs></ln><ln>The Menninger Clinic</ln><ln>P. O. Box 829</ln><ln>Topeka</ln><ln>KS 66601-0829</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Response
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response

Glen Gabbard

Glen Gabbard

Glen O. Gabbard
The Menninger Clinic
P. O. Box 829
Topeka
KS 66601-0829

Glen Gabbard  

Dear Sir

I appreciate Dr Schwaber’s clarification of her position. I’m pleased that she acknowledges the value of speaking from the analyst’s vantage point, however different it may be from the patient’s conscious perspective. On the other hand, I am concerned that Dr Schwaber has not fully understood the main thrust of my argument when she says, ‘The analyst’s inference about the patient’s unconscious intentionality does not make him more objective’. This is not my argument. Dr Schwaber is invoking the meaning of ‘objective’ as having a corner on the truth of the situation. As I argued in the paper, ‘The analyst’s position as an object, external to the thinking mind or subjectivity of the patient, offers a vantage point from which the analyst can share observations that are different from the patient’s by virtue of being external to the patient. Indeed, the patient’s unconscious intentions are often most accessible through the analyst’s careful scrutiny of his or her “object” responses’ (p. 15). In other words, it is not that the analyst has a greater access to some elusive truth about the patient. Rather, the analyst responds like other ‘objects’ in the patient’s world, although that response is inevitably filtered through the analyst’s subjectivity and therefore modified by that subjectivity to some extent.

Dr Schwaber and I both agree that a rigorous inquiry into the mutual influence of the analyst’s subjectivity and that of the patient is necessary for psychoanalytic work. We also share the view that the analyst may recognize aspects of the patient that escape the patient’s awareness. Dr Schwaber notes that I fail to address an entire array of responses or potential ones in the patient.

1221

responseSet.authorCitation: Oppenheim, D., Emde, R. N., Hasson, M. & Warren, S.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Preschoolers Face Moral Dilemmas: A Longitudinal Study Of Acknowledging And Resolving Internal Conflict

David Oppenheim , Robert Emde , Michal Hasson and Susan Warren

Susan Warren

David Oppenheim
Robert N. Emde
Michal Hasson
Susan Warren
Department of Psychology
University of Haifa
Haifa 31905
Israel

Susan Warren  

Following on from research that indicated significant moral internalisations by age 3, using a play narrative approach in which children were asked to complete story stems describing a range of moral dilemmas, the purpose of this study was to replicate the results, extend them with longitudinal information and assess the child’s developing capacities to acknowledge both sides of moral dilemmas and resolve them in a prosocial way. Fifty-one children were presented with three enacted story stems describing moral dilemmas as they might occur in everyday life. Story completions were obtained from children at ages 3, 4, and 5 and were coded for the level of acknowledgement of the dilemmas and the degree of prosocialness involved in story resolution. Results included the following: firstly, some children acknowledged the dilemmas and resolved them prosocially as early as age 3; secondly, the ability to acknowledge dilemmas and resolve them improved with age; and thirdly, children showed a greater capacity to acknowledge dilemmas with support from an examiner. The implications of these findings for our understanding early moral development are discussed, along with questions pointing to new research.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fanaticism.

By Dario Sor and Maria Rosa Gazzano. Buenos Aires: Editorial Anancé. 1993. Pp. 357.

Alicia Etchegoyen

Alicia Etchegoyen

Alicia Etchegoyen
51 Limerston Street, London SW10 0BL

Alicia Etchegoyen  

This is a very interesting book on a topic of major significance for both clinical practice and sociological understanding.

In a previous book, entitled Catastrophic Change (1987), the authors introduced the concept of ‘fanatic use’ as a seventh column on the horizontal axis of Bion's grid.

The authors’ theoretical base is that of

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1997

834

Bion's notion of ‘Catastrophic change’. This was first introduced in Transformations (1965), where Bion connected catastrophic change with the idea of mental growth. Later, in A Memoir of the Future (1981), Bion affirms that catastrophic change initiates mental growth. From this theoretical foundation the authors attempt to study fanaticism as a phenomena that annuls catastrophic change and mental growth.

This book develops to the full the concept of ‘fanatic use’, adumbrated in Catastrophic Change. A further elaboration is to contrast ‘fanatic use’, which is characterised as ‘power without responsibility’ with the positive use of ‘power with responsibility’. It then logically follows that the grid should be divided into four areas, one with a positive and one with a negative side.

The authors indicate the inherent difficulty in trying to ‘think about’ a phenomenon that, by definition, annihilates reflective thinking and emotional meaning. To address this problem they make use of a literary style ranging from paradox, imaginary dialogues with Bion, humour and discussion (chapters one to five). The book ends with a formal exposition of their theoretical ideas (Chapter Six)

The authors investigation originates from two sets of observations.

A sociological one: Sor & Gazzano (both born in Argentina), comment that

we have been born in a country where fanaticism has strongly developed. Both from a historical and everyday life perspective we are often exposed to areas of fanaticism that are accepted without criticism or questions, as a national ‘way of being’, as it were. We found ourselves severely limited in our capacity to think about this type of mental functioning characterised by the overwhelming power of psychological ideas denuded of emotional meaning (p. 241).

responseSet.title: Psychic Reality And Psychic Testing
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychic Reality And Psychic Testing

Evelyne Schwaber

Evelyne Schwaber

Evelyne Albrecht Schwaber
4 Welland Road
Brookline, MA 02146

Evelyne Schwaber  

Dear Sir:

I am writing in response to Dr. L. Grossman’s critique of my position in his paper, ‘“Psychic Reality” and Reality Testing’ (Int. J. Psycho-Anal., 1996, 77: 509-515). I believe it is incumbent upon me to do so lest the reader be misled to believe that I would sit by while a patient was ignoring a potentially life-threatening physical symptom, or, that there is anything inherent in my position that heightens the risk of such an eventuality. Regrettably, Dr. Grossman does not appear to have discerned the epistemological and conceptual implications in what I have written.

While he states that I suggest we ‘enter into the patient’s “psychic reality”’, viewing ourselves ‘as if one were not influencing it’ and ‘as able to suspend one’s own’ (p. 515), I have in fact emphasized the contrary, that our participation is a constant and continuing aspect of the patient’s experience, and that all we can know is from the vantage point of our own psychic reality. I do speak of the effort to locate the patient’s psychic reality, which includes the patient’s perception of our participation. Thus, I do not agree with his view that ‘empathy is … the appreciation of what a situation would mean to the analyst it he or she were in similar circumstances’ (Grossman’s italics), but an effort to apprehend what it means to the patient in those circumstances, realizing that this always reflects our own understanding.

I have written, ‘It is not that the analyst “goes along” with the patient’s view nor simply accepts it as such; rather, she uses her own view to enable her to locate the subtleties of the inner logic of the patient’s which have not yet been clear to her. We must surely employ our view, or experience, even vigorously so, as avenue to find the patient’s as long as we acknowledge ours for what it is—how it seems from within our vantage point—and listen with this realization’ (1996, p. 20). My central epistemological position has to do with the need to recognize the distinction between what are differing vantage points, essential to our understanding of the nature of our analytic evidence. Thus, when Grossman speaks of the ‘danger in the analyst’s temptation to overlook the fact that he or she is perceiving from the analyst’s vantage point’ (p. 515)

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Heresy: Sandor Rado and the Psychoanalytic Movement.

By Paul Roazen and Bluma Swerdloff. Northvale, NJ & London: Jason Aronson Inc. 1995. Pp. 219.

Nathan Kravis

Nathan Kravis

Nathan M. Kravis
141 East 55th Street
New York, NY 10022

Nathan Kravis  

Heresy was once a matter of life and death. Perhaps it still is. In her preface to this memoir, based upon her interviews with Sandor Rado recorded in the 1960s and edited for publication by Paul Roazen, Bluma Swerdloff notes that Rado has been ‘effectively written out of psychoanalytic history’ (p. vii). This is a remarkable vanishing act for someone who was once a close colleague of Freud's, Rank's successor as editor of the Internationale Zeitschrift für Psychoanalyse and Imago (1924-34), the first Education Director of the New York Psychoanalytic Institute (1931-41), and then the founding director of the Columbia Psychoanalytic Center (1945-55). With the trope of their title and their stated intention to redress this seemingly bizarre act of historiographic erasure, Roazen & Swerdloff suggest that Sandor Rado (1890-1972) was a brave dissident who took on the leviathan psychoanalytic establishment of his day and paid the ultimate price.

Having immersed himself in Freud's writings and performed yeoman service for the psychoanalytic movement at Freud's behest, Rado transformed himself into an independent and original thinker, becoming one of the earliest and most outspoken critics of psychoanalysis from within the profession. Once he cast off the mantle of acolyte, Rado waxed eloquent in his commentaries on the stultifying features of organised psychoanalysis and emerged in the 1930s as a self-styled champion of intellectual freedom. While still serving as Education Director at the New York Psychoanalytic Institute, Rado delivered an impassioned critique of psychoanalytic training, deriding the tendency to indoctrinate rather than foster true intellectual growth. At around the same time he embarked on a series of radical and unprecedented critiques of libido theory. At that time and in that context, these were truly courageous dissents from psychoanalytic orthodoxy and they earned Rado the enmity of many of his colleagues.

Later in his career, already demonised for his critique of Freudian drive theory, Rado (1956) was unafraid to challenge the sacrosanct centrality of the Oedipus complex, positing instead a supraordinate craving for omnipotence and a primordial fear of starvation, ideas that today sound strikingly Kleinian. Indeed, Melanie Klein referred repeatedly to two of Rado's early papers on intoxication and melancholia (Rado, 1926, 1927).
