responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Het geval Freud

Han Israëls

Han Israëls

Han Israëls
Amsterdam School for Social Science
Research
Oude Hoogstraat 24
1012 CE Amsterdam

Han Israëls  

Dear Sir

The October issue of your journal contained a ‘Book review essay’ by Mark Solms about my Dutch book Het geval Freud (The Freud Case). According to Solms, my scholarship is ‘extremely poor’ and the book is ‘riddled with errors and half-truths’. In order to illustrate my ‘lack of respect for the truth’, Solms criticizes five passages from the book. Since most of your readers are not able to check Solms’ comments because they do not read Dutch, I would like to show that his criticism is nonsensical.

1.Solms quotes from my book:

‘Freud’s [1934] letter to Meller clearly contradicts the image created by Bernfeld [1953] and Jones [1953] to the effect that Freud reacted without rancour to Koller’s discovery [of the local-anaesthetic property of cocaine]. So it is therefore quite understandable that Eissler wanted to keep the letter secret.’

Solms comments:

‘Israëls does not present any evidence to support his assertion that Freud reacted with rancour to Koller’s discovery.’

Solms carefully avoids mentioning what I quote from this letter, which Freud wrote spontaneously in response to a certain Professor Meller who had published about Koller’s discovery of half a century before:

‘K. Koller … was—and probably still is—a pathological personality, a self-tormenting quarreller, who was generally disliked because of his disputatiousness and his continuous aggressiveness.’

2.

Solms quotes from my book:

‘One treasure is still under Eissler’s command: he is the one who decides who does and who does not gain access to the letters that Freud wrote to his fiancée between 1882 and 1886.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From Instinct to Self: Selected Papers of W. R. D. Fairbairn Volumes I & II.

Edited by David E. Scharff & Ellinor Fairbairn Birtles. Northvale, New Jersey & London: Jason Aronson. 1994. Volum I—Pp. 172, Volume II—Pp. 490.

David Riley

David Riley

David W. Riley
73 Woodland Rise, London N10 3UN

David Riley  

Ronald Fairbairn's work has had a powerful influence on psychoanalytic thinking; an influence that, as the editors note, has been insufficiently acknowledged until recently. They are referring, of course, to the public recognition conferred by printed scientific debate. However, in this reviewer's experience, Fairbairn has always exerted a quiet influence, at least in the education of psychoanalysts and psychoanalytical psychotherapists in Great Britain. The state of affairs described by the editors is currently being remedied; a number of scholarly and critical appreciations have recently been published within the wider context of a burgeoning interest in comparative studies of what is broadly referred to as the British object-relations school, for example, Greenberg & Mitchell (1983) and more recently Grotstein & Rinsley (1994), together with Sutherland's biographical study Fairbairn's Journey into the Interior (1989). My impression is that the editors’ contention of neglect is overstated inasmuch as Fairbairn's informal influence may have been greater than they suppose: this is the ‘quiet’ aspect referred to earlier. For example, Eric Rayner (personal communication) described how many British analysts, especially Independents, mentioned Fairbairn surprisingly frequently when asked to name an early influence.

Nevertheless, it is misleading to maintain, as the editors do in their Introduction to Volume I, that his work has become ‘an accepted core of the thinking of the Independent Group of British Analysts’. In this reviewer's opinion, there is no accepted core in the editors’ theoretical sense: the core of the Independent group is a never-ending struggle to maintain respect for diversity amongst what is judged to be truly psychoanalytical.

Critical evaluations of Fairbairn are available in the aforementioned texts and elsewhere. The intention in these two volumes is to collate the unpublished and uncollected papers so that clinicians and scholars have ready access. These two volumes, together with Psycho-Analytic Studies of the Personality, would constitute the ‘collected works’.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Wording And Telling Sexuality

Christopher Bollas

Christopher Bollas

Christopher Bollas
1a Well Road
London NW3 1LJ

Christopher Bollas  

A patient remembers a childhood moment many times. Here are a few versions.

‘I was playing with a friend and … (pause) … he, ah, … or … I … ah … we … I suggested … we look … we tried to see … ah … (pause) … who could urinate furthest. I … ah … think I said … ah … “why don't we look at your mother's bloomers”.’

‘“X” and I were … just … fooling around. Urinating. We—one of us, but I think it was me— said “let's go have a look at your mother's bloomers”.’

‘I was with a chum with whom I had been having a contest to see who could urinate the furthest and later when we were sitting next to a low blue table with my nanny, I said to him “let's go get your mother's bloomers”.’

Although the basic structure of this memory remained unchanged, each telling slightly altered the narrative meaning.

The patient had a degree of interest in female undergarments and during childhood would procure clothing from a friend's sister or mother.

Let me word that differently.

The patient was fascinated by women's panties and as a child would sneak into homes and snatch what he felt was a kind of prize.

Or another way.

The patient stole into the houses of friends where he ripped off women's underwear which he used to excite himself.1

Each wording creates a slightly different event as the work of revision (nachträglich) re-signifies the remembered.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Correspondence of Sigmund Freud and Sándor Ferenczi, Volume 2, 1914-1919.

Edited by Ernst Falzeder and Eva Brabant with the collaboration of Patrizia Giamperi-Deutsch.

Thierry Bokanowski

Thierry Bokanowski

Thierry Bokanowski
48 rue des Francs-Bourgeois
75003 Paris

Thierry Bokanowski  

This second volume of the Freud-Ferenczi correspondence covers the period from July 1914, just after the assassination in Sarajevo of the heir to the Habsburg thrones, Archduke Franz Ferdinand, to the end of December 1919 and thus includes the entire First World War. The large number of letters exchanged (341) during these four and a half years is largely attributable to Freud's isolation, the Great War having prevented him from communicating as easily with correspondents outside the countries of the Triple Alliance (Germany, Austria-Hungary and Italy). This naturally brought him closer to Ferenczi, whom he met frequently owing to the freedom of travel between Vienna and Budapest.

Although this period was sombre and depressing in many respects, it was one of astounding creativity and productivity for Freud; for example, he composed his metapsychological papers. He shared the advances recorded in them unreservedly with his young friend, who in return informed him of his own writing plans and forwarded his papers to him.

However, a central aspect of these four and a half years of correspondence is Ferenczi's analysis and its repercussions both on the relationship between the two men and on Ferenczi's private life.

Ferenczi's analysis

Freud had been hesitant about this project, which he had been trying to put off for some years. However, in the upheavals of the time, Freud now somewhat reluctantly agreed to take Ferenczi into analysis. It proceeded in three phases between 1914 and 1916: three weeks from 1 to 20 October 1914; just over three weeks from 13/14 June to 10 July 1916; and a fortnight from 29 September to 12 October 1916. Although Ferenczi, like Freud, acknowledged on various occasions that this analytic experience was by no means negligible, it is clear today that Ferenczi never really felt that he had benefited from it as much as he had hoped.

While Freud was often dazzled by the exceptional analytic ability of his disciple, he sometimes found himself in a difficult position when confronted with Ferenczi's constant demands for help, which obviously had transference roots. Up to this point, Freud had sought as far as possible to play for time by advising him to continue his self-analysis. During those years of bitter fighting for ‘the Cause’ (die Sache), Freud seems to have been convinced that a transference on to psychoanalytic theory —which he regarded as substantially representing himself—would in itself constitute a sufficient analytic reference point for Ferenczi and an adequate equivalent to transference on to his person.

responseSet.title: The Problem of Perversion.: By Arnold Goldberg. New Haven: Yale University Press. 1995. Pp. 204.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Problem of Perversion.

By Arnold Goldberg. New Haven: Yale University Press. 1995. Pp. 204.

Lucy Lafarge

Lucy Lafarge

Lucy Lafarge
239 Central Park West, New York, NY 10024

Lucy Lafarge  

As psychoanalysis has become more pluralistic, contemporary psychoanalysts have become more aware of the influence of theory upon clinical data. An analyst hears and interprets facts that his theory prepares him to recognise. A good new theoretical model helps us to identify new patterns in clinical material. For the best analytic clinicians, theory structures listening, but not so tightly that the analyst cannot be surprised. Ideally, the influence of theory upon clinical data is reciprocal; surprising clinical findings lead the analyst to reconsider and revise his theory. In his recent volume, The Problem of Perversion, Arnold Goldberg, a leading self-psychologist, shows himself to be an excellent analytic listener. As we follow his exposition of the psychology of perversion, new patterns come into focus. These have the potential to enrich the clinical work of analysts from other schools, as well as those who work within self-psychology. It is to Goldberg's credit that his clinical observations sometimes challenge aspects of his model. At times, these lead him to broaden his theoretical frame; at other times, he appears to strain his theoretical model to contain them.

Goldberg's focus on the unfolding and developmental history of self-object transferences brings into high relief two organising themes that appear to coexist in the mental life of the person with a perversion. The first might be called a theme of deficient psychic structure. The pressure that these patients feel to enact their perversions, or even to fantasise about them, rises and falls with the status of their self-object ties. The absence or disruption of the tie to the self-object makes them more symptomatic; the restoration of the self-object tie brings relief. Because the ebb and flow of symptoms appears responsive only to the presence or absence of the self-object bond (and not to the circumstances or dynamics associated with its breach or restoration), Goldberg attributes this aspect of perverse psychopathology to a failure of internalisation. This theme as well as its prescribed cure by transmuting internalisation is a familiar one within self-psychology. The second theme might be called a theme of distorted psychic structure. The perverse patient's often observed split sense of reality may be seen, from the vantage point of self-psychology, to be embedded in a tie to a split self-object. This split self-object tie quickly emerges in the transference; the patient communicates detailed fantasies and memories of its history in both words and enactments. Its resolution requires detailed interpretation and reconstruction.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Aim Of Psychoanalysis In Theory And In Practice

Joseph Schachter

Joseph Schachter

Joseph Schachter
401 Shady Avenue
Pittsburgh, PA 15206-4409

Joseph Schachter  

Dear Sir:

Steiner describes a modified, traditional psychoanalytic theory of the etiology of neurosis, but acknowledges ‘it is not much use to us when we are actually in a session trying to understand what is going on’ (1997, p. 1082). My thesis is that if traditional psychoanalytic theory of etiology is not clinically useful, and, further, if its validity is increasingly open to question, we need to consider whether to maintain and defend this theory, which subjects psychoanalysis to widespread, destructive criticism, such as Stone’s (1997) recent article.

Steiner’s version of traditional psychoanalytic theory, which seems to apply to males but is not extended to females, is that ‘the wound to self-esteem that arises in the Oedipus situation … the castration threat … from a malignant powerful father’ (p. 1078) gives rise to resentment and the desire for revenge. These feelings block reintegration of the splitting created by projective identification, resulting in a narcissistic personality organization. Although reintegration of projected parts of the self may well produce therapeutic benefit, it cannot be argued, as Arlow (1995) has, that whatever makes a patient well (reintegration of split-off parts) must have been what had made him ill (irreversibly split-off parts of the self). To test Steiner’s etiological theory that the most irreversibly bound projections in childhood lead to the greatest adult psychopathology probably requires something like a prospective longitudinal study from childhood to adulthood of twins, which is essentially impossible.

In relation to another disorder, hypertension, although some patients benefit therapeutically from a low salt diet, it cannot be argued from that fact that a high salt intake had caused the hypertension. Further, it is quite clear in medicine and surgery that many disorders are treated effectively even though the etiology of the disorder, as, e.g., with hypertension, is unknown. So, too, psychoanalytic treatment may be beneficial whether or not we understand the etiology of the patient’s disorder. Thus, Steiner could demonstrate that reintegration of split-off parts of the personality is therapeutic without hypothesizing an etiological theory, one he characterizes as not useful clinically, and one whose validity is questioned from various points of view by increasing numbers of analysts.

responseSet.title: Internet Site Discussion: ‘A Reconsideration Of Objectivity In The Analyst’ By Glen O. Gabbard
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Internet Site Discussion: ‘A Reconsideration Of Objectivity In The Analyst’ By Glen O. Gabbard

Paul Williams

Paul Williams

Paul Williams
Flat 1, 15 Rosslyn Hill
London NW3 5UJ

Paul Williams  

The third journal paper to be made available for downloading and discussion from the IJPA Home Page (address http:/www.ijpa.org) was Glen Gabbard's assessment of the degree to which the psychoanalyst is capable of maintaining objectivity (or not, as is argued by social constructivists, intersubjectivists and perspectivists). Gabbard begins by surveying the contemporary field and cites the burgeoning literature (generated by a range of disciplines of a post-modern hue) that calls into question the existence of the concept of objectivity. This is a vast subject (as was soon apparent from the discussion) and Gabbard concentrates on recent theoretical derivatives from within psychoanalysis, judiciously leaving the wider philosophical debate. The subsequent, extensive discussion addressed many of the underlying philosophical implications, and this interested some contributors and irritated others. This raises an important point: it is evident that the exposition of the philosophical premises which underpin psychoanalysis is needed, not least from the responses to Gabbard's paper. However, avoidance of specialisation and intellectualisation, while maintaining a link with the clinical experience, is not easy in this area, and is perhaps even impossible to achieve. The result is that many readers are soon deterred. Would it be of interest to IJPA readers to have a distinct forum within the Home Page and/or Journal (a suggestion made at the end of this review) in which such issues could be given the attention they deserve?

Within the disjointed theoretical and technical debates that take place around the relative significance of drives, object-search, conflicts, deficits, levels of fixation and one-person or two-person psychologies, Gabbard regards the ‘objective’ versus ‘subjective’ controversy as exerting fundamental influence on contemporary psychoanalytic practice. The antagonism belies much diversity of thought. Two familiar, overarching positions might be: extreme relativism, which holds that objective truth per se is impossible to establish, and realism/perspectivism, which holds that both participants in the analytic dialogue shed light on emerging reality. Intersubjectivism reflects the realist/perspectivist stance in a variety of forms and to differing degrees (e.g. Natterson's ‘co-equality’ of perspective, Ogden's concept of the ‘analytic third’, Renik's ‘inescapable’ subjectivity and Gill's equation of intersubjectivity with constructivism). This level of flux requires an epistemology that accounts coherently for both subjectivity and objectivity in the analytic setting. Gabbard's proposal involves recasting the meaning of the term ‘objectivity’ according to its origins (a derivative of the term ‘object’) and he does this in the light of the different perspective the psychoanalyst-as-object introduces to the patient.

responseSet.title: The Many Faces of Eros.: By Joyce McDougall. London: Free Association Books. 1995. Pp. 257.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Many Faces of Eros.

By Joyce McDougall. London: Free Association Books. 1995. Pp. 257.

Jennifer Johns

Jennifer Johns

Jennifer Johns
88 Nightingale Lane, London SW12 8NR

Jennifer Johns  

‘Human sexuality is inherently traumatic.’ These are the first words in Joyce McDougall's book. Towards the end, she reminds us that, ‘all symptoms are attempts at self-cure in the face of unavoidable mental pain’. McDougall sees psychic life as a series of conflicts arising throughout development and in need of different strategies at each stage in order for the subject to survive and prosper as an individual. The multiplicity of possible responses and solutions to the repeated conflicts in psychosexual development gives rise to her title, The Many Faces of Eros.

In her prologue to this book McDougall focuses on her metaphor of psychoanalysis as a journey, a voyage, and draws a parallel with the journey of sexuality itself, which, in repeated need of solutions, is engaged in an eternal quest. Her book is not so much a map of that quest as a travel book, reminding us of the efforts of the earliest explorers of these strange lands, and in her clinical examples relating the vicissitudes of the journeys in which she has travelled alongside her own patients, sharing their pain, mapping their internal territories, the deserts, internal monsters and seemingly impassable barriers that have led them to their present plight. As such a travel companion, she shows her knowledge of and sympathy for her patients and their internal territories, and by showing those of us engaged on similar explorations and mapmaking expeditions the theoretical and practical equipment that she uses, as well as her techniques of use, she once more intrigues us with the exposition of her skills, and encourages us on our own way.

McDougall's roots are always with Freud; she shows us both the triumphs and failures of his struggles to understand and make sense of sexuality, and celebrates his discoveries of the importance of the child's attempts to master and eventually come to terms with the internal conflicts aroused by bisexuality, the oedipal situation, masturbation and primal scene fantasies.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Self-Envy: Therapy and the Divided Inner World.

By Rafael E. López-Corvo Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson. 1995. Pp. 211.

Elizabeth Spillius

Elizabeth Spillius

Elizabeth Bott Spillius
20 Achilles Road, London NW6 1EA

Elizabeth Spillius  

Rafael López-Corvo advances his interesting concept of self-envy to explain, at least in part, some of the self-destructive behaviour, acting out, negative therapeutic reactions, addictive

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1997

1254

behaviour and perverse transference of certain borderline and narcissistic personalities. He defines self-envy as ‘produced between an excluded part and a creative part of the self’ (p. 10) or, in more complex fashion, as

the envious attack by a part of the self, usually related to childhood self-objects, against another part of the self identified as a creative and harmonious mother-father or parent-sibling relationship, also within the self, which is now transferentially projected as a means of avoiding superego accusations (p. 24; quoting from his own article on self-envy in the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis, 1992).

López-Corvo’s approach is basically Kleinian, though with many additions, alterations and terminological changes of his own. He bases his idea of self-envy on Scott’s 1975 paper, ‘Self-envy and envy of dreams and dreaming’. He also uses Rosenfeld’s idea of conflict between parts of the self in destructive narcissism (Rosenfeld, 1971), Meltzer’s ideas about tyranny (1973) and John Steiner’s work on perversion of the transference (1982).

I think that López-Corvo’s term ‘self-envy’ is striking and stays in one’s mind, though I think it is slightly misleading. I believe that he really means envy of or by an internal object; he uses the term ‘self-envy’ because he thinks of ‘self’ as comprising all the inner world. This perhaps comes about because of ambiguity in the meaning of ‘self’; sometimes it is used to mean the total personality, sometimes to mean a part of the total personality. Where Klein usually distinguishes between ‘self’ (sometimes called ‘ego’) and ‘internal objects’ in the inner world, I believe that López-Corvo does not do so consistently, and usually thinks only of parts of the ‘self’. I believe that it is his view that when an object is introjected, it is identified with the self, and that if someone or part of someone is really an ‘object’, it must be external. It is for this reason, I believe, that he uses Kohut’s term ‘self-object’ or his own addition, ‘part self-object’, rather than ‘internal object’, a term that he uses rarely. This definition of ‘self’ has led him to develop complex terminology that is not likely to be easily or universally understood.

responseSet.title: Sexuality, Femininity, And Contemporary Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sexuality, Femininity, And Contemporary Psychoanalysis

Phyllis Tyson

Phyllis Tyson

Phyllis Tyson
3252 Holiday Court, Suite 102
La Jolla
California 92037-1807

Phyllis Tyson  

All of us struggle in our own way in our quest for love and satisfaction. Clashes inevitably arise as we attempt to experience, to tame, to control, and yet to know that sometimes wild, primitive, instinctual, exciting and creative inner force. Discord also arises as psychoanalysts continue their struggle to grasp the essence of this force, a force that has been called sexuality. Of all the aspects of human nature that have preoccupied psychoanalysts for over a century, sexuality takes centre stage. That the debate continues is, in part, because of our heritage from Freud, for there is tension within Freud's theories of sexuality—a mind-body tension, a tension among differing hypothetical levels of theory, and a tension caused as different subjects are classified as one.

The mind/body question dates at least as far back as Descartes. Freud's (1905) proposition of an ‘endosomatic’ force, libido, as central to the workings of the mind, brought that debate to the centre of psychoanalysis. While this ‘endosomatic’ force was purely hypothetical, Freud's theory was also a clinical one, as he was convinced that ‘the most significant causes of every case of neurotic illness are to be found in factors arising from sexual life’ (1898, p. 263). Concerns about the effects of erotic excitement, sexual urges and related unconscious conflicts on psychic functioning were central to all models of the mind that Freud proposed. In thinking that ‘anatomy is destiny’ (1924), Freud's interests turned to the concept of self. He ascribed distinctly separate biologically determined destinies to man and to woman. On the other hand, he separated ‘physical sexual characters’ and ‘mental sexual characters’ (1920, p. 170), and exploring ‘the psychical consequences’ of anatomical distinctions (1925), he insisted that psychological sexual features be distinguished from anatomical ones. He noted that contradictions arise as both women and men experience male and female parts of their character; their sense of masculinity or femininity is determined partly by early identifications and partly by the unconscious fantasies that arise with the recognition of anatomical difference.

responseSet.title: Child And Adolescent Psychoanalysis: Research, Practice And Theory
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Child And Adolescent Psychoanalysis: Research, Practice And Theory

Jonathan Cohen

Jonathan Cohen

Jonathan Cohen
Teachers
’
 College
Columbia University
300 Central Park West
New York, NY 10024
—
1513

Jonathan Cohen  

In the last fifteen years, there have been three major developments in child and adolescent psychoanalysis and psychoanalytically informed psychotherapy. After briefly reviewing what is clear and unclear about the nature of child psychoanalysis, the author describes and critiques three recent trends: (1) a greater differentiation of aetiological factors that involves an enhanced understanding about developmental influences and how they may be utilised therapeutically; (2) a shift in child analytic theory to a more equitable balance between internal and external factors, with more attention being paid to the external; and (3) a significant increase in the psychoanalytically informed, empirical study of development as well as the process and outcome of treatment. Changes in these three overlapping domains are examined and their relevance to clinical practice discussed. It is suggested that the increase in the systematic and empirical study of the child psychoanalytic process has been the most significant recent development in the field; it is this that has the greatest potential to minimise rhetoric, to support discovery and to clarify what is unique about psychoanalytically informed work with children and adolescents.

responseSet.title: The End Of Time: A Psychoanalytic Perspective On Ingmar Bergman's Wild Strawberries
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The End Of Time: A Psychoanalytic Perspective On Ingmar Bergman's Wild Strawberries

Jacob Arlow

Jacob Arlow

Jacob A. Arlow
94 Wildwood Road
Great Neck, NY 11024

Jacob Arlow  

On the morning of a special day, the day he is to receive an honorary doctorate for his contributions to humanity and science, Dr Izak Borg awakens from an unusual and disturbing dream. He is a testy old man of 75, a widower, with a surviving mother of 95, and a son, Ewald, like himself a physician. As Wild Strawberries, Ingmar Bergman's classic masterpiece, unfolds, through dreams, reminiscences and the events of that single day, we come to know and understand the central character with a penetrating insight, the kind that, as a rule, comes only from long and arduous psychoanalytic treatment. Accordingly, this film should hold a special enchantment for psychoanalysts.

In the dream, Dr Borg finds himself in a strange and unfamiliar neighbourhood, devoid of any sign of life. He looks up at a street clock. It has no hands. From his pocket he pulls a watch, a junior version of the larger clock. It too has no hands. All around there is dead silence, but at that moment we begin to hear the beating of Izak's heart. (For a clock without hands, time does not move. Izak is in the realm of timeless eternity, i.e. death.) Bewildered, Izak looks about and sees a man standing with his back to him. As he touches him, the man turns slightly, his face swathed in gauze-like bandages, obliterating his features.

The figure topples over and the head falls away from the body as blood gushes from the neck into a small pool. (In effect, Izak has killed a dead man.) At that moment the silence is broken by the tolling of church bells, while around the bend in the street comes a hearse drawn by two horses but with no rider. As the hearse proceeds past Izak, one of the rear wheels gets caught in a lamppost. The hearse thrusts forward, thrusts forward again and again, until the wheel, freed from its attachment, rolls back at great speed, barely missing Izak. In the process, the hearse tilts over, expelling the coffin it had held. As Izak approaches the coffin, the corpse grasps his hand, pulling him closer. To his consternation, Izak sees that the face of the corpse is his own. He awakens, deeply disturbed.

responseSet.title: Libido And Lethe: Fundamentals Of A Formalised Conception Of Metapsychology
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Libido And Lethe

Fundamentals Of A Formalised Conception Of Metapsychology

Cordelia Schmidt-Hellerau

Cordelia Schmidt-Hellerau

Cordelia Schmidt-Hellerau
Sprecherstrasse 4
8032 Z
ü
rich
Switzerland

Cordelia Schmidt-Hellerau  

On the basis of a chronological review of some of Freud's principal metapsychological writings, the author inquires into the logical consistency of the theoretical foundations of psychoanalysis. Particular attention is devoted to the functions ascribed by Freud to the two basic axioms of his model of the psyche, namely the two antagonistic drives (originally called the sexual and self-preservative drives and ultimately the life and death drives) on the one hand, and a principle (inertia, constancy, pleasure or Nirvana) on the other. The author adopts a formalised view of these concepts, considering the drives as vectors and concentrating on their directionality. The psychical apparatus as modelled by Freud is then seen to be constructed along homoeostatic lines, the dynamic stability of the psyche as a system being assured by the combination of, firstly, a (+)-drive and a (-)-drive as variables and, secondly, a structure, in accordance with the relevant regulating principle. She shows that Freud remained faithful to this basic idea throughout his career notwithstanding all the changes in his theory. Preserving this consistency and with a view to the exploration of a fresh dimension of the mind, the author suggests the introduction of a new concept, lethe, for the energy of the death drive.

responseSet.title: To the Editor
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

To the Editor

Lee Grossman

Lee Grossman

Lee Grossman
5335 College Avenue, Suite 27
Oakland
California 94618

Lee Grossman  

I am grateful to Dr. Schwaber for her clarification. These are difficult and important epistemological issues, so I will try to elaborate on the inferences I draw about Schwaber’s

158

position, and how I would distinguish it from my own.

When Schwaber takes issue w

ith my view of empathy as an appreciation of what a situation would mean to the analyst in similar circumstances, and says instead that it is an attempt to apprehend what it means to the patient, ‘recognizing that this always reflects our own understanding’, I am not sure what she means; if it reflects our understanding, on what is that understanding based, if not on the analyst’s actual or imagined personal experience—i.e., a so-called ‘trial identification’? The inference I draw from this sentence is that Schwaber believes that we can somehow account for the factor of our own understanding and more or less set it aside as we consider the meaning to the patient. Renik draws a similar inference about Schwaber’s position, referring to her ‘assumption that an analyst’s personal responses can be isolated and subtracted from the rest of his or her analytic activity’ (p. 561).

Schwaber’s 1992 paper is entitled ‘Counter-tran

sference: the analyst’s retreat from the patient’s vantage point’. I do not think it is a trivial distinction between the analyst trying to appreciate and make sense of the patient’s experience, withi

n the analyst’s own largely unconscious categories of listening, and the analyst apprehending the patient’s vantage point; in my view, the latter is impossible. I see the analytic process as a dialogue between two distinct vantage points that eventuates in both parties expanding their self-perceptions.

Cordially,
Lee Grossman

November 1996

responseSet.title: Obituary: Henriette R. Klein (1901-1996)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Obituary

Henriette R. Klein (1901-1996)

Robert Michels

Robert Michels

Robert Michels
Cornell University Medical College
525 East 68th St., Box 170
NY 10021

Robert Michels  

Henriette R. Klein, one of the leaders of the Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research and of the psychoanalytic profession in New York City, died at her Carnegie Hill Manhattan brownstone on 11 May, 1996 at the age of 94. Born in October 1901, her career exemplified the best of American psychiatry and psychoanalysis. She was a pioneer in the ascendancy of women to leadership roles in those professions, and an important model for the many people who followed her.

Klein was born in Evanston, Illinois and received her B.A. from the University of Chicago and her M.D. from the University of Maryland. She trained in psychiatry and child psychiatry at Columbia's New York State Psychiatric Institute and the Phipps Clinic at Johns Hopkins, and in psychoanalysis at the New York Psychoanalytic Institute, graduating in 1940. She served on the faculties of the Downstate Medical Center of the State University of New York, Emery University and the Columbia University College of Physicians and Surgeons, and was a central figure at the Columbia University Psychoanalytic Center from 1949 until her death. She became the first woman president of the Association for Psychoanalytic Medicine, and of the New York District Branch of the American Psychiatric Association, as well as the first woman trustee of the American Psychiatric Association from New York.

Klein made important scientific contributions to the study of childhood schizophrenia, the psychology of pregnancy and the psychodynamics of paranoia. She conducted one of the first systematic studies of psychoanalytic candidates, and published a monograph describing her findings. Her colleagues recognised her character and her special interest in professional ethics, by selecting her to chair the ethics committees of both psychiatric and psychoanalytic organisations. She was sought out as a consultant, a supervisor and a teacher, and was beloved by her many students as a mentor and a friend. Generations of analysts at Columbia saw her as the model of what a psychoanalyst should be.

responseSet.title: Thomas H. Ogden, ‘Reconsidering three aspects of psychoanalytic technique’Int. J. Psycho-Anal, 1996, 77:883-899
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Thomas H. Ogden, ‘Reconsidering three aspects of psychoanalytic technique’Int. J. Psycho-Anal, 1996, 77:883-899

Alfred Plaut

Alfred Plaut

Alfred Plaut
Rezonvillestr. 1
12167 Berlin

Alfred Plaut  

Dear Sir,

Ogden’s reconsiderations originate from a combination of Bion’s concept of reverie and Winnicott’s ‘third area’, i.e. between reality and fantasy, as well as the fruitful tension between communication and non-communication (privacy). Much of Ogden’s paper strikes a chord and is also in accord with Debussy, whose remark on the ‘space between the notes’ as the location of music he quotes. Yet I felt that ‘the notes’ receive insufficient attention, as without these there can be neither space nor music nor analysis. Here are two examples:

The first concerns the recognition and interpretation of the transference. If this is not sufficiently delineated, the reveries of patient and analyst must lead to an idealisation of the concept of reverie. This, in turn, would lead to a failure of technique in the direction to which Ogden himself refers as the ‘privilege of the positive transference’. We never hear of a reverie that transforms a beta-element of mute aggression or the death instinct into a relatively harmless (because manageable) image like the analyst throwing the patient out of the window or vice versa. In other words, there must be an analytic second such as a dialogue about disagreement before there can be ‘the analytic third’.

Second, and intimately connected with the analysis of the transference, is the establishment of trust. Winnicott, in Playing and Reality, (1971, p. 102) quoted from a paper of mine on this very point. To get to it may take up a lot of analytic time and is not usually possible during the opening phases of analysis. (Ogden makes no distinction between phases.) I fear that the premature or idealised reverie and privacy may gloss over this hurdle. The patient could have his own good reasons for distrusting the analyst. In that case, the analyst may be in a reverie about the patient but the patient will interpret the analyst’s

silence as his having a fantasy .

In Analysis Analysed (Plaut, 1993), I called the third area the ‘thematic centre’ around which the ‘mutual transference’ revolves. This demonstrates that Bion’s ‘thoughts in search of a thinker’ are not particular, whether they settle in the head of someone called a ‘Freudian’ or a ‘Jungian’ analyst. The thoughts ignore copyright, but are often in tune with the spirit of the times, or Zeitgeist, although their expression may vary from one terminology to another.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud, Judéité, Lumières Et Romantisme.

By Henri Vermorel, Anne Clancier and Madeleine Vermorel. Lausanne, Paris: Éditions Delachaux et Niestlé. 1995. Pp. 395.

Denise Weill

Denise Weill

Denise Weill
31, rue Claude-Bernard, Paris 75005

Denise Weill  

In this book, whose title translates as ‘Freud, Jewishness, the Enlightenment and Romanticism’, Henri and Madeleine Vermorel and Anne Clancier present contributions from a colloquium that they organised in 1990 at the Cerisy Cultural Centre on the subject of ‘Freud and psychoanalysis from Goethe and the German romantics to Viennese modernity’.

René Roussillon, one of the seventeen authors1 represented in the book, writes: ‘A vital question today concerns the management of the Freudian heritage, primarily by psychoanalysts, but also throughout the cultural region to which psychoanalysis may be relevant’. These contributions are extremely important from this point of view.

Against this background it is essential not to ‘fetishise’ Freud or to see him as self-engendered, unconnected with the culture that nurtured his ideas; he did not bring about a complete epistemological break, but should on the contrary be regarded as falling within the mainstream of western thought. The object of this book is to demonstrate the plurality of sources and predeterminations of Freud's thought, a vital task if that thought is to survive in the long term.

The erudition of the organisers and participants here assembled—psychoanalytic researchers, philosophers, scientists and experts in German language—shows how it was possible for psychoanalysis to arise at the intersection of the various currents that moulded the identity of its creator—an identity which, we are reminded, had the German language as its vehicle.

Translated by Philip Slotkin, MA, MITI.1 Janine Altounian, Jacques Ascher, André Bourguignon, Jacquy Chemouni, Michel Düe, Jean Gillibert, Jean Guillaumin, Bertrand Méheust, Bernd Nitzschke, Darius Gray Ornston, Catherine Parat, Francis Pasche, Jean Rochet, René Roussillon, Henri Vermorel, Madeleine Vermorel, Perel Wilgowicz.

responseSet.title: An Introductory Dictionary of Lacanian Psychoanalysis.: By Dylan Evans. London: Routledge. 1996. Pp. 200.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Introductory Dictionary of Lacanian Psychoanalysis.

By Dylan Evans. London: Routledge. 1996. Pp. 200.

Bernard Penot

Bernard Penot

Bernard Penot
36, rue de l
’
Arbal
è
te, 75005 Paris

Bernard Penot  

This work contains about two hundred entries, representing the terms used by Lacan over the course of nearly fifty years. It is an attempt to elucidate Lacanian psychoanalytical language, often accused of being deliberately obscure—‘an attempt’, Dylan Evans says, ‘to open Lacanian discourse up to wider scrutiny and critical engagement’. The author considers a dictionary a good instrument for shedding light on any theoretical discourse, because it has the same structure as a language: both are synchronic systems whose individual terms have no positive existence in so far as they are defined by their mutual differences. Lacan said of his own discourse that ‘each term is sustained only in its topological relation with the others’ (1973, p. 89).

In his introduction, Evans points out that earlier Freudian dictionaries—such as the classic work of Laplanche & Pontalis (1967) and that of Rycroft (1968)—have served as valuable tools for exploring psychoanalytic theory. But none of the dictionaries in French that deal extensively with Lacanian terms has yet been translated into English, so the anglophone student has been without a useful tool of reference until now.

Evans is aware of the inherent dangers of his project. The first is that ‘by emphasising the synchronic structure of language, the dictionary can obscure the diachronic dimension’ (p. x), i.e. the fact that psychoanalytic terms change their meaning with use. This concern has led him to incorporate etymological information and to trace the evolution of Lacan’s concepts from their usual beginning in his reading of Freud. ‘By showing how the changes are often gradual and hesitant’, the author hopes to avoid some ‘simplistic narratives of epistemological breaks’. Observing that ‘whenever Lacan’s terms acquire new meanings, they never lose their older ones’, Evans sets out to counter the illusion that all of Lacan’s concepts were always ‘already there’.

The entries have been chosen mainly on the basis of the frequency with which they occur in Lacan’s work (e.g. symbolic, neurosis); but some other terms that are not specifically Lacanian (Kleinian psychoanalysis, ego-psychology, I.P.A.) have been included to provide a historical and contextual elucidation of Lacan’s research. Still others (sexual difference, object relation) represent important sets of related themes, which would otherwise be divided into separate entries.

responseSet.title: Understanding Therapeutic Action. Psychodynamic Concepts of Cure.: Edited by Lawrence E. Lifson. Hillsdale, NJ and London: The Analytic Press. 1996. Pp. 261.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Understanding Therapeutic Action. Psychodynamic Concepts of Cure.

Edited by Lawrence E. Lifson. Hillsdale, NJ and London: The Analytic Press. 1996. Pp. 261.

Steven Ablon

Steven Ablon

Steven Luria Ablon
62 Chestnut Hill Road Chestnut Hill, MA 02167

Steven Ablon  

Therapeutic action is a subject always close to the heart of psychoanalysts, psychodynamic psychotherapists, patients, and all of us who hope to develop, improve and get better. However, therapeutic action is challenging in its elusiveness and has at its centre a great paradox. Therapeutic intent often interferes with therapeutic action and theories about therapeutic action often promote a limited perspective. In 1909 Freud wrote in a letter to Jung, ‘To salve my conscience I often tell myself: Above all, don’t try to cure, just learn and earn some money! These are the most useful conscious aims’ (p. 288. In The Letters of Sigmund Freud, ed. E. L. Freud. London: Hogarth Press, 1961, Letter No. 145.) About twenty years ago I was sitting with twenty-five other new psychiatric residents when Elvin Semrad (a training and supervising analyst and Clinical Director of the Massachusetts Mental Health Center) told us, ‘I do not want any of you curing patients’. As I recall, the room grew hushed. Semrad continued, ‘Just investigate, investigate, investigate’.

Lifson describes the mission of the book as

the hope of elucidating a comprehensive overview and synthesis of the different models of the therapeutic action of psychodynamic psychotherapy. We believed that such an approach needed to take into consideration structural conflict, structural deficit, and relational deficit (p. xi).

An important strength of this book is that Lifson has selected accomplished theoreticians and clinicians from differing points of view. This stimulates the reader to integrate these perspectives in his or her own unique and shifting amalgam. The book is divided into three parts, the first being ‘Therapeutic Structure and Structural Change’. This section is oriented towards structural conflict, although discussions of interventions based on drive and defence are limited and discussed from an historical viewpoint. Alonso’s first chapter, ‘Toward a new understanding of neutrality’, provides a valuable historical review of the concept of neutrality. It serves as a useful first chapter because it provides an overview of many ideas about the nature of therapeutic action. The presence of clinical vignettes here and in many other chapters is particularly helpful in enabling the reader to obtain a sense of the author’s point of view. Alonso’s chapter proposes that the evolution of ideas about neutrality has resulted in neutrality ‘having more to do with concepts such as fair-mindedness, non-judgmental empathy, nonintrusiveness, non-possessive caring, and respect for all aspects of the patient’s inner life’ (p. 9).

In the second chapter, Meissner discusses the therapeutic alliance and its relationship to the real relationship.

responseSet.title: Object Relations in Severe Trauma: Psychotherapy of the Sexually Abused Child.: By Stephen Prior. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson. 1996. Pp. 188.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Object Relations in Severe Trauma: Psychotherapy of the Sexually Abused Child.

By Stephen Prior. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson. 1996. Pp. 188.

Arthur Green

Arthur Green

Arthur Green
350 Central Park West
New York, NY 10025

Arthur Green  

This book presents an object-relations theory of childhood sexual traumatisation based on the author's clinical experience with sexually abused and neglected boys. He takes issue with the traditional psychoanalytic approach to borderline patients and individuals with severe personality disorders for failing to acknowledge the importance of traumatic childhood experiences of abuse and neglect in generating their psychopathology. Prior traces the contributions of various analysts working with borderline children and adults. He credits Mahler for introducing the concept of trauma caused by aberrant mothering during the rapprochement period as a precursor of borderline psychopathology. He also cites the work of Rinsley in describing the impact of this early trauma on the dynamics of disturbed children, e.g. lack of object constancy, excessive splitting, inadequate defensive structures, stunted ego development, confusion between self and others and the presence of unmetabolised part-object representations and unintegrated, primitive affects. Prior suggests, however, that the major focus on the rapprochement period as the source of traumatogenesis is unnecessarily limiting.

In reviewing the contributions of traditional object-relations theory to the understanding of adult borderlines, Prior criticises Kernberg and Pine for overemphasising the importance of innate constitutional factors contributing to an intensification of drive activity and ego deficits in the genesis of borderline psychopathology, while overlooking the role of external trauma.

The author perceives a recent shift in the reconceptualising of psychopathology in the light of an increased understanding of childhood trauma occurring in abused children. The role of child maltreatment has become increasingly recognised as a factor contributing to borderline personality disorder and PTSD by clinicians working directly with abused children (Terr, Goodwin, Finkelhor) and with adult survivors of parental physical and sexual abuse (Herman, van der Kolk, Shengold). Prior observes that the recent empirical studies of childhood trauma focus more on observable symptoms, and fail to discuss the real nature of the child's intrapsychic processes because they are less accessible to empirical verification. For example, Terr's (1991) research with traumatised children stressed the presence of strongly visualised or repeatedly perceived traumatic memories, repetitive behaviour, trauma-specific fears, changed attitudes towards life and the future and the dread of repeated traumatisation.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Michael Fordham: Innovations in Analytical Psychology.

By James Astor. London: Routledge. 1995. Pp. 271.

David Black

David Black

David M. Black
30 Cholmley Gardens, London NW6 1AG

David Black  

I should begin this review by declaring an interest. I trained first as a ‘Jungian’, and then, finding myself conceptually more and more unhappy in the Jungian world, re-trained as a psychoanalyst. Michael Fordham, the doyen of British Jungians, was in many ways a comforting figure for me at the time of my unhappiness: he had found a different solution, but I felt he had encountered much the same difficulty.

responseSet.title: Retaking The First Steps Towards Symbolisation: A 6-Year-Old Emerges From Adhesive Identification
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Retaking The First Steps Towards Symbolisation

A 6-Year-Old Emerges From Adhesive Identification

Teresa Leite

Teresa Leite

Teresa Rocha Leite Haudenschild
R. Tom
é
 de Souza, 1029 City Lapa
05079-200 S
ã
o Paulo SP
Brazil

Teresa Leite  

The author begins by reviewing, mainly with reference to the ideas of Bion, the development of the sense of identity and the transition from primitive modes of object-relating involving adhesive identification to more advanced forms. The major part of the paper comprises a clinical account of a three-year period in the analysis of an initially 6-year-old borderline girl, who communicated with the analyst mainly in drawings. The development of the patient's capacity for symbolisation and of her level of communication is illustrated by examples of her productions from different phases of her therapy. The author contends that if the analyst can accept and symbolise the primitive relationship, the analysand can develop his or her own symbolisation capacity based on the introjection of a comprehensive object. After two years of analysis the patient is shown to have acquired an ability to contain her own emotions when she draws a volcano to represent her rage. Later, after a significant dream in which a man appears for the first time, she is seen relating on a more advanced level, having given up her adhesive defence, although the necessary process of mourning for the primitive object is stated to be still in progress.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic Technique and the Creation of Analytic Patients.: By Arnold Rothstein. Madison, CT: International Universities Press. 1995. Pp. xviii + 147.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Technique and the Creation of Analytic Patients.

By Arnold Rothstein. Madison, CT: International Universities Press. 1995. Pp. xviii + 147.

Alan Skolnikoff

Alan Skolnikoff

Alan Z. Skolnikoff
205 Edgewood Avenue, San Francisco
CA 94117

Alan Skolnikoff  

This book describes the author's experience in practising psychoanalysis with patients demonstrating a wide degree of pathology. He acknowledges his debt to Stone (1954), who long ago advocated this extension of the psychoanalytic method. Rothstein clearly advocates that analysts should immerse themselves more deeply in analytic practice. His challenge is clearly and repeatedly stated to the reader: with the increasing questions about the viability of psychoanalysis as a science or clinical practice, how does an analyst at whatever stage of his career respond to the question of involvement? Can analysts continue to practise analysis maintaining the conviction that it is the best treatment for a wide variety of emotional conditions?

Throughout, he offers rich theoretical and clinical perspectives that will inspire analysts of different persuasions to re-explore their own emotional involvement and commitment to the task of conducting clinical analysis. Several points are central in his argument: in choosing patients for analysis, we should focus less on our initial diagnostic or prognostic questions and more on the feeling that we may have about whether or not we can help the patient with their psychological conflicts at this time. An undue focus on diagnostic questions or analysability often hides countertransference attitudes. In making the recommendation for treatment, we should convey an attitude of optimism about the psychoanalytic method as the treatment of choice for the patient. This does not imply that every patient is suitable for or ultimately wants analysis, but only that a suitably long trial of analysis will determine whether this is the appropriate treatment. Ideal patients are intelligent, relatively compliant, co-operative individuals who can express their transferences in words. The author stresses that other kinds of patients can also be suitable if the analyst is able to introduce modifications in technique. This includes defiant, uncooperative, enactment-prone patients with whom the analyst's task is to translate their actions into words.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Les chaînes d’Éros (Actualité du sexuel)

[The Chains of Eros (Contemporary sexuality)]. By André Green. Paris: Éditions Odile Jacob. 1997. Pp. 290.

Thierry Bokanowski

Thierry Bokanowski

Thierry Bokanowski
48, rue des Francs-Bourgeois, 75003 Paris

Thierry Bokanowski  

With Freudian metapsychology going through a critical stage and psychoanalysts divided into groups with conflicting theoretico-clinical and practical approaches, it has become impossible to ignore the scale of the attack directed at the Freudian conception of sexuality and the drives by recent theoretical reformulations in psychoanalysis. In certain currents of psychoanalytic thinking this has led to the adoption of radical positions that effectively dispense with the concepts of drive, libido and sexuality and attempt to replace them with alternative hypotheses that their advocates claim have the advantage of greater closeness to the analytic situation.

Throughout over a quarter-century of achievements in the clinical and theoretical field, André Green has always demonstrated his adherence to the central place and fundamental value, in working with the mind, of the concepts of sexuality and the drives bequeathed to us by Freud. Many articles and book chapters give ample evidence of this (1973, 1983, 1984, 1990, 1995a, b), in particular one of his most recent pieces, ‘Has sexuality anything to do with psychoanalysis?’ (1995c), which was the subject of a highly acclaimed lecture and also appeared to herald a more extensive piece of work.

Now, in Les chaînes d’Éros, his fourteenth book, Green gives a wide-ranging exposition of the revision, or rehabilitation, of the status of sexuality and the drives that he considers to be essential for psychoanalysis today, beginning with a critical evaluation of some contemporary theoretical viewpoints.

Green's thesis might be usefully summarised with this simple sentence: ‘This work arises from the need to shed some light on sexuality, and on the problem that its status is creating for post-Freudian psychoanalysis’ (p. 261). Stressing that contemporary sexuality seems to lack the aura that gave the earliest psychoanalysts the ‘sense of being explorers of the mind, discoverers of a new continent’, the author points to the diminishing importance of sexuality in psychoanalysis (a ‘desexualisation of theory and practice’, p. 26) and to the great reduction in status that he considers it has undergone in the last half-century both in the psychoanalytic literature and in psychoanalytic practice and theory.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

La interpretación en psicoanálisis; fundamentos y teoría de la técnica.

[Interpretation in psychoanalysis; fundamentals and theory of technique]. By Joan Coderch. Barcelona: Herder. 1995. Pp. 502.

Mercedes Samanes

Mercedes Samanes

Mercedes Samanes
Avenida Reina Victoria 56
28003 Madrid

Mercedes Samanes  

This diligently composed work on interpretation in psychoanalysis is very interesting in a number of respects. The author offers a highly systematic and didactic study of the theory of technique and the many technical variations that exist. The book will also stimulate the reader to question his own theoretical positions. Although it is stressed that theory contains a portion of truth and must be seen as a hypothesis to be verified with the patient in the clinical situation, it seems inevitable that interpretation, the conceptualisation of the analytic process and the therapeutic objectives will be influenced by one's theoretical postulates.

Joan Coderch, whose background is Kleinian, carries on a lively and enthusiastic dialogue with authors of different allegiances. He compares the various theoretical contributions and measures them against his own enormous clinical experience. His independence emerges when he expresses his personal critical views.

In my opinion, in discussing the transference and its interpretation the author is embarking on a complex and controversial adventure. He seeks to live with different conceptualisations of the theory of technique. For all the merits of his wish to reconcile different theoretical paradigms, he thereby runs the risk of introducing contradictions in the attempt to integrate theoretical postulates that sit uneasily together.

In the first chapter, on the complexity of the act of interpretation, he conveys his desire to pursue this task in depth. The aim of interpretation based on the Freudian model of The Interpretation of Dreams is to reveal the hidden sense of the clinical material. In the genesis of the interpretation designed to obtain access to the unconscious meaning of the transference fantasy, his preferred approach is that of intuitive understanding based on countertransference empathy. According to the author, the logical-deductive component of the interpretation unfolds in working through, investigation and communication.

[[RunningHead]]

Self-Sensuality In Fantasy: Reflections On Early Development Based On A Case History

Jorge Tizón

Jorge Tizón

Jorge L. Tizón
Lauria 115, 3
º
, 1
ª
08037 Barcelona

Jorge Tizón  

On the basis of a detailed account of the analysis of a severely disturbed female patient with suicidal tendencies and pronounced mood swings, in whom sensory impressions were particularly important, the author investigates the phenomena of hypersensuality and self-sensuality. These are shown to be closely bound up with the patient's primitive erotisation of both the transference and her life outside the analysis, their function being one of self-feeding designed to blot out the awareness of separation and separateness. Depending on a given subject's personality development and the position from which mental events are experienced, the author notes that this self-sen-suality may be external, acted out or largely confined to fantasy. The case history leads to some theoretical reflections, supported by the results of infant observation conducted by the author and his assistants, on the possible connection between erotised patterns of relating in such patients and the existence of autistic nuclei. In the final part of the paper, the author considers whether the two familiar Kleinian positions need to be supplemented by a third, ‘autistic’, position, discusses the relevant consequences and implications, and emphasises that the assumption of the existence of such a position carries the risk of its reification. He ends with a poetic example that sums up the isolation of the patients concerned.

[[RunningHead]]

Response

Allan Rosenblatt

Allan Rosenblatt

Allan D. Rosenblatt
Suite 205
3235 Holiday Court
La Jolla, CA 92037

Allan Rosenblatt  

Dear Sir:

I welcome Professor Cavell’s clarification of her positions. Without prior consultation with the author, reviewers are usually constrained to discern the author’s conceptions and beliefs from the text of the book. I will address Cavell’s disclaimers as she lists them, with the appropriate quotes from her text.

1) My full statement is: ‘Acknowledging that patients’ thoughts are often distorted, she asserts that they are responses to what are usually veridical perceptions. In this respect, she appears to revive the long discredited “doctrine of immaculate perception’” (p. 189). Note the qualifiers ‘in this respect’ and ‘appears’. On p. 6 of her book, Cavell states: ‘although the patient’s thoughts are often distorted, they are generally responses to perceptions that are more or less veridical’. It is just this belief that perceptions are veridical registrations of external reality, rather than constructed by interaction between external stimuli and subjective wishes, fears, and other imaginative processes, that has been termed by Schimek (1975, Int. Rev. Psychoanal., 2: 171-87), Sandler & Sandler (1987, p. 332, Int. J. Psychoanal., 68: 331-41), and no doubt others, as ‘the doctrine of immaculate perception’.

2) I have not ignored Cavell’s distinction between affects and emotions. Indeed, I cited it in my statements that she ‘takes a cognitivist position that thought is a necessary condition for emotion, and, since she believes thought does not exist pre-verbally, therefore the pre-verbal infant does not experience emotion … Similarly animals are said to lack affects that have any propositional content, i.e. emotions, in her terminology’ (p. 190). My point was not to ignore or even contest her somewhat idiosyncratic redefinitions of affect and emotion, but to point out ‘her all-or-nothing attitude about propositions … rather than acknowledging a continuum from the most rudimentary to quite complex abstractions’ (p. 190). Cavell’s continuum from ‘affects’ to ‘emotions’ addresses how much propositional content (as she defines propositions) an affective state may have, but my continuum addresses how rudimentary vs. complex is the almost always present propositional aspect of the state. Perhaps our differences lie in definitions of what constitutes a proposition. Even the most primitive ‘evaluative attitude’ (to use Cavell’s term) of ‘This is bad for me’, to my mind, counts as a proposition.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Femininity And Masculinity: Clinical Notes On Mutual Fears

Ramón Fandiño

Ramón Fandiño

Ramón P. Fandiño
Av. N. S. Copacabana 1018, s. 306
Copacabana
22060 Rio de Janeiro RJ

Ramón Fandiño  

There seems to be no end to misunderstandings and trauma, and to the defences against trauma in the realm of sexuality. Patriarchal society created gross ways to control women: the harem, chastity belts and clitoridectomy. Women were seen as embodiments of ‘evil’, associated with witchcraft, menstruation and pregnancy, and as having the power to pervert men, who were ‘good’. The Malleus Maleficarum of Krämer & Sprenger (1486), which deals mostly with women, was the basic book of the Inquisition. Even after the French Revolution of 1789, which brought radical changes throughout the world, the view of woman as passive and hypochondriacal, whereas men were active, continued to hold sway.

Men determined who was a prostitute, a mentally ill person or a pimp—all of whom were excluded from society and could live only in brothels. A powerful reaction formation, both positive and negative, gives rise to two types of man: the macho man (like Ernest Hemingway, or the man in Marlboro cigarette commercials) and the weakling, under the thumb of woman, complaisant and doing his best to satisfy all her desires, at the cost of depersonalisation and loss of his virility and masculinity. Feminist revolutions followed both World Wars in this century: men initially scoffed at these developments, ultimately failing to understand what was happening to women, and vice versa.

In his paper on the taboo of virginity, Freud (1918) comments that women who have just lost their virginity (except those who become mothers) may castrate men out of revenge: hence the custom of the jus primae noctis, in which the strongest, most powerful or most mature men must deflower the bride. As we know, a taboo arises when people are afraid of something: man fears that woman will deprive him of his strength; of being infected by woman's femininity. For a man who is afraid, the relaxation and drowsiness after sexual intercourse may become the prototype of his fear.

Let me add in passing that it can be argued that a final version of woman imagined by the male psyche appears in the work of Freud as the ultimate truth about woman: women are castrated men, who envy the penis, are ashamed of their own bodies, feel jealous as a direct result of this envy, have little sense of justice owing to their weak superego, and cannot achieve a good mental structure because they are not afraid of castration; thus, they do not renounce their oedipal wishes or internalise sexual prohibitions, narcissism and futility. To ground his theory of femininity Freud links activity to masculinity and passivity to femininity; he also points to a real phallic distinction, according to whether the individual is endowed or castrated.

Internet Site Discussion: Papers By Otto Kernberg And John Steiner

Paul Williams

Paul Williams

Paul Williams
Flat 1
15 Rosslyn Hill
London NW3 5UJ

Paul Williams  

In common with many academic journals, the IJPA is now ‘on-line’ at the IJPA Home Page, located on the Internet at the address http//:www.ijpa.org. The Home Page, created and edited by Joseph Sandler, contains information about journal subscriptions, submissions, staff, contents, forthcoming events, book reviews, links with other psychoanalytic Internet sites and a ‘live’ discussion group, the launch of which is considered in this review. The Internet is accessible to millions of people for the cost of a local telephone call, offering unprecedented access to information, often (though by no means always) of high quality. This fact has led one psychiatry journal editor to comment recently that within ten years the norm will be for journals to be published electronically, with printed versions available upon request. Many people who would not otherwise come into contact with the IJPA can now download information and psychoanalytic papers, and engage directly with psychoanalysts. Making contact with a wide audience is one method by which the contemporary identity of the psychoanalyst can be supported. That this is necessary may be seen, for example, from the ways in which therapies deriving from psychoanalytic concepts have proliferated and have become confused with psychoanalysis, in the public imagination. For the distinctive nature of psychoanalysis to be maintained, and for psychoanalysts to avoid being seen increasingly as ‘trainers of the trainers of trainers’, thoughtful engagement with a wider world is a requirement, not an option.

The IJPA discussion group, which is open to anyone who wishes to subscribe (to join, e-mail the Web Site Editor with your name and e-mail address), makes a pre-published paper (taken from the next issue of the journal) available for downloading free of charge. Comments on the paper are invited, restricted to 300 words per subscriber. These are edited and two, three or more contributions are sent as a bulletin by e-mail to subscribers on a weekly or fortnightly basis. It is unusual to be able to watch a discussion evolve in this way, not least to see how misunderstandings can arise in vivo, as demonstrated below. For several years David Tuckett has encouraged greater use of the journal's correspondence pages: the Internet would appear to be a less intimidating forum, judging by responses to date. That the discussion group is open to the public is potentially stimulating and hazardous. Here, the editorial role of Joseph Sandler is indispensable for the maintenance of openness and seriousness. Currently, contributors are not required to identify their relationship to psychoanalysis, which can leave readers in the dark about who is commenting. A line or two summarising identity (training, location, work etc.) would correct this. A further difficulty is that the quality of contributors’ English varies greatly.

responseSet.title: Normal Child and Adolescent Development.: By Ralph Gemelli. Washington, DC: American Psychiatric Press, Inc. 1996. Pp. 582.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Normal Child and Adolescent Development.

By Ralph Gemelli. Washington, DC: American Psychiatric Press, Inc. 1996. Pp. 582.

M. Richmond

M. Richmond

M. Barrie Richmond
1116 Linden Avenue, Highland Park, IL 60035

M. Richmond  

Dr Ralph Gemelli has taken great pains to write a clear, readable bio-psychosocial model of child and adolescent development—a psychiatric book for beginning mental health professionals. For readers coming out of medical school, psychiatric residency training programmes, or undergraduate and graduate programmes in social work and psychology, Gemelli provides a wealth of observations and insights about the child's and adolescent's maturational growth and development. To some extent, it leads the reader to sources and more intensive readings.

On the one hand, one can't help but respect the dedication and scholarship—the bibliography is thirty pages!—behind the author's extraordinary integrative effort. However, in its inclusiveness, which is particularly strong on infant research studies, e.g. Daniel Stern, Beebe, Emde, and cognitive development, e.g. Piaget, the psychoanalytic reader immediately misses the centring of psychological life in the dynamic unconscious; especially, the absence of an emphasis on the organising importance of genetic antecedents and developmental transformations in the self formation and character development of the child and adolescent. (Remarkably, the word character—or personality —and the term character development are absent in an otherwise well-indexed monograph.)

Gemelli brings together child development literature, memory and learning theory, infant research, and neurobiological object-related developmental lines. As the book's chapter contents, index, and cover jacket show, there is a wide range of psychoanalytic, cognitive psychology, and neuropsychiatric topics and subheadings considered as he follows his biopsychosocial model (Chapter Two) beginning with infancy (Chapter Three), toddlerhood (Chapter Four), early childhood (Chapter Five), latency (Chapter Six) and concluding with adolescence (Chapter Seven). For example, under the major heading ‘Adolescence’ one finds an extraordinary range of topics and subheadings: adaptational capabilities, biosocial stimuli, cognitive capabilities, defence mechanisms, major developmental tasks, gender identity, object-relations conflicts, self-regulation, sexual identity, social identity development, superego etc.

responseSet.title: Writing in Psychoanalysis.: Edited by Emma Piccioli, Pier Luigi Rossi and Antonio Alberto Semi. London: Karnac Books. 1996. Pp. 129.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Writing in Psychoanalysis.

Edited by Emma Piccioli, Pier Luigi Rossi and Antonio Alberto Semi. London: Karnac Books. 1996. Pp. 129.

Andrea Sabbadini

Andrea Sabbadini

Andrea Sabbadini
38 Berkeley Road, London N8 8RU

Andrea Sabbadini  

This is the first volume of a new Monograph Series produced by the Rivista di Psicoanalisi (the Journal of the Italian Psychoanalytic Society) for the English publisher, Karnac Books. The praiseworthy purpose of the Series is to make the writings of Italian psychoanalysts known to the wider English-speaking analytic world by addressing, in the words of the Editors, ‘a long standing problem—the relative isolation of our Psychoanalytic Society as a result of language barriers’ (p. vii). Indeed, the English-speaking professional community is likely to have some familiarity with only those few Italian colleagues whose books have so far been translated: Fornari, Gaddini, Mancia, Nissim Momigliano, Piontelli etc.

The papers included in Writing in Psychoanalysis were originally presented in 1993 at a colloquium organised by one of the book's Editors, Antonio Alberto Semi, and subsequently published in the Rivista. Including contributions by both Italian and ‘foreign’ participants, this Symposium consisted of a critical reflection upon the Rivista's own regulations, its structure and the relationships among its component parts, for the purpose of improving it through the diagnosis and treatment of its pathology. I believe that it is uncommonly courageous for an institution such as a psychoanalytic journal to embark upon this sort of public self-scrutiny, even more so as the Italian Psychoanalytic Society was then undergoing a historically momentous process of internal splitting that must have made such a self-analysis, necessary as indeed it was, also extremely painful.

Semi's own contribution makes reference to the complementary positions of spoken and written words in classical cultures. In the Greek one ‘it is a written text [Plato's Phaedrus] on the topic of writing, that praises the oral transmission of knowledge, whereas in Judaism reflections on writing relied for many centuries upon oral tradition’ (p. 55). These observations provide Semi with a backdrop to his views concerning the difficulty of translating our experiences into words. He wonders whether psychoanalytic knowledge can be transmitted only through oral, or only through written, language and points out that if writing in (or about) our profession is difficult, ‘writing about writing psychoanalysis is even more so’ (p. 58). Semi reaches the intriguing conclusion that ‘we find ourselves trapped between two apparently contrasting considerations. On the one hand, it is necessary to write psychoanalysis. On the other hand, writing psychoanalysis is impossible’ (p. 68).

responseSet.title: The Engineer's Thumb Or Sherlock Holmes On The Trail Of ‘The Uncanny’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Engineer's Thumb Or Sherlock Holmes On The Trail Of ‘The Uncanny’

Jacques Batail

Jacques Batail

Jacques Batail
20 all
é
e de la romance
95800 Cergy-le-haut
France

Jacques Batail  

Freud identified ‘primal phantasies’ (life in the womb, ‘primal scene’, seduction, castration). It is argued that ‘The Engineer's Thumb’, a short story from ‘The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes’, draws its uncanniness from the fact that it is underpinned by all the primal phantasies described by Freud. ‘The Engineer's Thumb’ therefore illustrates what analytical interpretation can contribute to the understanding of certain literary works. ‘The Engineer's Thumb’ may also serve as an introduction to a broader study of the Freudian concept of ‘the uncanny’. This study seems to confirm what Freud said when he pointed out that ‘an uncanny experience occurs either when infantile complexes which have been repressed are once more revived by some impression, or when primitive beliefs which have been surmounted seem once more to be confirmed’ (1919, p. 249). ‘The Engineer's Thumb’ has another interesting feature: in this short story, Conan Doyle, by setting up a ‘talking cure’, anticipates the creation of psychoanalysis and highlights in a striking way certain aspects of what was to become psychoanalytical treatment.

responseSet.title: Winnicott's Therapeutic Consultations Revisited
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Winnicott's Therapeutic Consultations Revisited

A. Brafman

A. Brafman

A. H. Brafman
20 Hollycroft Avenue
London NW3 7QL

A. Brafman  

Winnicott described in his book ‘Therapeutic Consultations’ (1971) how a diagnostic assessment of a referred child developed into a fruitful therapeutic intervention when he was able to discover the unconscious fantasy that underlay the child's symptoms. Because these were children who were, essentially, developing normally, he used the word ‘knot’ to depict the obstacle the child had met. Any conflicts the parents might have were not explored in that context. This work presents cases in which child and parents are seen together for the diagnostic assessment. The child's feelings about his world and his difficulties are explored through a variety of techniques including drawings. In the same interview, an analytic enquiry into the parents’ history and also their views of the child reveals how the child's fantasies and the parents’ past experiences interact and create a mutually reinforcing vicious circle. In other words, the ‘knot’ involves all of them. If the child's unconscious fantasy can be verbalised and if the parents are able to approach the child in a manner that acknowledges the child's real needs, the ‘knot’ disappears and normal development can be resumed.
