responseSet.title: Obituary: WILLIAM GOLDFARB (1915-1995)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Obituary

WILLIAM GOLDFARB (1915-1995)

Donald Meyers

Donald Meyers

Donald I. Meyers
4560 Delafield Avenue,
New York, NY 10471

Donald Meyers  

William Goldfarb was something of a wunderkind. He achieved his PhD in experimental psychology with distinction under the mentorship of Irving Lorge at Columbia University Teacher's College at the unusually young age of 21 and soon after, working with the Foster Home Bureau of the New York Association for Jewish Children, completed his research on the effects of institutionalisation on young children, a seminal piece of research in which he studied the distortions left in the personalities of adults who had experienced early childhood institutionalisation.

This study, one of the first of its kind, strongly influenced the movement away from institutionalisation of dependent children and towards foster care. For this outstanding and most practical contribution to the mental health scene he was presented with the Devereux Schools Award for Child Research at the Centenary Meeting of the American Psychiatric Association in 1944 at the age of 29, the first of many honours he received during his research career. His findings about early institutionalisation were featured in John Bowlby's report, Maternal Response and Child Mental Health, sponsored by the World Health Organisation.

Bill went on to do research as a public health officer and then, because of his conviction that a psychiatric and psychoanalytic perspective would contribute importantly to his research efforts, in his early thirties undertook medical training at Cornell University Medical School, completed a residency in psychiatry at Columbia University and psychoanalytic training at the Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research, broadening his research perspective and adding an exceptional clinical facility to his talents.

Recognising his special abilities, two of his mentors, Abram Kardiner, a pioneer in the application of psychoanalytic thought to anthropological research, and David Levy, another genius of child development research, gave him strong moral and financial support during these years of professional development and he maintained a close personal and professional relationship with them throughout their lives.

responseSet.title: Obituary: PETER BLOS (1903-1997)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Obituary

PETER BLOS (1903-1997)

Aaron Esman

Aaron Esman

Aaron H. Esman
1100 Park Avenue 1B,
New York, NY 10128

Aaron Esman  

Peter Blos died in his 94th year on 12 June 1997 at his home in Holderness, New Hampshire. He is survived by his wife, Betsy Thomas Blos, his son, Peter Blos, Jr, M.D., his daughter, Lillemor Beenhouwer, four grandchildren and three great-grandchildren.

The customary biographical data—his birth in Karlsruhe, Germany; his teaching diploma from the University of Heidelberg; his Ph.D. in biology from Vienna; his emigration to the United States in 1934; his years of teaching and guidance work in New Orleans and New York—evoke some of the flavour of his restless, enquiring mind and his devotion to education and psychological service to the young. But the heart of his professional life and the organising core of his work derived from those years in Vienna when he became a part of the psychoanalytic circle, began his psychoanalytic studies, and served as director of the now legendary Experimental School, in collaboration with Anna Freud, August Aichhorn, Dorothy Burlingham and his childhood friend Erik Erikson. Out of this experience grew his commitment to the welfare of the young, a commitment from which he never wavered and to which he brought the full measure of his creative energy.

My first encounter with him occurred at the Jewish Board of Guardians in New York, where, as a newly appointed junior psychiatric consultant, I was given the privilege of attending his staff training seminars on adolescence. It was there that he developed and refined his ideas about assessment and adolescent development that were elaborated in his now classic book, On Adolescence (1962). There he proposed his (by now canonical) classification of developmental substages, as well as formulating the process of ego development in adolescence and the crucial influence of sociocultural factors on that process.

On Adolescence established Peter as the founding father of the modern understanding of adolescent development and psychopathology. His later work served to deepen and consolidate this position. Drawing on the work of Margaret Mahler, he set forth in 1967 the concept of the ‘second individuation process’, describing the oscillation between progressive and regressive trends that, in his view, were indispensable for normal developmental progression. Still later, in a series of papers and in his book Sons and Fathers (1985) he propounded the view that the consolidation of psychic structure and character development in late adolescence (particularly in the male) is contingent on the resolution of the pre-oedipal dyadic attachment to the same-sex parent and its reactivation during the oedipal period and in early adolescence.

responseSet.title: Response To Kenneth Eisold
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response To Kenneth Eisold

Saul Scheidlinger

Saul Scheidlinger

Saul Scheidlinger
715 Bleeker Avenue
Mamaroneck
NY 10543

Saul Scheidlinger  

Dear Sir

I found Dr Eisold’s response (IJPA, 78: 823) to my letter very disappointing. To begin with, he failed to address my major concern regarding the simplest way one could account for the schism in Freud’s Viennese Society, and by extension, in the psychoanalytic movement, in general. He chose, instead, to ascribe to me a theoretical position of being ‘relatively unaware of complex group processes involving the entire group’. This is ironic, since I have repeatedly written, for over half a century, about ‘the inseparability of individual and of group dynamics in their interpersonal, intrapsychic, and group-as-a-whole dimensions’ (Scheidlinger, 1997, p. 146; ‘Group dynamics and group psychotherapy revisited: four decades later’, Int. J. Group Psychother., 47: 141- 159). In addition, I had even subjected Bion’s ‘group dynamics’ to detailed critical scrutiny (Scheidlinger, ed., 1980, Psychoanalytic Group Dynamics: Basic Readings, Madison, CT: Int. Univ. Press).

There is no support for Eisold’s claim that Bion’s group theories have ‘been verified and extended in countless circumstances’. As noted by Horwitz (1993, ‘Group-centered models of group psychotherapy’ in Comprehensive Group Psychotherapy, Baltimore: Williams & Wilkins, pp. 156-165, ed. H. I. Kaplan and B. J. Sadock), psychodynamically geared group therapists have generally abandoned Bion’s formulations for more eclectic approaches. These theories continue to hold sway, however, with the so-called ‘Tavistock (A. K. Rice) Group Relations Conferences’, which are designed to train human services professionals in group processes (Rioch, 1970, ‘The work of Wilfred Bion on groups’, Psychiat., 33: 56-66).

I continue to believe that our field is not well served when relatively commonplace phenomena such as authoritarian leadership behavior is explained by Bion through Melanie Klein’s intricate ideations such as ‘psychotic’ anxieties or ‘part-object’ fantasies. Interestingly, Bion ceased his interest in groups in his later years.

Freud as leader

Saul Scheidlinger PhD

Saul Scheidlinger

Saul Scheidlinger, PhD
715 Bleeker Avenue
Mamaroneck, NY 10543

Saul Scheidlinger  

To the Editor

As an early student of Freudian group psychology in particular (Scheidlinger, 1952), and of psychoanalytic group dynamics in general (Scheidlinger, 1980), I found Kenneth Eisold’s article, Freud as Leader: The Early Years of the Viennese Society, which appeared in your Journal’s recent issue, of great interest (Int. J. Psychoanal., 78: 87-104).

responseSet.title: Analyst-Patient Interaction, Collected Papers on Technique.: By Michael Fordham. Edited by Sonu Shamdasani. London: Routledge. 1996. Pp. 220.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Analyst-Patient Interaction, Collected Papers on Technique.

By Michael Fordham. Edited by Sonu Shamdasani. London: Routledge. 1996. Pp. 220.

Maria Rosen

Maria Rosen

Maria Rosen
40 Priory Gardens,
London N6 5QS

Maria Rosen  

These collected papers on technique in the practice of psychoanalytic psychotherapy by Michael Fordham are the result of a careful distillation of Jung’s work and of his own.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1997

207

They include a critical evaluation, which Fordham did of Jung’s work while editing his collected papers, reflections on his own experiences in two Jungian analyses, and his experiences and researches in his clinical practice including that with children.

The development of Fordham’s thought brought him more and more to a position of including again what had been eschewed by classical Jungians from psychoanalysis, especially the importance of transference in technique. It brought him, in fact, to a position where he felt very much at home with the work of leading psychoanalysts such as Heimann, Money-Kyrle, Klein, Winnicott and Bion.

Throughout the papers, he argues that the line of the classical school of Jungian analysts, which was to doubt whether technique could be studied, and their tendency therefore to avoid it, was to distrust technique as getting in the way of therapy and to a view that what Jung had not conceptualised was not able to be so. As a result of these attitudes, a great neglect of technical issues then occurred for many years.

Fordham also points out that Jung had at times seemingly contradictory attitudes on the significance of transference in analysis, expressing the view that it was not important at certain stages of life. But yet he did a very extensive study of the transference in his study of the Rosarium Philosophorum.

The selection of papers was made with a view to showing the progression of Fordham’s thought and this is quite comfortably achieved. But it is also acknowledged realistically that a fuller appreciation of the development of his thought would include a study of Fordham’s work on childhood development.

The clarity of the papers is especially to be appreciated since Fordham is often describing complex aspects of technique and the development in technique. He also manages to track the historical roots of whatever topic is under discussion. The papers allow a wide and deep reviewing of technique.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Michael Balint. Object Relations Pure and Applied.

By Harold Stewart. With chapters by Andrew Elder and Robert Gosling. London and New York: Routledge. 1996. Pp. 146.

Ken Wright

Ken Wright

Ken Wright
4 Mill Street, Nayland,
Colchester, Essex C06 4HU

Ken Wright  

This book is about Michael Balint, the man and his work. He was born in 1896, the same year as Donald Winnicott, his contemporary and colleague in the Independent Group of the British Psycho-Analytical Society. 1996 was a centenary year for each of them and, potentially, a time of evaluation.

Both men made important contributions to theory and practice and both were psychoanalysts with professional interests reaching beyond the couch. Winnicott always kept one foot in the world of paediatrics; Balint straddled general medicine, in particular, general practice, but his interest extended beyond this to the applied fields of focal psychotherapy, marital work and psychosexual medicine.

Both men had original and creative minds and succeeded in extending the range of psychoanalysis: outwards, beyond its ivory tower; and inwards, beyond the treatment of classical oedipal disorders. Both were interested in patients with early ego disturbances and both were deeply interested in the problems and potentials of regression that such patients forced upon the analyst's awareness. Both made technical innovations in this area. Both also made important theoretical statements about early development and the mother-in-fant relationship.

Yet in the centenary year that has just passed, Winnicott was celebrated, at least in England, with numerous events, while Balint made little impact on the analytic consciousness. Stewart's book, in this sense, goes counter to the current. Not least because of this, it is a welcome and timely reminder of a creative psychoanalyst, who at the present moment is in danger of being marginalised.

Stewart believes that this should not happen. In this book he sets out the major outlines of Balint's work in a clear, unbiased and eminently accessible way. He tackles this in a historical perspective, dealing with each of his major publications in order. Thus after two chapters offering a biographical sketch and a brief introduction to the major themes of Balint's work, Stewart considers in turn his three main psychoanalytic books: Problems of

responseSet.title: Letter
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter

John Steiner

John Steiner

John Steiner
28 Park Drive
London NW11 7SP

John Steiner  

Dear Sir,

Thank you for showing me the letter from Dr Joseph Schachter, which begins with a couple of comments on my paper. My comment about the usefulness or not of theory was not meant in the way he takes it.

I agree that a theory should be clinically useful. It can help the analyst to think about sessions, to understand what he believes might have been going on, to communicate with colleagues and to serve as a background orientation to help understand the patient. I am suggesting that it is not much use when we are actually in a session where it may in fact function as a defence against understanding and where it is more important to open ourselves to the communication from the patient. Between sessions a good theory can be very helpful and important and if it is clear and well understood it can more easily become a background orientation rather than intrude consciously in the course of a session.

Dr Schachter’s further points seem to me to be an attempt to make analysis impervious to attacks from what he calls ‘widespread destructive criticism’. I fear that such attempts will never satisfy desructive critics and that they impoverish much of what we believe to be most valuable in the psychoanalytic method.

Yours sincerely
John Steiner

583

<autaff><addr><ln><cs style="AuthorName">Ramon Ganzarain</cs></ln><ln>Psychoanalytic Institute</ln><ln>Atlanta Emory University</ln><ln>1861 Clairmont Rd., Apt. 204,</ln><ln>Decatur, Georgia 30033</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Is The Transference Feared By The Psychoanalyst?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Is The Transference Feared By The Psychoanalyst?

Ramon Ganzarain

Ramon Ganzarain

Ramon Ganzarain
Psychoanalytic Institute
Atlanta Emory University
1861 Clairmont Rd., Apt. 204,
Decatur, Georgia 30033

Ramon Ganzarain  

Dear Sir

After reading Grinberg’s paper ‘Is the transference feared by the psychoanalyst?’ (IJPA, 78: 1-14, 1997), I reflected that the difficulties in handling counter-projective identification are much greater than those of transference. In spite of coining and studying that phenomenon for decades even Grinberg may be its prisoner! He could not focus on the patient’s manic denial of guilt for giving up dealing with her ‘untreatable’ father. Grinberg’s intuition guided him instead to his patient’s persecutory anxieties. Her simultaneous use of other primitive defenses (projection and projective identification) was actually producing a confusing mixture of paranoid, depressive and manic phenomena. Grinberg’s current hindsight regarding such an omission may now be of interest to us.

Matte Blanco’s (1975) bilogic may help to formulate more clearly what Grinberg grasped intuitively, but with a confused nonconceptual understanding. A brief overview of Matte Blanco’s theory follows. His formulations will be applied to the clinical situations in which Grinberg made his most decisive interventions.

Matte Blanco used the term ‘symmetrization’ to describe a basic characteristic of the logic of the Unconscious system. He described it as ‘selectively ignoring certain asymmetrical or difference relations’, whereby ‘it can operate as a defense’ (Rayner, 1995, p. 25). However, its function is ‘just to experience sameness’ (p. 25). We need both ‘sensitivity to sameness’ for recognition, and ‘difference discrimination’ for location: a refined combination of both is ‘necessary for survival’ (p. 27). Ryavec (1997) emphasised that Matte Blanco’s clinical theory focuses on similarity and difference. She explored how we see ‘similarity-with-a-difference’ around us all the time, moments in which we recognise elements of a repetitive situation combined with something else, so that the experience feels simultaneously the same and different. The lack of understanding of the primitive (Ucs.) process logic can spark an intense reactive anxiety, which disrupts the capacity to operate in a secondary process manner. When the capacity to see ‘similarity with-a-difference’ is achieved, the person increasingly relies on his/her ability to disentangle bilogic, to decode the symmetrizing and the differentiating strains within it, so that these two modes of logic can stand in a creative bimodal relationship. Confusion or the ‘disruption of the capacity to operate in a secondary process manner’ occurs particularly during violent counter-projective identification.

responseSet.title: Theory of Affects: Affects as a Process. By Joseph M. Jones. Hillsdale, NJ and London: The Analytic Press. 1995. Pp. 268.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Theory of Affects

Affects as a Process. By Joseph M. Jones. Hillsdale, NJ and London: The Analytic Press. 1995. Pp. 268.

Pirooz Sholevar

Pirooz Sholevar

Pirooz Sholevar
222 Righters Mill Road
Narberth, PA 19072

Pirooz Sholevar  

The lack of a satisfactory theory of affects in psychoanalysis and psychology has been widely recognised. The two complementary books, Affects as a Process by Joseph Jones and Affect, Object, and Character Structure by Morton Kissen, make rigorous and informed attempts to propose a remedy to this problem. Jones states that a major reason for the lack of a satisfactory theory of affects in psychoanalysis is that Freud's covert philosophical agenda underlying his metapsychology stripped affects of their process role. He then argues intelligently that we should return to affects the process property that has been taken away, to pave the way for a satisfactory and clinically helpful theory. Affect, Object, and Character Structure examines the subject of affect in the significant contexts of object relations and character structure. Furthermore, the importance of ‘positive affects’ in human interactions and the intolerance of positive affects in neurotic disorders is emphasised. Kissen suggests that psychoanalytic theory has ignored the important function of positive affects in human development and their dysfunction in neurotic symptomatology and character disorders.

Following Lichtenberg, Jones uses the conceptual model of motivational systems with affects in the central position. He endorses four of the five motivational systems proposed by Lichtenberg but challenges the concept of aversive system. Instead of the latter, Jones poses two systems, one organised in infancy to respond to the need for self-protection triggered by fear and one organised to respond to competitive/territorial needs triggered by rage, contempt and shame. The following are the six motivational systems with specific affects that serve as their monitors: (1) regulation of physiological requirements (Freud's self-preservative instinct); (2) attachment/affiliation (the core of object-relations theory and Freud's libido theory); (3) assertiveness (competence/mastery system); (4) aversive reaction through antagonism or withdrawal (Freud's aggression); (5) sensual enjoyment and sexual excitement (Freud's libido theory); (6) competitiveness/ territoriality.

George Makari

George Makari

George J. Makari
Cornell University Medical College
525 East 68th St., Box 171, New York, NY 10021

George Makari  

On a spring afternoon, I approached a Manhattan cross-walk with my 3-year-old daughter in hand. Not a car was in sight. I began strolling across the street when I felt a tug. My daughter pointed to the blinking red sign and said: ‘it says don’t go’. As the father of psychoanalysis, Sigmund Freud tried to formulate rules to teach his students how to make their way effectively and safely across many a mental street.

Response

Mark Solms

Mark Solms

Mark Solms
The Anna Freud Centre
21 Maresfield Gardens
London NW3 5SH

Mark Solms  

Dear Sir

Israëls has in his letter distorted my review in much the same way that he distorted the cocaine episode itself in his book. I will respond only to his five main points, in order to illustrate what I mean. The reader is assured that the same disingenuity characterizes the other points that he makes in his letter.

(1) In his book, Israëls claimed that Eissler suppressed a rancorous letter about Carl Koller that Freud wrote to Meller in 1934. Israëls claimed further that this was done in order to uphold a false impression (created by Bernfeld and Jones) to the effect that Freud had in the mid 1880s reacted in a gentlemanly fashion to Koller's discovery.

responseSet.title: Hypochondria Versus The Relation To The Object
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Hypochondria Versus The Relation To The Object

Jutta Gutwinski

Jutta Gutwinski

Jutta Gutwinski
Neckarhalde 17
D - 720170 T
ü
bingen

Jutta Gutwinski  

In the context of ideas of Freud, Klein, Bion, Rosenfeld and others on hypochondria, psychic development and object relations, the author presents the case history of a female patient who anxiously takes flight from the analyst's interpretations into somatic pains. The experience of separateness and of the ‘facts of life’ is so unbearable to her that she prefers a dialogue with her body to one with the analyst. Cathexis is withdrawn from the frustrating external object and transferred to an organ of the body, which, however, also proves inadequate and is hated, because, as the author shows, the processes of projection and introjection are inescapable. Clinical material is given to demonstrate how the patient uses hypochondriacal states to ward off a psychotic breakdown. Progress ensues when the analyst realises that the patient's hypochondria is a form of communication in body language. In a particularly telling sequence, the patient physically averts her eyes from the unbearable reality of a rose in the consulting room because it stands for the separateness of the analyst. But interpretation gradually ushers in processes of symbolisation, whereby the concrete physical injury felt by the patient to have been inflicted on her by the sight of the rose is turned into a symbolic ‘allergic’ reaction and the allergy becomes metaphorical.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ultimate Intimacy: The Psychodynamics of Jewish Mysticism

Edited by Mortimer Ostow. Madison, CT: International Universities Press. 1995. Pp. xix + 412.

W. Meissner

W. Meissner

W. W. Meissner S. J.
Carney Hall 420 D, Boston College,
Chestnut Hill, MA 02167-3806

W. Meissner  

In the field of interdisciplinary effort represented by the dialogue between psychoanalysis and religious thought, collaborative endeavours engaging distinguished scholars from both sides of the discussion are both welcome and valuable. Mortimer Ostow, Sandrow Visiting Professor Emeritus of Pastoral Psychology at the Jewish Theological Seminary and a practising analyst, well known for his contributions to apocalyptic thinking, has gathered the contributions of a group of distinguished scholars from both psychoanalysis and biblical and talmudic exegesis to examine some of the psychoanalytic implications of Hebrew mysticism. The contributors, in addition to Ostow himself, are scholarly contributors to the study of Hebrew mystical traditions and texts and a complementary group of psychoanalysts who comment on the various essays. The aggregate makes a very meaningful and useful contribution to an aspect of Jewish religious experience that is much neglected by analytic students of religion.

The volume is divided into six sections, three of which are contributed by Ostow. In the opening section, Ostow provides a somewhat sketchy overview of mystical experience, followed by a presentation of his view of the apocalyptic complex. In a subsequent section on merkavah and hekhalot mysticism, he offers an interpretation of the tale, drawn from the Babylonic Talmud, of the four who entered the garden. This is followed by a discussion of the psychodynamics of merkavah mysticism. The first and last of these provide new material; the others have appeared previously. The basic interpretive paradigm centres on the apocalyptic complex that embraces fantasies relevant to themes of death and rebirth and which find their expression in dreams, illusions, beliefs, and in other social phenomena such as messianism, millenianism, and fundamentalism.

Other contributions include David Halperin's lengthy chapter on Ezekiel's vision of the elders of Judah, focusing on chapters 8-11 of Ezekiel.

The Supervisory Encounter: A Guide for Teachers of Psychodynamic Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

By Daniel Jacobs, Paul David and Donald Jay Meyer. New Haven and London: Yale University Press. 1995. Pp. x + 285.

Steven Wein

Steven Wein

Steven J. Wein
125 East 87th St.
New York, NY 10128

Steven Wein  

Noting the relative paucity of literature and formal training in supervision, the authors have written a guide to supervision of psychotherapy and analysis, applied to a wide range of situations. They draw from the experiences of supervision study groups at the Cambridge Hospital (Massachusetts) and at the Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute, Beth Israel Hospital, and the Clinical Psychology Program at Mount Auburn Hospital (Cambridge).

In contrast to previous writing on supervision, they intend this book to cover issues that apply to the most naïve trainee beginners in psychiatry, clinical psychology and social work, in the current atmosphere of rushed, superficial interventions, through the supervision of advanced psychoanalytic candidates and young analysts. In contrast to previous authors, they attempt to focus on the process of supervision, not the content of what is being taught.

[[RunningHead]]

Life, Sex and Death: Selected Writings.

By William H. Gillespie. Edited and introduced by Michael D. A. Sinason with an overview by Charles W. Socarides. London and New York: Routledge. 1995. Pp. 208.

Jacqueline Amati Mehler

Jacqueline Amati Mehler

Jacqueline Amati Mehler
Via Lucrezio Caro 62
00193 Rome

Jacqueline Amati Mehler  

This short volume is divided into two main sections. The first thirty-five pages offer a detailed biographical note on Gillespie's life by Michael Sinason as well as Charles Socarides’ interesting remarks on Gillespie's theories on perversion. The second section (123 pages) contains twelve of Gillespie's papers selected by Sinason, and is divided into two sub-sections. One is entitled ‘The Psychoanalytic Theory of Sexual Development and Sexual Deviation’ and covers some of Gillespie's main contributions on the subject of sexual perversions. The other, entitled ‘The Forces of Life and Death’, renders justice to the attractive title of the book. The final ten pages of the volume consist of a comprehensive chronological list of Gillespie's published and unpublished writings and the References. Gillespie himself shared some of the editorial task and contributed to giving shape to the biographical notes.

I think that the inclusion of the papers on ‘Aggression and instinct theory’, ‘Some regressive phenomena in old age’ and ‘The end of life’ was an excellent editorial choice, because it allows for a retrospective appreciation of some of Gillespie's early affirmations on the role of drives in psychopathology and especially on the role of aggression in perverse organisations. They also enable us to follow the developing process of Gillespie's formulations and the presence in his lucid mind (an ‘anatomist of the psyche’, Socarides calls him) of doubts and queries that have paved the way for further research and clinical validation of normal and pathological psychosexual development. An example of this can be found in his argumentations questioning Freud's claim of the existence of a death instinct, meant as a strong tendency to return to an inorganic state.

This volume devoted to textual variants of Lacan's Écrits opens with an introduction by Elisabeth Roudinesco, who begins by drawing attention to the importance of the author's work in describing Lacan's thirty-year intellectual odyssey and then considers in detail the circumstances of the publication of the Écrits, which were published in November 1966 and contain a selection of papers almost all of which had already appeared in a variety of journals between 1936 and that year.

The title Écrits was chosen by Lacan, who wished to distinguish his written oeuvre from that presented orally at the seminars he had held without interruption since 1951. Although Lacan called it a compilation (Recueil)

Translated by Philip Slotkin MA, MITI.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Black Hamlet.

By Wulf Sachs. Baltimore, MD: Johns Hopkins Press. 1996. Pp. 280.

Susan Phillips

Susan Phillips

Susan Lipshitz Phillips
34 Rotherwick Road
London NW11 7DA

Susan Phillips  

At the time Wulf Sachs wrote this book, in the 1930s, few had attempted to straddle the terrains of psychoanalysis and anthropology with any psychological knowledge in depth of individuals of different cultures. This was in spite of theoretical interest in the generality of the Oedipus complex and unconscious life expressed particularly by Jones and Geza Réheim, but probably was a testament to the inherent methodological difficulties of doing cross-cultural field work. Sachs’s book is thus a rare document, a pioneering study of his relationship with John Chavafambira, an African medicine man. The two, worlds apart, found common ground in interpreting John’s dreams, in understanding the unresolved feelings he had, like Hamlet, about his mother’s remarriage and his loss of place, and in seeing the operation of sexuality and violence in their own minds and those consulting them.

They shared a sense that self-knowledge, training and experience prepared you for practice and that you needed to be ready, through a process of internal and external legitimation by benign paternal and maternal figures (spirits or imagos) to take on the profession. Although John used bones, herbs, and spells to divine and heal in his basically paranoid-schizoid universe that was full of bad luck, poisoners and superstition, Sachs could recognise familiar features.

responseSet.title: Dispatches from the Freud Wars—Psychoanalysis and its Passions.: By John Forrester. Harvard University Press. 1997. Pp. 309.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dispatches from the Freud Wars—Psychoanalysis and its Passions.

By John Forrester. Harvard University Press. 1997. Pp. 309.

Susan Budd

Susan Budd

Susan Budd
30 Laurier Road
London NW5 1SG

Susan Budd  

Any psychoanalyst who has ever attended a dinner party will have realised that ours is not exactly a popular profession. The mixture of awe, hostility and basic ignorance with which many cultural commentators regard Freud's work is something that most psychoanalysts find all too easy to dismiss under the pretext of just wanting to get on with the job. But John Forrester, a historian and philosopher of science at the University of Cambridge, has seen that the reactions to Freud are themselves an interesting commentary on our culture as a whole. His latest book consists of six essays on Freud and his effects, focusing on the various kinds of reactions to and interpretations of him. While by no means an unqualified admirer, he assumes that Freud is a supremely important figure in the twentieth-century's attempt to understand itself.

In the Introduction, Forrester discusses how the vitriolic tone of the debates over Freud are part of the process of coming to terms with the post-Freudian view of moral reflection, the creation of the self, and the importance of sex. What many moralists have—correctly—discerned and disliked in psychoanalysis is not its morals but its attempt to have no morals. (Much British comment on Freud from the elderly-member-of-establishment direction sometimes seems to me to imply that if only he had never existed, sex would never have been invented.) Freud has always been viewed with suspicion, but the brickbats come from different directions in different societies at different times. In the 1970s, it was probably feminists who found him the most dislikeable. There is an oscillation between attacking Freud as a person—dishonest, fraudulent, bullying, sleeping with his sister-in-law, believing his patients, not believing his patients and so on—and attacking large-scale intellectual and clinical movements that are attributed solely to him. But his enemies and friends all assume that they ‘know’ Freud—perhaps we need to unknow him, to try to realise the strangeness of what he was saying.

responseSet.title: The Impact Of Parental Interventions On The Analysis Of A 5-Year-Old Boy: A Clinical Account
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Impact Of Parental Interventions On The Analysis Of A 5-Year-Old Boy

A Clinical Account

Geoffrey Baruch

Geoffrey Baruch

Geoffrey Baruch
Brandon Centre
26 Prince of Wales Road
London NW5 3LG

Geoffrey Baruch  

In this paper, the author describes the pressure put on the analysis of a 5-year-old boy by his parents, who repeatedly tried to terminate the analysis soon after it had started. Detailed clinical material illustrates the impact this pressure had on the analyst and the boy, including a considerable conflict of loyalty between his parents and the analyst and being overwhelmed by intolerable feelings of violence and persecution. The clinical account also shows how this boy struggled to cope with feelings of destructiveness, badness and being unwanted and with intense pressures and demands placed on him by his parents to grow up, to abandon his infantile self and to control his impulses, particularly his anger. Despite the turbulence of the analysis, he was able to make some moves in the direction of tolerating his feelings of guilt and containing his destructiveness. The discussion focuses on the role of parents in child analysis: their concerns and anxieties, the impact they can have on treatment and the interventions available to help them to allow their child's analysis to proceed.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What Do Psychoanalysts Want? The Problem of Aims in Psychoanalytic Therapy.

By Joseph Sandler and Anna Ursula Dreher. London and New York: Routledge. 1996. Pp. 141.

John Steiner

John Steiner

John Steiner
28 Park Drive, London NW11 7SP

John Steiner  

Despite the enigmatic title, this book presents an interesting, useful and detailed account of the changing aims that psychoanalysts have pursued in their work both in theory and practice. The authors wisely pursue a historical approach, having failed to find any coherent contemporary formulation of aims and, dividing the history of psychoanalysis into decades, they describe the way that analysts in different periods were preoccupied with different themes. This provides a convenient structure for the book, and most analysts will value the detailed and thoughtful descriptions, not only of aims but of the trends in psychoanalytic theory and technique that each chapter provides.

Chapter 1 deals with Freud's views and although many will be familiar with the main themes this is one of the most fascinating in the book and shows Freud's impressive stature in relation to those who followed him. He is always interested in first principles and the authors show how his formulation of amis changed as his theory of the nature and structure of the mind developed. Making the unconscious conscious appears as early as The Interpretation of Dreams (1900) and soon the overcoming of resistance (1905) is added as a necessary task for the analyst. It is the confronting of resistance, including the resistance against insight, which has remained both a frustration and a challenge for later generations and this obstacle to development has had a variety of formulations over the years.

Sandler & Dreher point out how often Freud uses metaphors based on a military model when he speaks of resistance, conflict, defences, and fight against recovery, and are concerned with the dangers of adopting a moralistic stand if the analyst sees himself as helping the patient in a kind of war of good against evil. Freud himself avoided such a posture, but he did continue to see intrapsychic conflict as central, ultimately relating this to the operation of the death instinct in its endless struggle with the life instinct.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Logiques Et Archéologiques Du Cadre Psychanalytique.

By René Roussillon. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France. 1995. Pp. 245.

Gilbert Charbonnier

Gilbert Charbonnier

Gilbert Charbonnier
13 rue de Candolle, 1205 Geneva

Gilbert Charbonnier  

René Roussillon is a representative of the important French current of psychoanalytic thought that sets out to revisit, question and rethink Freud's texts and to bring them into the present in truly living form. He has also assimilated the ideas of Winnicott, involving the notions of paradox, play and transitionality, as well as the theories of systems and of change; these appear as constant linking elements in his reflections, which seek to identify the conditions of psychoanalysis, to determine how change occurs and to specify the limiting transference constellations that can unite analyst and analysand in the treatment of borderline or psychotic pathology. A consistent pattern is emerging in Roussillon's works as each new contribution is published and he is now beginning to be acknowledged as one of the leading French-language psychoanalytic authors.

The author begins this book, whose title might be rendered ‘Logics and Archaeologics of the Psychoanalytic Setting’, by summarising his work as a historian of psychoanalysis, which is concerned with the practices and theories of hypnosis and with the foundations of psychoanalysis, and of which a full account is given in his book Du baquet de Mesmer au ‘baquet’ de Freud (Paris: PUF, 1992). He notes that Freud did not arise out of the blue, nor did he represent an epistemological discontinuity in the history of ideas. On the contrary, the practice of psychoanalysis is shown to proceed from a progressive destruction of hypnosis on both the practical and the theoretical levels, one of the consequences of this process being that residues are left behind. This validates the archaeological metaphor used by Freud, who regarded the work of psychoanalysis as an archaeology of the ego, observing that a recent building includes certain raw materials used in the construction of the older edifice upon which it is built.

The author revisits certain texts in order to discover how Freud built up the theory and practice of psychoanalysis while gradually distancing himself from hypnosis. The question of the origins of the symptom, which he ultimately proved unable to resolve—seduction theory versus fantasy theory—entailed the assignment of a new status to the ego, which came to be seen as the author and locus of a conflict. Science demands consistency between theory and practice, so that the theory of the psyche corresponds to that of the setting, and both are organised in accordance with the model of the dream: the therapeutic crisis induced by hypnotists was transformed little by little into a ‘crisis’ that is modelled on dreams and must be perfectly contained in the analytic setting.

Desire and the Female Therapist. Engendered Gazes in Psychotherapy and Art Therapy.

By Joy Schaverien. London and New York: Routledge. 1995. Pp. 233.

Stella Pierides

Stella Pierides

Stella Pierides
15 Victoria Road, London N4 3SH

Stella Pierides  

What do we do when the patient brings paintings to the session? Nothing different from what we usually do in our everyday work; we listen, we observe, we think, we interpret. And yet, we are placed in a situation where particular demands are being made on us, some aspects of which most of us feel unqualified to address. Marion Milner is one of the few

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1997

203

analysts who made use of the pictures her patients painted, as well as her own painting and drawing experiences, to think in a creative and insightful way about the content as well as the function of the images on many levels. Part of Milner’s contribution can be understood as encouraging us to examine the inner life of the artist while it is being translated and transfigured in the most concrete sense through the work that he does, which is not him and is him at the same time. The painter of the picture is externalising on to the painting a major part of his own individual and unique sense of self, including the bodily one. In this, he may not only be recreating internal reality in a reparative sense, although he is doing so as well, but may be creating the power to perceive it by placing it out there, endowing it with an otherness that may then be explored by him as the viewer.

To my mind, Schaverien’s contribution—which, unfortunately, does not make use of Milner’s considerable work in this area—is in thinking about aspects of the meaning of this first externalisation and the affects and effects on the therapist-patient dyad. Drawing on Lacan and Jung, she discusses a number of considerations regarding art in psychotherapy with particular emphasis on the aesthetic countertransference in both, the patient viewing the picture he painted, and the therapist looking at the picture of her patient. Her aim is to lead to ‘an understanding of the aesthetic effects of imagery on the transference and countertransference’. For instance, a mysterious image of a castle ‘seduces’ the viewer who then wants to know more; her interest is aroused: ‘the therapist becomes an interested viewer and this begins to draw her into the client’s inner world’. One needs to be very cautious of this particular way of ‘drawing’ and ‘being drawn’, which may lead to fascination rather than considered work, and which bypasses the question of what happened to the therapist’s interest before the picture was painted. Nevertheless, there is a rich and sensitive illumination of the themes involved that include the gaze that is engendered, reflections, mirrors and narcissism, within the context of the therapeutic couple facing a picture.

responseSet.title: Letter
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter

André Green

André Green

André Green
9 avenue de l
’
Observatoire
75006 Paris

André Green  

Dear Sir,

I would like to thank Dr Sherwood Brown for his appreciation of my paper and for informing me of the existence of the ‘body shots’. If course, the symbolic significance of the navel is quite well known in psychoanalysis. The displacement from the vagina is clear enough but suggests a condensation between this organ, the mouth and the anus. Anyhow, it is also the consequence of the equivalent of a castration anxiety as it is the result of the cutting of the chord uniting baby and mother. Marie Delcourt has written extensively on the ‘navel of the world’ (L’oracle de Delphes. Paris: Éditions Payot, 1955), which was supposed to be located in the temple of Apollo in Delphi, whose sulphuric emanations were supposed to inspire the Pythia. This mythological complement to the psychoanalytic view is indispensable for the understanding of its meaning, with its obviously incestuous implications.

Yours sincerely
André Green

578

responseSet.title: ‘Interaction And Transference’ By Maria Ponsi
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Interaction And Transference’ By Maria Ponsi

Paul Williams

Paul Williams

Paul Williams
Flat 1
15 Rosslyn Hill
London NW3 5UJ

Paul Williams  

Maria Ponsi's paper ‘Interaction and transference’ is the fourth paper to be selected from the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis for pre-publication and e-mail discussion via the IJPA web site (http://www.ijpa.org). As is now customary, the paper was made available for downloading free of charge several weeks prior to its publication in the Journal, and an extensive discussion ensued.

Ponsi began her paper by marking the shift in emphasis from the classical psychoanalytic position of viewing the present as a means of discerning the past (via symptoms and the repetition compulsion) to a fundamentally different view of the past and thus of the function of transference; the past is today widely considered to be a vehicle for illuminating the present. An emphasis on the analysis of here-and-now states, including the changing mental state of the analyst, also constitutes a significant revision of the roles of patient and analyst and has drawn attention to the kinds of interaction that these roles generate. Ponsi suggested that many reconstructive statements in contemporary psychoanalysis across all schools revealed a concern with illuminating the nature of immediate experience. She cited the ‘hermeneutic’ and Kleinian models as examples: in the former, mutual construction of a meaningful narrative tends to subsume prior, historical versions, abolishing the distinction between here-and-now and there-and-then. In much of Kleinian thinking interest in the past is ‘ancestral’ as opposed to historical, extending to the earliest developmental phases, which are inscribed in and constructed by deep, primitive states in the present. A concomitant of transferential interest in unconscious fantasies in the present is an interest in countertransference movements, to the extent that taken together they now form common ground for many schools, expressed increasingly through the study of interactive movements.

The concept of ‘interaction’ is recent (it was never used by Freud), indicating a departure from purely verbal exchanges, and yet one which is also contained within the ‘pragmatic’ elements of language through which intersubjective influences are derived and exerted. Ponsi cited a number of the authors who have struggled with the controversial concept of interaction and discussed their divergent opinions of its value. The key suspicions of its critics (e.g. Schwaber, 1995; Green, 1993) centre on the adoption of the external referent or viewpoint that is required to address interactions. The psychoanalytic concern with inner (intrapsychic) life is jeopardised, they argue, and risks replacement ultimately by an interpersonalistic theory.

responseSet.title: Dimensions Of Mentalisation: Outlining Levels Of Psychic Transformation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dimensions Of Mentalisation: Outlining Levels Of Psychic Transformation

Serge Lecours and Marc-André Bouchard

Marc-André Bouchard

Serge Lecours
Marc-André Bouchard
42 rue Courcelette
Outremont
Quebec
Canada

Marc-André Bouchard  

The authors of this paper use the term ‘mentalisation’ as a supra-ordinate concept that encompasses processes of representation, symbolisation and abstraction. Mentalisation is defined as a preconscious or ego function that transforms basic somatic sensations and motor patterns through a linking activity (Freud's notion of Bindung). This binding proceeds from initial associations of somatic/motoric substrata with mental representations, on to the multiplication and organisation of these representations, thus allowing the emergence of mental contents and structures of higher levels of complexity (symbolism and abstraction). Inspired by the contributions of Marty, Luquet and Bion, the authors propose a conceptual model of formal levels of mental elaboration. Mentalisation is defined as consisting of two theoretically separate and independent dimensions. Each presents levels of a hypothesised and gradually increasing mental elaboration. The first dimension considers different channels of drive-affect expression: somatic and motor activity, imagery and verbalisation. The second specifies five descriptive levels of affect tolerance and abstraction: disruptive impulsion (acting out), modulated impulsion (catharsis), externalisation, appropriation and abstract-reflexive meaning association. A clinical case illustrates how the two dimensions may influence listening and interpreting. Some metapsychological aspects of the process of mentalisation are discussed.

responseSet.title: Reverie And Metaphor: Some Thoughts On How I Work As A Psychoanalyst
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reverie And Metaphor

Some Thoughts On How I Work As A Psychoanalyst

Thomas Ogden

Thomas Ogden

Thomas Ogden
306 Laurel Street
San Francisco, CA 94118

Thomas Ogden  

In this paper, the author presents parts of an ongoing internal dialogue concerning how he works as an analyst. He describes the way in which he attempts to sense what is most alive and most real in each analytic encounter, as well as his use of his own reveries in his effort to locate himself in what is going on at an unconscious level in the analytic relationship. The author views each analytic situation as reflecting, to a large degree, a specific type of unconscious intersubjective construction. Since unconscious experience is by definition outside of conscious awareness, the analyst must make use of indirect (associational) methods such as the scrutiny of his own reverie experience in his efforts to ‘catch the drift’ (Freud, 1923, p. 239) of the unconscious intersubjective constructions being generated. Reveries (and all other derivatives of the unconscious) are viewed not as glimpses into the unconscious, but as metaphorical expressions of what the unconscious experience is like. In the author's experience, when an analysis is ‘a going concern’, the analytic dialogue often takes the form of a verbal ‘squiggle game’ (Winnicott, 1971a, p. 3) in which the analytic pair elaborates and modifies the metaphors that the other has unself-consciously introduced. The analytic use of reverie and of the role of metaphor in the analytic experience is clinically illustrated.

responseSet.title: Disclosures And Refutations: Clinical Psychoanalysis As A Logic Of Enquiry
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Disclosures And Refutations: Clinical Psychoanalysis As A Logic Of Enquiry

Jorge Ahumada

Jorge Ahumada

Jorge L. Ahumada
Ave. Las Heras 3898 3
°
H
1425 Buenos Aires
jahumada@impsat1.com.ar

Jorge Ahumada  

The author argues that the empirical status of psychoanalysis has been distorted by the controversy surrounding Aristotelian and Galilean-Newtonian schemes of science, whose core ideas, respectively, are scientific concepts or forms and scientific laws. Bound by the Euclidian axiomatic tradition, Newtonian-style ‘laws’ do not obtain in the social sciences, and in biology they apply only at near-molecular levels, not at the level of mind. Continuing the traditional Aristotelian reliance on ‘exemplars’, neither Freud's nor Darwin's work fits the deductivist, infallibilistic scientific criteria used by philosophers such as Tarski, Popper or Grünbaum. Rebutting the charge that the workings of psychoanalysis are not explicit enough, this paper unfolds the logic of enquiry it utilises. In contrast to Newtonian inductivism, Popper's method of conjectures and refutations and Lakatos's method of proofs and refutations, clinical psychoanalysis employs a ‘practical logic’ of disclosures and refutations, a constantly evolving enquiry of multi-layered and tentative evidence of discordance and analogy. Such epistemic fallibilism, relying on a build-up of ostensive evidences rather than on ‘certainties’, is illustrated in the text by Moore's famous ‘two-hands’ argument, is held to fit in with the workings of everyday discernment and, arguably, with Darwinian evolution.

responseSet.title: The Historical Roots Of Psychoanalytic Orthodoxy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Historical Roots Of Psychoanalytic Orthodoxy

Martin Bergmann

Martin Bergmann

Martin S. Bergmann
1136 Fifth Avenue
New York
NY 10128

Martin Bergmann  

The historical roots of psychoanalytic orthodoxy may now be examined because new hitherto unpublished sources on the history of psychoanalysis have become available. The early defections of Adler and Jung can be seen in a different light from that in Freud's (1914) history of psychoanalysis. The controversial discussions between Anna Freud and Melanie Klein can also yield new insights. In the author's view, the fact that the Kleinians were not expelled gave rise to a new type of creative analyst, that of the modifier. Modifiers complicate the task of psychoanalytic training, but they also keep psychoanalysis alive. Criticisms of psychoanalytic training are cited and the claim is made that psychoanalytic orthodoxy may have been necessary historically speaking, but it extracted a heavy toll. It no longer serves a useful purpose. A new historical approach is recommended. The author believes that understanding the history of psychoanalysis can contribute to tolerance between different psychoanalytic schools.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Apprehension Of Psychic Reality: Extensions Of Bion's Theory Of Alpha-Function

Paulo Sandler

Paulo Sandler

Paulo Cesar Sandler
Avenida Macuco 49 ap. 41
04523-000 S
ã
o Paulo SP

Paulo Sandler  

The author proposes the need for a term which describes a universal primitive tendency of the human mind to concretise non-sensual mental phenomena. Drawing on Bion's theory of thinking, he suggests that there is an active process whereby a-elements are transformed into b-elements, which can then masquerade as intelligible sense data. This process is identified and illustrated through a series of clinical vignettes in which the analyst was tempted to relate to the patient's material as if it were truly describing concrete external reality rather than disguising immaterial psychic reality. He introduces the term anti alpha-function, to name the process, linked with projective identification, operating in the mind of both patient and analyst, by which painful depressive-position experience may be evaded. He suggests this kind of mental functioning is far more widespread in society than is immediately apparent, accounting for serious distortions in our collective relationship to reality.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Experience With Psychoanalytic Post-Termination Meetings

Joseph Schachter , Gary Martin , Michael Gundle and Mary O’Neil

Mary O’Neil

Joseph Schachter
Gary C. Martin
Michael J. Gundle
Mary Kay O’Neil
401 Shady Avenue
Pittsburgh, PA 15206

Mary O’Neil  

The authors describe post-termination meetings in order to examine the controversial issue of the proper relationship between analyst and patient at this stage. In the first case the meetings facilitated the patient's re-entering treatment, leading to significant further growth. In the second and third cases, the meetings re-ignited mourning for the analyst and furthered analytic gains. The authors’ overall impression was that the post-termination contacts were helpful for all three patients. Their limited data contradicted the long-standing assumption that all post-termination contact, unless specified as a course of further treatment, is harmful to the patient. The authors suggest that such meetings stabilise analytic gains, provide a joint assessment of analytic outcome from a useful later perspective and provide an opportunity for further analytic work. The consideration of any post-termination changes in the patient allows for a more inclusive evaluation of the analysis than was possible earlier. While acknowledging the need to consider such meetings thoughtfully in order to avoid any harmful enactment of wishes by the patient or the analyst, the authors encourage analysts to explore and report such meetings to replace unexamined assumptions and develop a body of clinical data about the effects of post-termination contacts.

Gerard</arttitle><artqual rx="IJP.076.0019A"/><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Britton, Ronald" listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Ronald</nfirst><nlast>Britton</nlast></aut>

responseSet.title: Psychic Reality And Unconscious Belief: A Reply To Harold B. Gerard
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychic Reality And Unconscious Belief: A Reply To Harold B. Gerard

Ronald Britton

Ronald Britton

Ronald Britton
24 Hillfield Road
London NW6 1PZ

Ronald Britton  

‘realism and idealism both go too far’—(Henri Bergson)

It is flattering to find my paper the focus of a discussion on the conflicting epistemologies of Descartes and Spinoza by Harold Gerard. It is more flattering to be joined with Freud, even though it is in the dock, where we are both on a charge of intellectual naïveté. However I must put aside any gratification this might offer in order to protest that my paper has been misunderstood and misrepresented. I could say the same for Freud but I leave his defence to his own subtle and profound writings on the individual's relationship to external and psychic reality.

I say in response to Harold Gerard's paper that he is not disagreeing with my views about the relationships between phantasy, belief and knowledge but that he misunderstands them. He seems to agree with me that believing is anterior to reality testing and can exist in the absence of evidence. Furthermore, he appears to agree with my argument in the paper that a major affective and cognitive struggle in assimilating new knowledge is the relinquishment of existing but discredited beliefs. However, nothing could be further from what I think than his statement that ‘Descartes argued, as Britton does and Freud did, that phantasies or ideas are not accepted as beliefs until they check out against reality’. My contention was that belief is a status awarded by the ego to phantasies or ideas which gives to them the force of reality whether or not they correspond to external reality; in other words it gives to them psychic reality. The ego has, however, a further task, which is testing the external reality of its beliefs about the world. The word external is used here both to mean the world external to the person and also as external within the mind of the individual to the particular belief. In other words, it refers not only to the process of checking by correspondence with the outside world through the evidence of the senses but also to a process of checking from within the mind by such evidence as memory, logic, and known facts—a sort of internal reality testing. This process of internal reality testing is of particular interest to analysts as we see it prevented by such processes as splitting or distorted by a variety of other psychic mechanisms.

responseSet.title: Distortions Of Time In The Transference:: Some Clinical And Theoretical Implications
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Distortions Of Time In The Transference:

Some Clinical And Theoretical Implications

James Rose

James Rose

James Rose
25 Drylands Road
London N8 9HN

James Rose  

The author considers that patients’ distortions of time can frequently and readily be observed in clinical psychoanalysis, reflecting both their psychopathology and their reactions to the temporal aspects of the psychoanalytic setting. These phenomena are considered in order to examine the assumptions that can implicitly be made about the nature of space and time in object-relations theory. Two case histories are given to exemplify these clinical phenomena—the first being an example of a fixation and the second one of a psychic retreat. These cases are compared to demonstrate the unconscious processes underlying the particular time distortions being considered, their impact on the patient's lives and their manifestation in the clinical setting. On the basis of these studies, it is suggested that the asymmetry of the ‘arrow of time’ cannot be assumed in the structure of psychic reality. The clinical evidence suggests that psychic reality has to be seen as discontinuous and that the structure of the discontinuities is revealed by the impact that the temporal aspects of the psychoanalytic setting have on the patient.

<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Enrico E. Jones</cs></ln><ln>Psychology Clinic</ln><ln>2205 Tolman Hall, 1650</ln><ln>University of California at Berkeley</ln><ln>Berkeley, CA 94720</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Modes Of Therapeutic Action
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Modes Of Therapeutic Action

Enrico Jones

Enrico Jones

Enrico E. Jones
Psychology Clinic
2205 Tolman Hall, 1650
University of California at Berkeley
Berkeley, CA 94720

Enrico Jones  

The dialectic in psychoanalysis between theories about the mutative effects of interpretation and psychological knowledge and those concerning the effects of interpersonal interaction constitutes an important tension for approaches to psychoanalytic technique. This essay briefly summarises the thinking around these alternative conceptualisations of therapeutic action, and introduces a new empirically derived model, that of ‘repetitive interaction structure’, which attempts to bridge therapeutic action by insight and by relationship. Interaction structure is a way of formulating those aspects of the analytic process that have come to be termed intersubjectivity, transference-countertransference enactments and role responsiveness. The concept operationalises important aspects of interpersonal interaction, and can help specify the two-person patterns that emerge in an analysis. Patient and analyst interact in repetitive ways; these patterns of interaction, which are slow to change, probably reflect the psychological structure of both patient and analyst, whether psychic structure is conceptualised in terms of object-representations or compromise formations and impulse-defence configurations. Therapeutic action is located in the experience, recognition and understanding by patient and analyst of these repetitive interactions. Interaction structures stress the importance of the intrapsychic as a basis for what becomes manifest in the interactive field. Clinical illustrations from a psychoanalysis are provided, and research on repetitive interaction structures is described.

responseSet.title: The Theory Of Seduction And The Problem Of The Other
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Theory Of Seduction And The Problem Of The Other

Jean Laplanche

Jean Laplanche

Jean Laplanche
55 rue de Varenne
F-75007 Paris

Jean Laplanche  

The author offers a survey of his general theory of seduction, elaborated in order to give an account of the origin of the psychic apparatus and the drives, starting from the adult-infant relation. This theory supposes that, in the sexual domain, such a relation is asymmetrical, the sexual message originating in the adult other. The author develops here the consequences of such an originary primacy of the other, especially for the notion of sublimation and the process of the analytic treatment. The analytic situation and method, as invented by Freud, implies a radical change of perspective in the philosophical and anthropological conception of the human being, in forcing us to move from a self-centred, ‘Ptolemaic’ vision, belonging to the old philosophy of the subject, to an othercentred, ‘Copernican’ vision.

responseSet.title: A Mind Of One's Own
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Mind Of One's Own

Robert Caper

Robert Caper

Robert Caper
360 North Bedford Drive
Beverly Hills, California 90210

Robert Caper  

The author argues that in the transference the patient unconsciously identifies the analyst with what he has projected into him, creating what Strachey called an external phantasy object. In the intimate rapport of the analytic session, the analyst tends to identify with these projections as well, which brings him into a relationship with the patient that complements the patient's narcissistic object relationship with him. The analyst's identification with the patient's projections is a type of pathology of his receptivity to the patient that is exacerbated by the patient's skill at producing states of mind in the analyst with which the analyst is supposed to identify. The analyst's ability to distance himself from the patient's projections and therefore to interpret them depends on his having links to his internal objects that survive the patient's projections and unconscious manipulations. Interpretations make the patient aware simultaneously that he and the analyst are separate and that the analyst has a link to internal objects that are not under the patient's control. The latter is a rudimentary oedipal situation. The author argues that these two ‘awarenesses’ are really the same, and that working through the depressive position and working through the oedipal situation are therefore fundamentally the same thing.

responseSet.title: The Role Of Working Through In Bringing About Psychoanalytic Change
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Role Of Working Through In Bringing About Psychoanalytic Change

J. Burland

J. Burland

J. Alexis Burland
15 Colwyn Lane
Bala-Cynwyd, PA 19004

J. Burland  

In reviewing psychoanalytic case summaries while serving on the Committee on Certification of the American Psychoanalytic Association, the author gradually became aware that there was a recognisable similarity in what the analysands experienced in spite of variations among psychoanalysts as to how they conducted the analyses. Based on this observation, the author turned to his own clinical practice where more detailed process data was available, and focused his attention in particular on what the analysand does during a psychoanalysis. A detailed explication is offered of his findings, with a focus on the nature of ‘working through’. A clinical vignette is given to highlight that what the analysand experiences is an essential ingredient in attempting to understand how structural change occurs in psychoanalysis.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Intuition Of The Negative In Playing And Reality

André Green

André Green

André Green
9 avenue de l
’
Observatoire
75006 Paris
andregreen@compuserve.com

André Green  

The author draws some parallels between his own conceptions on the work of the negative with some ideas presented by Winnicott in the ultimate version of his paper on ‘Transitional objects and transitional phenomena’. He underlines the germs of the intuition of the negative in Winnicott's general theory (‘not me possession’, transitional object as not being the breast, the decathexis of the representation of the object). He then goes on to examine in detail the clinical material of a patient, in the last part of the paper, which has been published posthumously. In this last part the negative is mentioned explicitly and the idea is openly presented. We have here a concept of which Winnicott had the intuition without having the possibility of considering its full development and implications. It so happened that the patient whose clinical material is published in ‘Playing and Reality’ came to the author to continue her analytic experience. One can compare the material presented in these two analytical relationships. The author comments about his relationship with the patient and of the way she meets his own hypotheses.

responseSet.title: The Elusive Concept Of ‘Internal Objects’ (1934-1943) Its Role In The Formation Of The Klein Group
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Elusive Concept Of ‘Internal Objects’ (1934-1943) Its Role In The Formation Of The Klein Group

R. Hinshelwood

R. Hinshelwood

R. D. Hinshelwood
18 Artesian Road
London W2 5AR

R. Hinshelwood  

The author traces a debate about the concept of ‘internal objects’ that took place between 1937 and 1943 at a time when a group of British analysts was forming around Melanie Klein. The debate is set within a complex of personal, group and organisational dynamics, which the paper makes a start on unravelling. The history of the British Psycho-Analytical Society at this time exemplifies Bion's notion of group schism. The events in the Society's history demonstrate defensive aspects of the interaction between the opposed groups, which support members against various anxieties. These include the stress of the work of analysis, but also in this instance the particular anxieties deriving from the collapse of psychoanalysis in Europe, the state of war of the country as a whole, and the death of Freud shortly after he came to London. This psychoanalytic anxiety/defence model clarifies some aspects of the debate about internal objects, and demonstrates the way in which these various anxieties and defences become organised around a scientific debate in a scientific society.

responseSet.title: Intimidation At The Helm: Superego And Hallucinations In The Analytic Treatment Of A Psychosis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Intimidation At The Helm: Superego And Hallucinations In The Analytic Treatment Of A Psychosis

Franco De Masi

Franco De Masi

Franco De Masi
Via Ramazzini 7
20129 Milan

Franco De Masi  

On the basis of the development in the course of analytic treatment of a young male patient in a state of hallucinatory terror, the author discusses in this paper the nature of the psychotic superego. He shows how the process of recomposition of the self after a psychotic breakdown involves the transition from a terrorising and destructuring type of superego to one more reminiscent of that seen in depressive illness. The author describes this process against the background of the variations in the auditory hallucinations that began during the analysis. In the first phase of the analysis the patient is helped to free himself from the intimidating power of the hallucinatory superego, while the second phase centres on the patient's own involvement in producing the hallucinations. The author shows how the analysis mitigated the destructive hate resulting from unbearable psychic pain and describes how insight and transformation gradually ensued in the hallucinatory state. The resulting restoration of an internal psychic space is stated to be essential to the reconstitution of a whole and separate self After drawing an interesting parallel between the psychotic superego and the attitude of God in the Old Testament story of Job, the author places his thesis in the context of the ideas of Klein, Bion and Rosenfeld.

responseSet.title: Projective Identification: The Analyst's Involvement
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Projective Identification: The Analyst's Involvement

Michael Feldman

Michael Feldman

Michael Feldman
5 Cholmeley Crescent
London N6 5EZ

Michael Feldman  

The author emphasises that what is projected into the analyst is a phantasy of an object relationship that evokes not only thoughts and feelings, but also propensities towards action. From the patient's point of view, the projections represent an attempt to reduce the discrepancy between the phantasy of some archaic object relationship and what the patient experiences in the analytical situation. For the analyst, too, there are impulses to function in ways that lead to a greater correspondence with some needed or desired phantasies. The interaction between the patient's and the analyst's needs may lead to the repetitive enactment of the painful and disturbing kind that is described. It may be very difficult for the analyst to extricate himself (or his patient) from this unproductive situation and recover his capacity for reflective thought, at least for a while. The difficulty is compounded when the projection into the analyst leads to subtle or overt enactments that do not initially disturb the analyst but, on the contrary, constitute a comfortable collusive arrangement, in which the analyst feels his role is congruent with some internal phantasy. It may be difficult to recognise the defensive function this interaction serves for both the patient and the analyst and the more disturbing phantasies it defends against.

responseSet.title: Opening Remarks To A Discussion Of Sexuality In Contemporary Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Opening Remarks To A Discussion Of Sexuality In Contemporary Psychoanalysis

André Green

André Green

André Green
9, avenue de l
’
Observatoire
75006 Paris

André Green  

Reproduction and pleasure

More than ever, sexuality is defined by the separation between the reproductive function assigned to it by evolution and the human pursuit of pleasure in its practice, or indeed by the mutual independence of these two aspects. However, procreation may also entail pleasure, or alternatively unpleasure, whether or not shared. These two points of view set the distinction between biological sexuality and psychosexuality, which constitutes a fundamental duality. The discovery of infantile sexuality has had two consequences: firstly, the observation of erotic manifestations long before puberty and in fact practically from birth (erections in babies) and, secondly, the classification of oral, anal and other activities under the heading of sexuality. After Freud, other authors described further erotogenic zones, including, for instance, the look and the voice (Lacan) and the skin-ego (Anzieu).

The existence of pleasure being recognised from earliest infancy, sexuality may be said to be the ‘pleasure of pleasures’ in the same sense as the prohibition on incest has been deemed the ‘rule of rules’. The link between sexuality and pleasure is surely the foundation of the sexual in psychoanalysis. However, in view of the solidarity of pleasure and unpleasure, we are compelled to see these two entities as a pair of opposites neither of whose terms can be contemplated without the other. Yet the gamut of unpleasure has widened greatly since Freud, with later authors emphasising early developmental manifestations (Klein: psychic pain and threat of annihilation; Winnicott: agonies and fear of breakdown; Bion: dread and catastrophe and so on).

From the pleasure principle to that which lies beyond it

At the heart of the entire range of manifestations of sexuality is the pleasure-unpleasure principle. On the one hand, the reality principle appears as a modified pleasure

This paper will be presented at the panel on ‘Review of the Psychoanalytic Theory of Sexuality’, 28 July 1997.

responseSet.title: Imaginative Reality Observed During Early Language Development
responseSet.authorMast: Robert Emde,  Lorraine Kubicek and  David Oppenheim
responseSet.authorCitation: Emde, R., Kubicek, L. & Oppenheim, D.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Imaginative Reality Observed During Early Language Development

Robert Emde , Lorraine Kubicek and David Oppenheim

David Oppenheim

Robert Emde
Lorraine Kubicek
David Oppenheim
Program for Early Developmental Studies
University of Colorado Health Sciences Center
4200 East 9th Avenue, C268-69
Denver, CO 80262

David Oppenheim  

The authors argue that our thinking about psychic reality is challenged by research observations of the child during the period of early language development. The toddler, at the beginnings of propositional speech, expresses the capacity for ‘two kinds of psychic reality’. A world of imaginative pretence occurs quite early, and supplements the child's everyday experience. The toddler is not confused by these two experiential worlds and, correspondingly, the use of imaginative activity is strongly supported by caregivers. While in some ways there is a rich history of developmental observation and psychoanalytic thinking about such early imaginative activity, its significance is only now being appreciated. Imaginative reality refers to a process in which the child makes use of what is familiar in the remembered past in order to try out a world of new possibilities in the present that, to some extent, are oriented towards the future. Conditional modes of thinking that are affectively meaningful are thereby engaged and practised in play. The authors present vivid observations that illustrate the early imaginative pretence mode of psychic reality. The observations are followed by a developmental discussion integrating recent research with psychoanalytic theory. As would be expected, thinking about early imaginative reality leads to a variety of new questions for research and clinical work.

responseSet.title: Transitions In Psychic Structures In The Light Of Deterministic Chaos Theory
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transitions In Psychic Structures In The Light Of Deterministic Chaos Theory

Jean-Michel Quinodoz

Jean-Michel Quinodoz

Jean-Michel Quinodoz
53 A, chemin des Fourches
1223 Cologny/Geneva

Jean-Michel Quinodoz  

The author begins by discussing the recent development of deterministic chaos theory to account for physical phenomena of seeming disorder formerly regarded as random, especially in the field of complex, dynamical systems. With reference to both the scientific and the psychoanalytic literature, he seeks in this paper to draw parallels between this new information and certain aspects of psychic functioning as observed in psychoanalysis. Expanding previous authors’ analogies and presenting some original ones of his own, he bases his argument on the role of strange attractors in the ‘chaotic’ functioning of complex systems and on the concepts of state-to-state transitions, the tuning variable and period-doubling cascades. Unconscious fantasies are shown to be a form of strange attractor and to be fractal in nature, as illustrated by a clinical example. The author goes on to examine the transitions of psychic structures between states and discusses how the transference/countertransference relationship and the early infantile relationship may be compared to a tuning variable in determining such transitions. In terms of methodology, he uses the concepts of the analogy-based model and of anaclisis in a widened sense to forge links between the physicomathematical field of deterministic chaos theory and the psychic field proper to psychoanalysis.
