responseSet.title: Freud-Ferenczi: Controversy Terminable And Interminable
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud-Ferenczi: Controversy Terminable And Interminable

Luis Martín

Luis Martín

Luis J. Martín
Joaqu
í
n Bau 7
28036 Madrid

Luis Martín  

Following Balint's view that the psychoanalytic community was traumatised by the Freud-Ferenczi controversy, the author considers whether the roots of the confrontation between drive-based and object-relations theories might lie in a failure to examine this disagreement in sufficient depth. Increasing interest, as reflected in the literature, is now focusing on Ferenczi's ideas after many years of neglect due in part to Jones's ‘official’ account of the ‘mental deterioration' of his last years. Adducing late works by both Freud and Ferenczi, the author shows that the latter amplified Freud's ideas rather than breaking with them. To Freud, who had always been concerned to stress the role of the drives in psychopathology, Ferenczi's emphasis on external trauma smacked of his own, rejected, early seduction theory, but trauma does feature prominently in Freud's discussion of interminable analyses, as does Ferenczi himself. In the author's view, a careful reading of the two men's late works reveals a conception of trauma as lying in an intersubjective space as a consequence of the meeting of drive and object. Present-day conceptions of transgenerational trauma are shown to be already present in Ferenczi. The problems in the relationship between Freud and Ferenczi and their theoretical disagreements are also considered in the light of the latter's inadequate analysis with Freud.

<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Glen O. Gabbard</cs></ln><ln>The Menninger Clinic</ln><ln>P. O. Box 829</ln><ln>Topeka, Kansas 66601-0829</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: A Reconsideration Of Objectivity In The Analyst
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Reconsideration Of Objectivity In The Analyst

Glen Gabbard

Glen Gabbard

Glen O. Gabbard
The Menninger Clinic
P. O. Box 829
Topeka, Kansas 66601-0829

Glen Gabbard  

The notion of objectivity has fallen out of favour to some extent, with an increasing emphasis on post-modern trends such as social constructivism, intersubjectivity and perspectivism. In this paper it is argued that the notion of objectivity is still useful in the psychoanalytic domain and that psychoanalysis can and should have a healthy dialectical tension between modern and post-modern themes. The origin of objectivity in the term object is often overlooked. The analyst's position as an object, external to the thinking mind or subjectivity of the patient, offers a vantage-point from which the analyst can share observations that are different from the patient's by virtue of being external to the patient. Indeed, the patient's unconscious intentions are often most accessible through the analyst's careful scrutiny of his or her ‘object’ responses. Even though the analyst cannot transcend the intersubjectivity of the analytic couple, part of that intersubjectivity involves a perspective outside that of the patient.

responseSet.title: Towards A Clinically And Empirically Sound Theory Of Motivation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Towards A Clinically And Empirically Sound Theory Of Motivation

Drew Westen

Drew Westen

Drew Westen
The Cambridge Hospital and
Harvard Medical School
1493 Cambridge St.
Cambridge, MA 02139

Drew Westen  

The author outlines a theory of motivation that attempts to integrate psychoanalytic theory with current psychological thinking and research. Emotions and other sensory feeling states are evolved mechanisms for channelling behaviour in directions that foster adaptation. The avoidance of unpleasant states and pursuit of pleasant ones leads to goal-directed mental and behavioural processes, including defences and compromise formations. Affects provide a flexible motivational mechanism in humans, as they become associated with representations of perceived, feared, wished-for, or otherwise valued states through the interaction of environmental events and highly specific naturally-selected biological proclivities. This reconceptualisation of motivation points towards a resolution of a contradiction in Freud's models of affect and motivation between a theory of drive-reduction and a theory of affect regulation, and of the apparent contradiction between motivational models that emphasise either sexual desire or relational needs. The model also has implications for the theory of transference, since it suggests that neutrality is not the feature of the analytic situation that evokes meaningful transferential processes.

responseSet.title: Personal Experiences—Professional Interests: Joan Riviere And Femininity
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Personal Experiences—Professional Interests: Joan Riviere And Femininity

M. Hughes

M. Hughes

M. Athol Hughes
3 Antrim Grove
London NW3 4XP

M. Hughes  

The author proposes that the subject chosen for psychoanalytical investigation bears a relationship to autobiographical experiences. Aspects of the life of the British psychoanalyst, Joan Riviere (1883-1962), are examined and related to her investigation into a form of pathological femininity presented in her paper ‘Womanliness as a masquerade’. Information about her formative years is gained from a diary kept by her mother that gives details of Joan Riviere's early months and years as well as further information about her as a young adult. Riviere's own diary, which lists events and activities in which she took part throughout more than a decade before she became a psychoanalyst, gives valuable insights into her life at that time. The author examines the descriptions of the patient in Riviere's paper and relates it to many facets of the latter's life. The defensive femininity shown by her patient is characterised by appeasement and denial of intellectual and professional attributes to escape detection and the retribution feared as a consequence of having, in phantasy, stolen potency from men. It is suggested that Riviere's own life experiences, particularly in relation to men, are represented in her paper.

responseSet.title: An Emerging Culture For Psychoanalytic Research?
responseSet.authorMast: Robert N. Emde and  Peter Fonagy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Emerging Culture For Psychoanalytic Research?

Robert Emde and Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy

Robert N. Emde
Peter Fonagy
Robert N. Emde
University of Colorado Medical Center
4200 East Ninth Avenue
Denver, CO 80262

Peter Fonagy  

Psychoanalysis has had a difficult time promoting research. As a profession, we are deeply committed to concerns that involve individuality, struggles with conflict, and constructing psychological meaning in the midst of the surrounding culture and life context. Moreover, the calls for attention to such concerns are likely to increase as the public becomes aware of the complexities of our increasing knowledge of genetics, the neurosciences and development. There will be demands to know more about the implications of such knowledge in terms of an individual's experience and in terms of the potential for improvement in particular environmental transactions. There will also be demands to know more about the usefulness of various psychological therapies as new forms of disorder and risk are specified. In furthering research that responds to these demands in ways that extend our humanistic and therapeutic interests psychoanalysts have much to offer. But our history and our organisational structures have placed the field of psychoanalysis in a paradoxical position.

Although it began by generating an extraordinarily innovative body of knowledge, psychoanalysis grew in isolation from universities and focused on one method of enquiry, namely, the psychoanalytic situation. Moreover, psychoanalytic training and continuing education has focused until now on the art of clinical practice and neglected empirical research. All too often we teach that enquiry is confined to our one method, and that the evaluation of theory is confined to judgements of narrative coherence. Correspondingly, the culture we often reflect in our training is one that values confirmation and closure rather than exploration and systematic investigation of what we do not know. This leads to a paradox because psychoanalysts are among the most curious and investigative of professionals, and because research, by its nature, is ‘multi-method’ and must make use of multiple settings.

Call for Papers

The Editor and Regional Editors wish to announce a special interest in encouraging papers discussing empirical research in psychoanalysis and in debating their value. The Editors applaud the efforts demonstrated in this Guest Editorial and hope to publish work as it becomes available. Clinical research and critical comments on it are both specially welcome.

responseSet.title: Case histories and confidentiality
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Case histories and confidentiality

Glen Gabbard MD

Glen Gabbard

Glen O. Gabbard, MD
The Menninger Clinic
PO Box 829
Topeka, Kansas 66601-0829

Glen Gabbard  

Dear Sir

I applaud Arnold Goldberg’s recent editorial on the writing of case histories in psychoanalytic journals. This issue has long been shrouded in secrecy and it is high time it was brought out into the light of day for open discussion. As Dr Goldberg points out, our need to advance the field by publishing case reports inevitably conflicts with our obligation to maintain patient confidentiality. I share his view that every solution to this problem is imperfect, and I also agree that the cessation of publishing case reports is not a viable alternative.

Beyond those areas of agreement, however, I view the dilemmas he describes somewhat differently. I think he overstates the problem with disguising case material. A welldisguised case can nevertheless be a highly factual report of what actually happened in the course of a psychoanalytic treatment. The disguise generally involves changing the identifying features of the patient to preserve confidentiality while leaving elements of the process intact. Indeed, the American Psychoanalytic Association Committee on Scientific Information developed a model of reporting clinical material that emphasized detailed process accounts of dialogue in analytic sessions while minimizing any biographical information about the patient.

Dr Goldberg also states that ‘It may come as a surprise to realize that most case histories, long and short, are written and/or presented with only a minimal or even no effort at disguise’. How does he reach this conclusion? I know of no data that would support this assertion. In my career as a writer and a reviewer of manuscripts for many editorial boards, my impression is quite the opposite.

In discussing the ever-present conflict of the obligation to protect our patients with our other obligation to advance our science, Goldberg suggests that we will run a risk of essentially writing fiction if we become firm protectors of confidentiality. I think his doomsday scenario is premature. By using the following guidelines, writers can avoid the dire future that Dr Goldberg fears: (1) disguise identifying features of the patient rather than altering the content of the sessions. (2) The goal of disguise is that no one other than the patient and analyst will know whose treatment is being described. (3) Do not write about patients currently in treatment. (4) If there is a reasonable chance that the former patient may read psychoanalytic journals, meet with the patient and obtain informed consent. (5) Do not write about patients who are candidates or who are in the field without obtaining consent.

responseSet.title: Dialogue With Other Sciences: Opportunities For Mutual Gain
responseSet.authorMast: David Olds and  Arnold M. Cooper
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dialogue With Other Sciences: Opportunities For Mutual Gain

David Olds and Arnold Cooper

Arnold Cooper

David Olds
Arnold M. Cooper
2211 Broadway, 1H
New York, NY 10024

Arnold Cooper  

Psychoanalysis, from its very beginnings, has greatly valued what we could learn from and what we could contribute to other areas of knowledge and discovery. Creative ideas often come from such cross-fertilisation, and it is inherent in the nature of science to be refreshed by discoveries from other disciplines. In the past our mutual interactions with anthropology, literature, linguistics, biography, sociology and history, as some examples, have enriched both disciplines. Currently, advances in neuroscience and cognitive sciences have aroused new interest in the exciting possibility of developing the study of mind-brain relationships and interactions. Freud himself was an early pioneer in biological interdisciplinary study, and we are reminded that he gave up this line of inquiry in part, at least, because neuroscience had not reached the point where such a project could be fruitful.

Now, however, an enormous amount of new information is becoming available through techniques of molecular biology, brain imaging, genetics, computer modelling and other studies and this may be the time to begin thinking anew of the linkages of psychology and the brain. More particularly, we may begin to ask what these new studies can contribute to psychoanalysis, as well as how the psychoanalytic understanding of mental functions may help to guide empirical studies of cognition and neural structures. Kandell (1983), some years ago, reporting on the molecular and cell biology of learning, pointed up the obvious—all mental life proceeds by alterations of brain activity, and learning represents a permanent change in the brain. Psychoanalysis, at root, is a highly specialised method of investigating and altering how and what we learn and we might wish to consider whether greater knowledge of brain functions will help us to a deeper understanding, and perhaps a rethinking, of some of the theories and techniques of psychoanalysis.

Freud considered the territory of psychoanalysis to be the realm of psychic conflict and unconscious thought. While Hartmann (1937) felt that in adding the broad area of the ‘conflict-free’ sphere, psychoanalysis could become an all-encompassing theory of the mind, a general psychology, most analysts today would accept that psychoanalysis is a particular branch of psychology, focused on psychic conflict, unconscious mental functioning, and aspects of development, with a special emphasis on object relations, affects and mental representations.

responseSet.title: Vicissitudes Of Remembering In The Countertransference: Fervent Failure, Colonisation And Remembering Other Wise
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Vicissitudes Of Remembering In The Countertransference

Fervent Failure, Colonisation And Remembering Other Wise

Roy Schafer

Roy Schafer

Roy Schafer
241 Central Park West #1A
New York, NY 10024

Roy Schafer  

The author argues that certain kinds of transference can have great impact on the analyst's ego functioning. One such effect is impairment of ready recall of the analytic material, such as life historical data, the analytic process itself, and his or her role as analyst. The analyst strives to remember otherwise by analysing the patient's projective identifications and other defensive, manipulative strategies. When successful, these strategies may be said to colonise the analyst's mind. Although other ego functions are often affected by colonisation, remembering is of particular interest owing to its importance in building up and keeping ready at hand the contexts within which balanced and timely interpretations can be formulated with some hope that they will be heard and used effectively and progressively by the threatened, phantasy-ridden patient. Special attention will be paid to themes of failure; other themes, such as omnipotence and erotised transferences will be considered later on. Clinical illustrations are included.

responseSet.title: Metaphor And The Theory Of Libidinal Development
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Metaphor And The Theory Of Libidinal Development

Burton Melnick

Burton Melnick

Burton A. Melnick
1, chemin des Tulipiers
1208 Geneva

Burton Melnick  

The author argues that ongoing work in cognitive linguistics affords a better understanding of how metaphor functions, both in psychic life and in the formulation of psychoanalytic theory. Lakoff and Johnson have shown that a great deal of our thought and perception depends on mainly unconscious metaphors, drawn in large part from basic bodily experience. Their findings can be most immediately related to psychoanalysis via the theory of libidinal development. There are two reasons for this. Firstly, the theory of libidinal development was itself generated in part by a metaphor—a misleading embryological one, which, historically, has tended to encourage reification. Secondly, the links that the theory points out between experience of cathected zones and later character traits are essentially metaphorical (or metonymic) in nature. The author considers that it should be possible to re-express the classical theory in language that both avoids reification and gives due importance to metaphor, and that such a re-expression would offer a number of advantages, including that of clarifying the relationship between possession of a penis and ‘phallic’ character traits.

<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Vann Spruiell</cs></ln><ln>P. O. Box 56270</ln><ln>Metairie</ln><ln>LA 70055</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Review Of The Psychoanalytic Theory Of Sexuality: Comments On The Assault Against It
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Review Of The Psychoanalytic Theory Of Sexuality: Comments On The Assault Against It

Vann Spruiell

Vann Spruiell

Vann Spruiell
P. O. Box 56270
Metairie
LA 70055

Vann Spruiell  

I am grateful though somewhat surprised to have been invited to present an opening discussion to review the psychoanalytic theory of sexuality. So much has been written in this century since Freud's Three Essays on the Theory of Sexuality that it would be absurd to attempt, in an abbreviated way, to encompass the subtractions and additions made over the years by Freud himself, much less those of his followers and opponents to this day. I shall limit the introductory remarks to a very general survey of the theory, confining it to specific psychoanalysts. This in no way demeans the other disciplines involved in any future general theory, for example psychiatry, biology, sociology, anthropology etc. I will not explore gender disorders, for instance, the multifarious effects of major bodily disasters such as congenital blindness or the effects of genetic mistakes. That will make it possible to focus on the curious history of our theory of sexuality since Freud's book: the fact that it has been abandoned by not only many non-analysts, but by many colleagues who consider themselves psychoanalysts.

Freud was hardly surprised about how the book would be received by non-psychoanalysts. He ended his preface to the Fourth Edition this way:

It must … be remembered … that some of what this book contains—its insistence on the importance of sexuality in all human achievements and the attempt that it makes at enlarging the concept of sexuality—has from the first provided the strongest motives for the resistance against psychoanalysis. People have gone so far in their search for high-sounding catch-words as to talk of the ‘pan-sexualism’ of psycho-analysis and to raise the senseless charge against it of explaining ‘everything’ by sex. We might be astonished at this, if we ourselves could forget the way in which emotional factors make people confused and forgetful … (1905, p. 134, my italics).

To the alterations Freud made to the 1900 text, and his many important papers subsequently, Strachey added an Appendix that lists thirty-three subsequent papers by Freud with central references to sexuality and remarked on the many references in his other papers (1905, pp. 244-5). Over the years these reflect deepening insights, with accompanying theoretical expansions, which, in turn, facilitated further integrations, for example, epigenetic conceptions of development, internalisations and much more elaborated views of the ego, along with what came to be called the id and the superego.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalyst At The Movies
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalyst At The Movies

Glen Gabbard

Glen Gabbard

Glen O. Gabbard
Menninger Clinic
P. O. Box 829
Topeka, Kansas 66601 0829

Glen Gabbard  

In a recent interview, Claude Chabrol, the esteemed French film-maker, noted that he had collaborated with the psychoanalyst Caroline Eliacheff in the writing of his film, La Cérémonie (Feinstein, 1996). He went on to explain why such collaboration was exceedingly helpful: ‘It's very hard, when you deal with characters, not to use the Freudian grid, because the Freudian grid is composed of signs that also apply to the cinema’ (p. 82). Indeed, the intimate relationship between the medium of film and the psychoanalytic study of the mind has long been recognised. As early as 1916, Harvard psychologist Hugo Mnsterberg wrote The Film: A Psychological Study, in which he suggested that film transforms the external world into the mechanisms of the mind, including memory, imagination, attention and emotion.

By 1926 psychoanalysts were consulting with film-makers. Although Freud himself had reservations about the cinema as an art form, he did not discourage Karl Abraham and Hanns Sachs from acting as consultants to the German director G. W. Pabst (Schneider, 1985). The result was the classic Secrets of a Soul, which featured dream sequences rich in symbolism and compelling in their demonstration of Freud's dreamwork. The narrative of the film revolved around a chemist with a knife phobia, homicidal impulses and impotence, who was cured by psychoanalytic treatment.

In this issue, with Jacob Arlow's discussion of Ingmar Bergman's Wild Strawberries, we inaugurate something that will become a regular feature of the Journal: film reviews. Unlike book reviews, our film reviews will not focus primarily on the quality of the work. Our reviewers will be asked instead to bring a psychoanalytic perspective to the understanding of a particular film. In other words, how does a psychoanalytic reading of the film uniquely illuminate its meaning? We will be reviewing both classic and more recent films. The films we choose will all be available on video so that readers who are interested can view them before or after reading the review.

<aut authindexid="Resnikoff, Deborah" listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Deborah</nfirst><nlast>Resnikoff</nlast></aut>
<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Judith Wallerstein</cs></ln><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Deborah Resnikoff</cs></ln><ln>290 Beach Road</ln><ln>Belvedere, CA 94920</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Parental Divorce And Developmental Progression: An Inquiry Into Their Relationship
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Parental Divorce And Developmental Progression: An Inquiry Into Their Relationship

Judith Wallerstein and Deborah Resnikoff

Deborah Resnikoff

Judith Wallerstein
Deborah Resnikoff
290 Beach Road
Belvedere, CA 94920

Deborah Resnikoff  

The authors argue that the patterning of conscious and unconscious needs, wishes, and expectations that parents and children bring to each other is often profoundly altered under the impact of divorce and the parents’ subsequent adult relationships. These changes, which are inherent in the divorce experience, have the power to modify significantly or derail the young child's developmental course. Clinical observations from an ongoing longitudinal research project show striking changes in the child's image of his parents, as portrayed here in the vivid play and fantasy of a psychologically sturdy child, concurrent with psychological changes in both parents and in their parenting. A follow-up, twelve years later, is reported. The authors suggest that the stable parenting of this child's first three years represented an internalised template of good relationships, which enabled him, despite the father's subsequent abandonment, the mother's grave acting out and the stepfather's rejection, to turn away from his disappointment in his parents, to wrest what was available from school, friends and others, and to propel himself forward at each point to the next developmental stage, moving increasingly away from his family towards independence and competence. The consistency of the child's inner life and integrity is contrasted with the depression of his parents and the instability of their parenting.

<aut authindexid="Garella, Alessandro" listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Alessandro</nfirst><nlast>Garella</nlast></aut>
<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Fausta Ferraro</cs></ln><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Alessandro Garella</cs></ln><ln>Via B. Cavallino 91/c</ln><ln>80131 Naples</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Termination As A Psychoanalytic Event
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Termination As A Psychoanalytic Event

Fausta Ferraro and Alessandro Garella

Alessandro Garella

Fausta Ferraro
Alessandro Garella
Via B. Cavallino 91/c
80131 Naples

Alessandro Garella  

After noting that the debate on termination encompasses both the singularity of clinical practice and the generality of theory, the authors propose an approach that focuses on the actual event of termination, for which the termination process is seen as a preparation. They show that termination as a process has featured in psychoanalytic theory for many years and give examples of different conceptions of it from the literature of the last seventy years. However, in their view the event of termination, as an act of passage and an entity that can be consigned to memory, is of particular importance and comprises a complex configuration that has the three coordinates of direction, communication and temporality, which are discussed in turn. Dreams, parapraxes and acting out, as well as parting gifts from the analysand to the analyst, assume particular significance in the context of termination, which sometimes emerges only after the analysis has ended. The concept of termination as event, in the authors’ view, offers a typological framework that accounts for the differences between successfully terminated analyses, analyses that are broken off unilaterally by the patient or the analyst, impasse situations and interminable analyses. The points raised are illustrated by clinical vignettes.

<artinfo lang="en" artsubtype="ART" j="IJP" artsource="A1V2" arttype="ART" id="IJP.078.0587A" ISSN="0020-7578"><artyear>1997</artyear><artvol actual="78">78</artvol><artpgrg style="arabic">587-593</artpgrg><arttitle lang="en">I: How Brain Development Is Shaped By Genetic And Environmental Factors</arttitle><artsub lang="en">Developments in related fields Neuroscience</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Pally, Regina" listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Regina</nfirst><nlast>Pally</nlast></aut>
<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Regina Pally</cs></ln><ln>11980 San Vicente Blvd., Suite 810</ln><ln>Los Angeles, CA 90049</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: I: How Brain Development Is Shaped By Genetic And Environmental Factors: Developments in related fields Neuroscience
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

I: How Brain Development Is Shaped By Genetic And Environmental Factors

Developments in related fields Neuroscience

Regina Pally

Regina Pally

Regina Pally
11980 San Vicente Blvd., Suite 810
Los Angeles, CA 90049

Regina Pally  

We have entered an era of extraordinary discovery about the human brain. Old notions of dichotomy between mind versus brain, nature versus nurture, have been supplanted by a rich web of synergistic relations between mind and brain, nature and nurture. Specifically, according to modern neuroscience, this means that all mental phenomena are assumed to be the result of biological activity of neuronal circuits in the brain. The development of these circuits relies in part on genetic programmes, but is also heavily dependent on the individual's experiences with the environment.

Recognition of the remarkable degree to which brain development is experience-dependent is a striking example of how neuroscience can be integrated with psychoanalysis. These ideas can be considered to lend support to analytic assumptions that early developmental experiences shape subsequent psychological functioning. It is with the aim of integrating the two fields that I present a series of brief articles that are designed to provide a schematic overview of neuroscience topics that are relevant to the theory and practice of psychoanalysis. The series offers a global perspective. In this way the reader will not only know facts about brain functions, but be able to think conceptually about how these functions operate with one another and how they may inform us about our analytic work.

For some, this way of considering ‘The Mind’ may be rather foreign and difficult to accept. However, as Olds & Cooper in their recent editorial on the value of neuroscience for psychoanalysis recommend: ‘We should at least understand what we are being offered, before deciding whether or not’ it is profitable to us (1997, p. 221). The idea that mental life is derived from biological events in neuronal circuits is the reigning doctrine of neuroscience, and therefore must be taken as a starting point for understanding the empirical research based on it. For those who criticise these attempts at integration as ‘reductionistic’, I want to clarify that the emphasis here is that mental phenomena are derived from biological activity. There is no intention to equate the mental with the biological.

responseSet.title: Winnicott And The Capacity To Believe
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Winnicott And The Capacity To Believe

Brooke Hopkins

Brooke Hopkins

Brooke Hopkins
Department of English
University of Utah
Salt Lake City, Utah

Brooke Hopkins  

Like Freud's, Winnicott's writing displays an enormous interest in words, in their histories as well as their current usage. The author discusses his use of two words, ‘capacity’ and ‘belief’, combined in the phrase ‘capacity to believe’. What Winnicott has to say about this capacity sheds light on the nature of both religious and cultural experience generally. The paper's argument has two strands that are woven together throughout. The first is Winnicott's concern for words and how a knowledge of their roots can enrich their current meaning. The second is his concern for the nature of belief, the ‘capacity to believe’, and his conviction that in exploring this capacity psychoanalysis might have something to teach religion. These concerns are interrelated in a number of ways in Winnicott's writing and are ultimately connected with his notion of a ‘cultural field’, a place to grow, where ‘inventiveness’, even verbal inventiveness, is ‘just one more example … of the interplay between separateness and union’, that is the separateness of individual language users but also their union through the language they share.

responseSet.title: Review of The Psychoanalytic Mind: from Freud to Philosophy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Review of The Psychoanalytic Mind: from Freud to Philosophy

Marcia Cavell and Donald Davidson

Donald Davidson

Marcia Cavell
Donald Davidson
Marcia Cavell
1570 Olympus Avenue
Berkeley, California 94708
Donald Davidson
Philosophy Department
U. C. Berkeley, CA 94720-2390

Donald Davidson  

Dear Sir

I write in response to Allan D. Rosenblatt’s review of my book The Psychoanalytic Mind, from Freud to Philosophy (Int. J. Psychoanal., 78: 188-193). There is certainly room for disagreement about many of my contentions in that book, but Mr Rosenblatt attributes to me several positions that are simply not mine. Disagreement is one thing; talking past the other, another. Since I know my book can be rough reading, I hope here to make a little clearer what some of the issues are with which my readers might disagree.

1) Rosenblatt asserts that I revive ‘the long discredited “doctrine of immaculate perception’”. What I do claim is that it is a requirement of interpretation—whether the interpreter is psychoanalyst, anthropologist, or friend with friend—that the interpreter take some of the other’s reports to be true. (A good part of the longest chapter of my book—Chapter One—is devoted to a discussion of this idea and to the concept of truth at its heart. There I discuss also the considerations that have led people to identify truth with correspondence or with coherence, and I say why I think both views say something partially right and partially wrong.) This is a long way from any doctrine of ‘immaculate perception’.

2) He ignores my distinction in the chapter on emotions (Chapter Seven) between affects and emotions, and my explicit thesis that there is a continuum from affects, which animals and infants can certainly have, to those emotions (for example, pride, guilt, resentment) that are constructed, in part, by a propositional content, which animals and infants cannot.

3) He says that I ‘seem to say that awareness or consciousness cannot exist unless there is self-awareness’ and that I deny awareness and consciousness to animals and infants. Of course they have both, in some sense. My discussion of this subject (in Chapter Five) focuses on the ambiguity of these concepts and how easily one can slip from one sense to another, as I say (in that chapter) that I think Daniel Stern does.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Mind: from Freud to Philosophy.: By Marcia Cavell. Cambridge, Massachusetts and London: Harvard University Press. 1993. Pp. x + 276.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Mind: from Freud to Philosophy.

By Marcia Cavell. Cambridge, Massachusetts and London: Harvard University Press. 1993. Pp. x + 276.

Allan Rosenblatt

Allan Rosenblatt

Allan D. Rosenblatt
3252 Holiday Court, Suite 205
San Diego, CA 92037

Allan Rosenblatt  

‘What are the necessary conditions for mind? Such a question will not be settled by empirical research’ (p. 3). With this question and forthright assertion, Marcia Cavell begins her exploration of Freud's central psychoanalytic concepts as an adherent of the contemporary ‘anti-subjectivist’ movement in the philosophy of mind. A Visiting Professor of Philosophy at New York University, she describes herself as ‘a philosopher with some psychoanalytic training and considerable acquaintance with the analytic couch’ (p. 6).

The thesis of her book is that ‘we cannot divorce the activities of thinking and speaking’ (p. 20). In opposition to the subjectivist, internalist view that thought originates internally and that this world of subjective experience is then provided with linguistic labels, she follows the noted philosopher Davidson in arguing that mind and the mental are social phenomena and that ‘language is the condition for the mental in general, whether conscious or unconscious’ (pp. 38-9).

responseSet.title: Psychic Reality And Unconscious Belief: A Reconsideration
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychic Reality And Unconscious Belief: A Reconsideration

Harold Gerard

Harold Gerard

Harold B. Gerard
502 Rialto Avenue
Venice, CA 90291

Harold Gerard  

In a recent paper Britton attempted to distinguish between a phantasy that has achieved the status of a belief and one that has not, and between a belief and knowledge. The author argues that, in the light of the seventeenth-century controversy between Descartes and Spinoza, both of these distinctions are untenable. Descartes argued, as Britton does, and as Freud did, that phantasies or ideas are not accepted as beliefs until they are tested against reality. Furthermore, Britton maintains that for a belief to acquire the status of knowledge, it must be supported by incontrovertible evidence. Spinoza, on the other hand, proposed the seemingly preposterous notion that a comprehended proposition is automatically believed. Doubt may subsequently be engendered by disconfirming evidence. As it turns out, research in a number of domains suggests that Spinoza was correct and Descartes was wrong. This evidence and its clinical implications are discussed. As suggested by Bion, instilling doubt regarding deeply ingrained (Spinozan-formed) phantasies is a principal goal of treatment.

<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Victor Sedlak</cs></ln><ln>2, Foxhill Court</ln><ln>Weetwood Lane</ln><ln>Leeds</ln><ln>LS16 5PL</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Dream Space And Countertransference
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Dream Space And Countertransference

Victor Sedlak

Victor Sedlak

Victor Sedlak
2, Foxhill Court
Weetwood Lane
Leeds
LS16 5PL

Victor Sedlak  

The author describes some similarities in the psychoanalytic literature between the conceptualisation of the dream space and that of the analyst's function as a container of the patient's projections. He also notes the correspondence between the creative use of a dream and the use of an analytic session to further understanding. Detailed clinical material is then presented from the analysis of two male patients, both of whom were unable to complete a dream about a particular emotional dilemma. The way in which they created severe disturbance in the countertransference at the same time is reported. The analyst's struggle to work through this difficulty and the beneficial consequences of this for the patient are described. It is argued that this demonstrates the way in which patients may project into the analyst dilemmas that they are unable to dream about in order that the analyst's functioning may transform the dilemmas into ones that can be thought and dreamt about. It is further argued that some patients have a developmental need to engage the analyst in this way.

responseSet.title: Obituary: W. CLIFFORD M. SCOTT (1903-1997)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Obituary

W. CLIFFORD M. SCOTT (1903-1997)

Athol Hughes

Athol Hughes

Athol Hughes
3 Antrim Grove
London NW3 4XP

Athol Hughes  

The working life of Clifford Scott as a psychoanalyst and psychiatrist, spans the period from the 20s to his death from pneumonia on 19 January 1997. He was born on 11 March 1903 in a small Ontario town, where his father was a Presbyterian minister. His keen intelligence was apparent from the start as it was throughout his life and he showed

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1997

392

a particular aptitude for science. However, his father encouraged him to study medicine, since, according to Scott, his father knew how one could make a living as a doctor but did not know how one could make a living as a scientist.

His interest in science led him to take two years out of his medical training at the University of Toronto to obtain a new degree, a Bachelor of Science in Medicine, which almost led him to leave medicine to work in the field of genetics. However, he returned to his medical studies and gained some experience in psychiatry in Toronto, which was followed by psychiatric training in New York, and later in Baltimore at Johns Hopkins Hospital where Adolf Meyer was professor. There, and later in Boston, he met psychoanalysts such as Paul Schilder and became enthused about analytic ideas, which led him to the realisation that he wanted and needed analytic training. A grant helped him to reach London in 1931, where he was accepted by the British Psycho-Analytical Society for training and assigned by Ernest Jones to Melanie Klein for analysis. He was her first candidate to qualify and met the requirement for adult and child analytic work.

On qualification he said he did not have the courage to go into private practice at that time and so developed his interests in working psychoanalytically with psychiatric patients in the Maudsley Hospital and later at the Cassel Hospital. His published works from that time were concerned with analytic approaches to manic depressive disorders, schizophrenia and the body image. He left the hospital posts to practise and teach in London just before and in the first year of World War II. In the last years of the war he was involved in the treatment of adult and child patients in centres outside of London. This war-time experience with the Emergency Medical Service of short-term treatment using analytic principles helped him to understand and interpret the relationship between deep-seated and long-term conflicts and more contemporary ones that led to the presenting problems.

<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Nitza Yarom</cs></ln><ln>14 Brandeis Street</ln><ln>Tel Aviv 62001</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: A Matrix Of Hysteria
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Matrix Of Hysteria

Nitza Yarom

Nitza Yarom

Nitza Yarom
14 Brandeis Street
Tel Aviv 62001

Nitza Yarom  

The psychoanalytic literature of the last decades on hysteria is reviewed and its overemphasis on pre-oedipal concepts is demonstrated. A matrix of hysteria, which is a contemporary re-reading of Freud's basic conceptualisation, is presented. It integrates oedipal concepts with others borrowed from object-relations theories, self-psychology and the intersubjective approach. In this way the focus on the issues of gender and sexuality in hysteria is continued, without the original roots being lost. The focus is on the content, and the structure may vary. Within the first axis of the matrix, the author proposes to identify the conflict of gender and sexual identity as the unconscious and conscious solutions that a hysteric adopts to the question ‘am I a man or a woman?’ In an attempt to identify hysteria's particular psychic dynamics, a range of concepts is suggested. Repression and conversion are incorporated to constitute the second and third axes of the matrix. Repression, denial, dissociation and fantasy are shown to be employed in the context of gender and sexuality and along a continuum of personality structures. In this context, the turning to the body, the conversion, still acts as an effective language, to which we have to listen.

<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Cláudio Laks Eizirik</cs></ln><ln>Rua Visconde de Rio Branco 708</ln><ln>Porto Alegre</ln><ln>RS 90220-230</ln><ln>Brazil</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis And Culture: Some Contemporary Challenges
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis And Culture: Some Contemporary Challenges

Cláudio Eizirik

Cláudio Eizirik

Cláudio Laks Eizirik
Rua Visconde de Rio Branco 708
Porto Alegre
RS 90220-230
Brazil

Cláudio Eizirik  

The evolution and current usage of the concept of culture is briefly discussed. Two divergent views on the initial relationship between psychoanalysis and culture are presented and the influence of the former on the latter illustrated, both in the previous decades and at present. Some general aspects of present-day western culture are then outlined, among them the great flood of information, the culture of narcissism, the temptations of conventionality, the ideology of visual media and the ‘light culture’, and five propositions that attempt to characterise ‘post-modern sensibility’. Finally, four challenges faced today by psychoanalysis are discussed: the changing nature of philosophical and cultural cross-currents as it influences psychoanalysis as a discipline; the empiricist versus the hermeneutic approach; attacks on psychoanalysis as an elitist discipline and profession; and the movement away from the subjectivist and existential concerns and the focus on a more collectivist and pragmatic relationship to reality. The author suggests possible ways to face each of these challenges.

responseSet.title: Letter
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter

Thomas Ogden

Thomas Ogden

Thomas H. Ogden
306 Laurel Street
San Francisco
CA 94118

Thomas Ogden  

Dear Mr. Tuckett,

I am grateful to Dr. Plaut for his careful reading of my paper ‘Reconsidering three aspects of psychoanalytic technique’ and the interesting questions of technique that he raises in his letter. He suggests that perhaps the use of the analyst’s state of reverie that I discuss might lead to an undervaluing of transference interpretation or perhaps may even be seen as a substitution of reverie for the interpretation of the transference. In responding to this concern, I should make clear that I view the goal of the analyst’s use of his reverie experience to be the understanding of, and eventual interpretation of the leading anxiety in the transference.

160

Thinking about Children.

By D. W. Winnicott. Edited by Ray Shepherd et al. London: Karnac Books. 1996. Pp. 343 + xxxii.

Paola Mariotti

Paola Mariotti

Paola Mariotti
37 Woodsome Road, London NW5 1SA

Paola Mariotti  

Thinking about Children is a collection of thirty-one papers and talks by D. W. Winnicott, with an introduction by the editors, Ray Shepherd, Jennifer Johns and Helen Taylor Robinson. It is the latest of several books posthumously publishing Winnicott's papers.

All but three of the papers were previously unpublished. The three papers that have appeared were part of an early edition of The Child and the Outside World (1957) but were not included in the later revised editions of the book. The works span four decades; the earliest dates from 1931 and the last was delivered in 1970. The talks were originally prepared for various and diverse audiences, among whom were psychologists, doctors or medical students, social workers and trainees, or the lay public.

This book is not, primarily, about psychoanalytic theory or technique. It is about the application of psychoanalytic concepts and discoveries to disciplines other than psychoanalysis, to medicine and paediatrics, social work and to the professions dealing with children with special needs.

responseSet.title: The Languages of Psychoanalysis.: By John E. Gedo. Hillsdale, New Jersey: Analytic Press. 1996. Pp. 209.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Languages of Psychoanalysis.

By John E. Gedo. Hillsdale, New Jersey: Analytic Press. 1996. Pp. 209.

Martin Bergmann

Martin Bergmann

Martin S. Bergmann
1136 Fifth Avenue, New York, NY 10128

Martin Bergmann  

John Gedo occupies a unique position in contemporary psychoanalysis. After decades of psychoanalytic work, including teaching and supervising at the Chicago Psychoanalytic Institute, he retired not to escape the burdens of the ‘impossible profession’ but to reflect and write about his experiences. A division of life into vita activa and vita contemplativa was highly praised in antiquity, but it is rare in psychoanalysis today. The bibliography in the book under review contains sixteen papers and eleven books by the author, most of them written after his official retirement. Fortunately for the reader not familiar with the author's previous writings, they are summarised and commented upon in the present book.

A sustained reflection such as Gedo had undertaken demonstrates that cases that failed, or those of limited success, tend to push their way into the foreground of the analyst's reflection. In the language of Gestalt psychology, failed cases, like interrupted tasks, leave a ‘Zygarnik effect’ on our memories. They highlight our personal limitations, but, even more painfully, the limitations of psychoanalysis itself. To reflect systematically as Gedo does on these cases is painful and avoided by most of us.

The title of the book contains its basic idea. The messages that the analysand sends to the analyst are encoded in a variety of languages. Success in treatment depends on the ability of therapist and patient to communicate simultaneously in several shared languages (p. 66). Therapeutic talent consists of the ability to use the widest variety of communicative modes in a flexible manner (p. 59). The analyst's language should be penetrating, concrete, simple, affect-laden and dramatic (p. 84). Since mental dispositions are formed early in childhood, they are more likely to be affected by metaphor, metonymy, synecdoche and irony. The blank language cultivated by American psychoanalysts during the Hartmann era was, in Gedo's view, counterproductive. Important fantasies, particularly those that constitute the psychotic core, have never been verbally encoded. What can be verbalised is already in the realm of the ego. Gedo warns that verbalisation alone does not mean that what has been verbalised is amenable to modification.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Understanding Mental Objects.

By Meir Perlow. London and New York: Routledge. 1995. Pp. 206.

James Rose

James Rose

James Rose
25 Drylands Road, London N8 9HN

James Rose  

Meir Perlow's intention in this book is to look closely at what is meant by the idea of an ‘object’ in psychoanalytic object-relations theory. In particular, he examines what has happened to the ‘object’ as psychoanalysis has shifted from a drive-oriented approach to one that has an interest in an individual's interpersonal processes in the formation of his mental organisations, processes and capacities. This book seeks to provide a conceptual structure to enable these developments in object-relations theory to be better considered. Undoubtedly, Perlow has rendered a valuable service to experienced psychoanalysts, candidates and all others interested in psychoanalytic theory by providing such a structure and he has implicitly thrown down a challenge which, I think, deserves taking up. If further theoretical development is to be achieved the issues he raises cannot be ignored.

The ‘object’ has now entered the psychoanalytic vernacular to the extent that it can be used without knowing or thinking about what it means in the context of it being used.

responseSet.title: Disavowal: Structural Effect And Pathogenic Dimension
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Disavowal: Structural Effect And Pathogenic Dimension

Myrta Casas De Pereda

Myrta Casas De Pereda

Myrta Casas De Pereda
Av. Gral. F. Rivera 2516
Montevideo
Uruguay

Myrta Casas De Pereda  

Introduction

Freud places sexuality at the centre of psychic structuring and hence of psychoanalytic theory and practice. The defence mechanisms that reveal the presence of the drives are part of this concept of sexuality. I shall discuss one mechanism, disavowal, in terms of both its structuring function in shaping the subject and its pathogenic dimension. On the basis of changes in the imaginary social being (Castoriadis, 1983) and their implications for pathology, I draw a parallel between a prominent feature of today’s society, namely a degree of flexible correspondence between antinomies (Lipovetsky, 1983, p. 11), and the important pathology of disavowal, the metapsychological and dynamic facets of which I shall describe with a clinical vignette.

Sexuality and genitality

The concept of sexuality, which Freud (1915, p. 117f.) describes as fundamental, includes but goes beyond genitality. Involving the most radical expression1 of the structuring symbolic level of the psyche, sexuality maintains the essential division between conscious and unconscious aspects in the psychic subject by drive repression and emergence of the unconscious wish.

The roots of psychic conflict lie in driverelated functioning, whose field, between wish and defence, constitutes the singularity of each subject and is simultaneously moulded by the parents’ wish. Libido, sexuality and psychic organisation therefore all partake of the same substance. An indispensable other always manufactures symbolic regulations that bind the subject into civilisation. Every threat to the subject is sexual, because sexuality is constitutive of the psyche and the unconscious. Furthermore, we constitute ourselves with reference to the other, who wishes us to live.

Genitality is an effect of structuring, which takes account of the unconscious configuration of the wish. In the social sphere, we recognise it in the ethics and aesthetics of the link between men and women. Acceptance of the difference between the sexes is both an effect of unconscious organisation and a social fact that challenges psychoanalysis today. It is interesting to consider how social changes cause changes in the symptoms that are the response to psychic conflict (‘choice of neurosis’).

[[RunningHead]]

The Suicidal Adolescent.

Edited by Moses Laufer. Madison, CT: International Universities Press. 1995. Pp. 154.

Chrstopher Cordess

Chrstopher Cordess

Chrstopher Cordess
16 Highlever Road, London W10 6PS

Chrstopher Cordess  

This book of eleven chapters by thirteen different authors—a panel discussion is included —combines papers written predominantly by staff of the Brent Adolescent Centre and Centre for Research into Adolescent Breakdown, London, with additions from a conference of the same title. The emphasis is on experience gained ‘hands on’, in day-to-day clinical work in this walk-in (ambulant) clinic, and is necessarily, and admirably, pragmatic. The service invites youngsters to come for ‘any reason’, so as to make initial contact with as many young people as possible ‘who [are], or could soon be, in serious trouble mentally’ (p. xiv). One such group ‘consists of adolescents who feel hopeless or who have attempted suicide’. This volume is focused upon how the clinic staff, consisting of psychoanalysts and psychotherapists, sets about its aims of helping this group and, specifically, of preventing suicide and self-harm. In summary, they do so by addressing the range of different routes which may lead to adolescents becoming suicidal, by improving—through improved psychodynamic understanding—the early recognition of danger signals, and—from the evidence of this book—by maintaining a clinical research orientation to their clinical practice.

One of the central and perennial questions in work with the adolescent is that of assessing the status, at any one time, of changing mental experience, behaviour and symptomatology. Is it within acceptable developmental ‘norms’, or does it bespeak major developmental failure, psychopathology and incipient illness? Is there a risk of self-harm, or other violence? While in no way suggesting that there are any easy answers, the authors of this volume—using frequently harrowing, as well as instructive case examples—explore their own particular experiences and points of view.

The inclusion of the contributions to the closing discussion of the conference of not only the panel members, but also those of speakers from the floor, helpfully places the concentrated and specialised experience of this Centre's work within the wider context. Under the general title, ‘Can we prevent suicide in adolescence?’, this session generated such fundamental questions as how to engage the young person in the first place and how especially difficult it is to reach the younger adolescent. What of the different modes of communication of desperation in adolescents who do not belong to the dominant culture, or to that to which their therapists belong? What are the relative merits of working in groups with adolescents, in contrast to individual work? To what extent, in any given case, should the family be involved? Perhaps most important of all, how can we generalise the knowledge and experience contained in this (relatively slim) volume, so as to make it more available and applicable in the varied settings where most severely troubled adolescents are seen, for example, the school, the youth club, social services, the GP's surgery, probation, or general psychiatric services? As Joan Schachter remarks elsewhere in the book, ‘I think there is also a need for clinical discussions about the effectiveness of various ways of engaging and working with adolescents at risk. I would agree that all such adolescents need psychotherapeutic attention. But I am not sure that all can benefit from psychotherapy sessions’.

Universals of Psychoanalysis in the Treatment of Psychotic and Borderline States.

By Henri Rey. London: Free Association Books. 1994.

David Morgan

David Morgan

David Morgan
Flat 1, 15 Rosslyn Hill
London NW3 5UJ

David Morgan  

Henri Rey is most famous in Britain for his pioneering work with psychotic and borderline patients. He was born in Mauritius but trained as a psychiatrist and psychoanalyst in London mainly working as a consultant psychotherapist at the Maudsley Hospital, an institution to which he developed a strong attachment and one that provided him with a base in which he was able to make a unique contribution to the fields of both psychiatry and psychotherapy. Many young psychiatrists and other mental health workers who were lucky enough to experience his teaching, particularly in the realms of supervision and assessment of deeply disturbed patients, were inspired to think psychodynamically about the psychotic and borderline patients they were seeing.

[[RunningHead]]

Danger and Defense.

Edited by Marianne Goldberger. Northvale, New Jersey: Jason Aronson. 1996. Pp. 386.

Allan Compton

Allan Compton

Allan Compton
Los Angeles Center for Psychoanalytic Research
1081 Moraga Drive, Los Angeles, CA 90049

Allan Compton  

This volume, subtitled The Technique of Close Process Attention, is a festschrift in honour of Paul Gray. It consists of an introduction by the editor and thirteen chapters by twelve different authors. While not always easy to read, the chapters are thoughtful, stimulating and generally well written. The book as a whole is well edited and nicely produced. It deserves the widest possible psychoanalytic readership.

Danger and Defense is a second-generation exposition of Gray's original and challenging technical and theoretical innovations. Most of the authors have worked with Gray in one form or another and several of them constitute a study group that has been exploring and expanding upon his work for some years. Almost all of the chapters are clearly related to the technique of close process attention (CPA) and explore that technique and its utility from different angles. An interesting thread runs through the first eleven chapters, uniting careful descriptions of clinical technique with empirical research possibilities and ongoing endeavours, creating a tension between this kind of approach and what might be called ‘post-modern looseness’ as it appears intermittently all through the book, and especially in the last two chapters.

The introductory first chapter by Davison, Pray, Bristol & Welker is really a manual for CPA technique, in the best sense of the term ‘manual’. The essential concepts of the technique are defined, its precepts and principles stated and abundantly illustrated, the biases inherent in the approach are recognised and articulated, and an extended case example is presented. The authors also discuss the research implications (and promises) of CPA and the manual. If a set of scales were added (not appropriate in this presentation), measurement of whether or in what degree the technique is being carried out seems readily possible. Like other good manuals, this one appears to carry great utility as a teaching vehicle as well.

The limitations of the CPA approach, at least as conceptualised by these four authors in their research-oriented piece, are also not disguised. The theoretical framework is that of more or less classical ego-psychology, with almost exclusive emphasis on superego analysis focused on verbal manifestations of defences against unpleasure affects that appear to be motivated by moral strictures (superego activity). Even within this somewhat narrow area, the focus is sharply upon instances of aggression turned against the self.

The Baby and the Bathwater.

By Nina Coltart. London: Karnac Books. 1996. Pp. 172.

James Rose

James Rose

James Rose
25 Drylands Road, London N8 9HN

James Rose  

This book bills itself as a valediction and as such is a summary and review of the development of the author's outlook upon psychoanalysis and its practice. There is no doubt that she has inspired much affection and respect in the course of an illustrious career. She has, therefore, the right to make this statement in what is undeniably a very personal book.

In consequence, this book should, I think, be read as a story. Nina Coltart has absolutely no desire to proselytise or proclaim a particular point of view to gain converts. The structure of the book has a clear sense of a beginning, a middle and an end. The telling of, and listening to, stories about the lives of human beings forms the essence of this book and is the medium of its message. The story is about the author's journey from literature, via medicine, to a meditative view of life, heavily influenced by Buddhism. The purpose is to show the impact these influences have had upon her view and practice of psychoanalysis.

Clinical practice forms the base and three case histories give a clear sense of the author as a psychoanalyst in action at work.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1997

L’objet; ou l’objet, l’absence et l’ombre.

By Jean Guillaumin. Paris: L’esprit du temps (distributed by Presses Universitaires de France). 1996. Pp. 286.

Pierre Chauvel

Pierre Chauvel

Pierre Chauvel
9 all
é
e Turenne, 44000 Nantes

Pierre Chauvel  

As indicated by its title, which means ‘The object; or the object, absence and the shadow’, this book by Jean Guillaumin, a member of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society and member of the Lyon Psychoanalytic Institute, links together two important subjects: the object and loss of the object. A moment's reflection tells us that it is impossible to separate them, but our ‘objective’ thinking habits often lead us to consider them independently of each other. The marshalling of concepts is a requirement of both the scientific and the analytic spirit. However, the resulting risk of impoverishment of thought must not be overlooked, as Guillaumin's work constantly reminds us.

This approach to the theme of the object presupposes that the subject also be taken into account. The author points out that the very term ‘subject’, which is traditionally contrasted with the object in philosophical definitions, was seldom used by Freud. The current analytical trend in France, as embodied in particular in Raymond Cahn's paper presented at the Congrès des psychanalystes de langue française in 1991, restores its well-deserved importance; it is not then contrasted with the ‘ego’ but has the connotation of a subject in the process of formation and development, for which the vehicle is symbolisation, a process in which forward movement alternates with failure.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1997

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysts Talk.

By Virginia Hunter. New York, NY: The Guilford Press. 1994. Pp. 456.

Bertram Cohler

Bertram Cohler

Bertram J. Cohler
5730 South Woodlawn Avenue, Chicago, IL 60637

Bertram Cohler  

The question of the relationship between life experiences and understanding of the field of work is an interesting one. Psychoanalysis in particular recognises the importance of understanding the personal past and collective memory as factors closely related to current pursuits. Unfortunately, while this is the implicit theme of the present volume, the book fails to inform the reader regarding the relationship of biography to life-work. The author, an independently educated psychoanalyst from southern California, initially educated as a social worker specialising in family and group practice, elicited interviews from eleven analysts in the United States and England. In the process of conducting these interviews, she also provided a very brief vignette, including a dream, from a case of a ‘borderline’ woman patient from which she elicited comments in the form of a ‘supervisory’ conference. The analysts chosen for this report include André Green, Hanna Segal, Frances Tustin, John Bowlby, Ernest Wolf, Peter Giovacchini, Arnold Goldberg, Rudolf Ekstein, Robert Wallerstein, Arnold Modell and Jacob Arlow. In each instance, Hunter recorded a free-wheeling interview which, together, comprise much of the book (the interviews of André Green, Hanna Segal, Frances Tustin, and John Bowlby and Ernest Wolf have all been previously published in the journal of the National Psychological Association for Psychoanalysis).

[[RunningHead]]

The Anatomy of Prejudices.

By Elisabeth Young-Bruehl. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press. 1996. Pp. 632.

Donald Moss

Donald Moss

Donald Moss
80 University Place, 5th Floor
New York, NY 10003

Donald Moss  

For a psychoanalyst, one of the many felicitous consequences of reading Elisabeth Young-Bruehl's impressively panoramic study, The Anatomy of Prejudices, is being drawn into a reconsideration of the relationship between the terms ‘clinical’ and ‘prejudicial’. This relationship is often thought tangential. But for Young-Bruehl, it is an intimate relationship. She thinks of individual psychopathologies and socially mediated hatreds as conceptually bound. After all, both categories are grounded in efforts to externalise, flee from, and, if necessary, destroy an ‘other’ known only as the source of pain.

Young-Bruehl brings together the two categories and positions them as interestingly complementary. But for Young-Bruehl, perhaps best known to a psychoanalytic readership by way of her acclaimed and controversial Anna Freud: A Biography (1988), this complementarity can be overstated, and often has been. When it is, important differences between what she calls ‘ethnocentricisms’ (antagonisms grounded in history and for which psychic determinants are secondary) and ‘ideologies of desire’ (antagonisms grounded in myth and fantasy, and whose primary determinants are psychic—racisms, sexisms, homophobias, anti-Semitism) are obscured.

The difference between these two forms of hatred is central to Young-Bruehl's argument. She grants that the two forms can overlap. Ethnocentricisms can take on a patina of, for instance, psychically determined racism; homophobias can be expressed with the use of, for example, supplementary ethnocentric images. But such overlap, she argues, should not be allowed to obscure the radical differences between the two categories. Ethnocentrisms, for Young-Bruehl, have a ‘referent in the world and a history’ while ideologies of desire (for which Young-Bruehl coins the word ‘orecticisms’)

Ira Brenner

Ira Brenner

Ira Brenner  

In her latest book, No Voice is Ever Wholly Lost, Louise J. Kaplan has demonstrated, once again, that she has mastered the fine art of bringing psychoanalytically informed topics to a wide-ranging audience. This volume deals with the problems of mourning and the attempts to preserve or restore ‘the inner dialogue’ with the lost object. She quite com passionately addresses the near-impossible tasks that confront both parents and children who have to bear the trauma associated with an untimely death of a loved one.

responseSet.title: Hidden In The Imagery: An Unconscious Scene In The Conformist
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Hidden In The Imagery: An Unconscious Scene In The Conformist

Herbert Stein

Herbert Stein

Herbert H. Stein
New York, NY 10021

Herbert Stein  

The Conformist: 1970

Director B. Bertolucci

Paramount Home Video (1986)

555 Melrose Ave.

Hollywood, CA 90038

Bernardo Bertolucci's 1970 film, The Conformist, is based on Alberto Moravia's novel about a young man, Marcello, in pre-war Italy, who volunteers his services to the Fascists, not out of ideology, but to blend in, disguising his perverse sado-masochistic tendencies by expressing them through a perverse cultural norm. Bertolucci has changed the focus of Moravia's plot by presenting most of the story through Marcello's reminiscences as he is driven towards the film's central scene, a particularly violent assassination. Marcello has helped set up the assassination of his former teacher, Luco Quadri, but now he is chasing after Quadri's car with the vague hope of saving Quadri's wife, Anna, who has unexpectedly joined him on the fatal trip. The car and Marcello's memories converge on the assassination, making it the intense focal point of the film.

Marcello's reminiscences are presented with the subtle distortions of subjective memory. Through them we share some of Marcello's own confusion about his motives. We see that he harbours anger towards his psychotic father and drug-addicted mother. We also learn that as a child he was the victim of an attempted seduction at the hands of a young man, Lino, and ended up shooting his seducer, leaving him for dead. He marries a woman he does not love and falls in love with a woman he hardly knows. In its subjectivity and uncertainty the film is similar to an analytic hour. If we approach it that way, we can see that hidden in this tale of an abused and tortured man is another, shorter tale of a child and a primal scene fantasy. It is as if we could reconstruct an early screen memory that adds meaning to the current surface.

One clue to the unconscious in an analytic hour is the presence of repetitions. The Conformist can easily be seen to have continual repetitions of primal scene imagery, imagery relating to a child witnessing parental intercourse. Throughout the film, each scene contains some obvious elements of the primal scene while other elements are disguised or missing. As viewers, we never experience the primal scene directly, but are continually teased with fragmented primal scene imagery.

[[RunningHead]]

The Unorthodox Freud: The View from the Couch.

By Beate Lohser and Peter M. Newton. New York and London: The Guilford Press. 1996. Pp. 241.

George Makari

George Makari

George J. Makari
Cornell University Medical College
525 East 68th St., Box 171, New York, NY 10021

George Makari  

On a spring afternoon, I approached a Manhattan cross-walk with my 3-year-old daughter in hand. Not a car was in sight. I began strolling across the street when I felt a tug. My daughter pointed to the blinking red sign and said: ‘it says don't go’. As the father of psychoanalysis, Sigmund Freud tried to formulate rules to teach his students how to make their way effectively and safely across many a mental street. Though the promised textbook on psychoanalytic technique never appeared, Freud offered his acolytes ways of learning through his expositions of dream analysis, his published case reports, and of course his seminal papers on technique. Freud also taught a select few psychoanalysts by performing their training analyses. And in those analyses—at least by his analysands’ reckoning—Freud did not practise all that he preached. In fact, he seemed to be jaywalking all the time.

The authors of this volume seek to foreground and refine our understanding of the contrast between Freud's technical writings and his actual analytic work. To do so, they present elegantly distilled summaries of some of the most famous published accounts by Freud's later analysands: Smiley Blanton, Hilda Doolittle, John Dorsey, Abram Kardiner and Joseph Wortis. There are no surprises here: all of this material has already been published in book form.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1997

[[RunningHead]]

Textbook of Psychoanalysis.

Edited by Edward Nersessian and Richard Kopff, Jr. Washington, DC: American Psychiatric Press Inc. 1996. Pp. 726.

James Lomax and Lucy Puryear

Lucy Puryear

James W. Lomax
Lucy J. Puryear
Department of Psychiatry, Baylor College of Medicine
One Baylor Plaza, Houston, TX 77030

Lucy Puryear  

This textbook has long been awaited by educators within psychoanalytical institutes, psychiatric residency programmes, clinical psychology graduate schools and the more psychodynamically oriented and sophisticated social work programmes. The authors intended to produce a textbook to give readers ‘an understanding of the basic tenets of psychoanalysis while at the same time providing an up-to-date picture of the field’ and hoped that ‘both the seasoned practitioner and the neophyte student will find the chapters contemporary, valuable, and useful’. Obviously, the authors also wished to avoid a compromise product that might seem simplistic to the ‘seasoned practitioner’ while simultaneously being obscure to ‘the neophyte’. Kopff & Nersessian have done an admirable editing job in their efforts to achieve such balance, and for the most part succeed in their quest.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1997

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Getting Started: An Introduction to Dynamic Psychotherapy.

By Joel Kotin. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson. 1995. Pp. 304.

Fredric Busch

Fredric Busch

Fredric N. Busch
10 East 78th St., Suite 5A, New York, NY 10021

Fredric Busch  

Writing a primer for beginning students of psychotherapy is clearly a very difficult task. Beginners are impressionable, want to do the right thing, and can tend to take advice as gospel in order to avoid painful levels of uncertainty. The author of such a text must be skilled at communicating basic psychoanalytic concepts along with cogent clinical examples. He must also be able to make his recommendations about therapeutic approaches with a supporting rationale, while noting possible alternatives. The author needs to be concerned about oversimplification, excessively rigid recommendations, and idiosyncratic viewpoints. While providing many instances of excellent explications of the psychotherapeutic process, Kotin does not adequately avoid these pitfalls.

An example of oversimplification is found in Chapter Two, ‘An approach to interviewing’, which discusses approaches to patients in the initial interview, emphasising that ‘one finds out the most by saying the least’. Kotin describes a vignette in which a patient answers a query ‘Tell me about yourself’ with the response ‘Well, there's not much to tell’. Kotin then states ‘Such a patient is almost certainly depressed’. It is not clear on what basis he draws this conclusion. In fact, the patient may be schizophrenic, with a paucity of thought, or have a character disorder with passive aggressive features, or any of multiple other disorders. In addition, there may be a variety of meanings or affects involved in such a statement. Unfortunately, a beginner, anxious to learn the ‘right way’, may readily interpret a patient's similar comment in accordance with Kotin's viewpoint, without further consideration.

The tendency towards oversimplification noted above is also represented in the absence of discussions of multiple psychic determinants of feelings, behaviours and fantasies. Kotin's reference to the multiple meanings of countertransference fantasies in Chapter Eleven is an exception to this tendency. Since the consideration of multiple determinants is essential to psychodynamic psychotherapy, it would have been valuable to demonstrate this mode of thinking more regularly in the case vignettes.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Towards A Clinically And Empirically Sound Theory Of Motivation

Charles Spezzano

Charles Spezzano

Charles Spezzano
2252 Fillmore Street
San Francisco
CA 94115

Charles Spezzano  

Dear Sir

Drew Westen (IJPA, 78: 521-548) makes a number of crucial points about affect and motivation and he carefully documents his sources including my own book on affect theory. While Westen adds significantly to our understanding of the issues his efforts to distinguish his position from that of others runs the risk of missing the extent to which a

1216

consensus on affect and motivation is growing within psychoanalysis.

His central proposition in this regard is ‘The attempt to regulate affect—to minimise unpleasant feelings and to maximise pleasant ones—is the driving force in human motivation’ (p. 542) and that ‘we stand on clinically and empirically firm ground to suppose that whatever motives move us do so because of their affective significance to us’ (p. 543). An important step that must be taken to fully establish this position and one, as I argued in 1994, that is ‘the antithesis of Freud’s key theory of affect’ (Spezzano, 1994, ‘A relational perspective on anxiety’, in Anxiety as Symptom and Signal, ed. S. Roose and R. Glick, Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press, p. 132) is that ‘the sexual excitement and rage—that, in turn, lead to so much human anxiety—are not understood to be flowing constantly out of libidinal and aggressive drives. Sexual excitement results from certain bodily activities of the child and out of relational events. It is an intrinsically sought after experience. We do not learn to desire it. We desire it by our very nature as human beings’ (p. 132).

Westen adds that, in this light of an affect-centered theory, we don’t need to choose between the bedrock drives argued for by contending theorists (e.g., sex versus relationship). Although he leaves the impression that I, among others, fall short of taking this final step into affect theory with him, I wrote in 1993 that while each analytic theory offers one ‘bedrock conflict on drives, needs, or tendencies’ (Affect in Psychoanalysis, Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press, p. xii) as the motivational core of human psychology, ‘Through the lens of affect theory, by contrast, we find in psychoanalysis what Philip Rieff (1966) vividly described as “a jostling democracy of contending predispositions” (p. 55) each of which reflects a fundamental human desire to feel or avoid feeling a specific affective state’ (xii-xiii). I added later that ‘The full array of these affects defines the human agenda’ (p. 152).
